text: In the continuation of "Subtle Words," written by Yuan Mei during the Qing dynasty: **Chapter One: The Wolf Commander** There was a man named Qian who returned from the market late and was walking in the foothills. Suddenly, dozens of wolves appeared and surrounded him, ready to devour him. Feeling extremely threatened, Qian saw a pile of firewood about ten feet high by the roadside and quickly climbed up and grabbed hold of the branches, climbing up to avoid them. The wolves could not climb up, and some of them rushed away. A little while later, a group of wolves came carrying an animal, placing it in the center. The wolves leaned their ears to its mouth, as if whispering and listening closely. A short while later, each wolf leaped up, pulling the firewood from below, and the branches almost scattered apart. Qian was greatly frightened and called for help. After a long time, some woodcutters heard the sound and shouted together to come. The wolves were startled and scattered away, but the animal that was carried came alone. Qian then looked closely at it with the woodcutters. It resembled a wolf but was not a wolf, with round eyes and a short neck, a long beak, and sharp teeth. Its hind legs were long but soft, unable to stand, and its voice sounded like a monkey's cry. Qian said, "Ah! I have no grudge against you, yet you are acting as the wolf commander-in-chief, planning to harm me!" The animal bowed its head and mournfully cried, showing signs of regret. They then took it together to a nearby village tavern and cooked and ate it.

A Shoe on the Table and the White Dragon Pond My fellow scholar, Zhu Meifu, the Vice Minister, had a son late in life and loved him dearly. He was very upright and solemn, and whenever he saw me, he treated me with great respect, behaving as if he were a younger relative. Due to his poverty, he sought employment in the household of a certain general in the capital, and the relationship between host and guest was harmonious. One morning, he found a woman's embroidered shoe on the table and became extremely angry, scolding the family members: "I am here as a teacher, and you have placed this object on the table, which will make the owner think of me as what kind of person? Throw it away immediately!" The family members looked around the table and found no such shoe, yet Zhu continued to scold fiercely. The general heard the commotion and entered, and Zhu immediately fled to the foot of the bed, covering his face with his hands and saying, "I am so ashamed, so ashamed! I cannot face the master!" The general tried to explain, but Zhu had already picked up a stick from under the bed and was hitting himself while cursing, causing his brain to burst open. The general thought he was going mad and called for a doctor immediately, but Zhu was already dead. The White Dragon Pond In the old city of Miluo County, where Han and non-Han peoples live together, people live around the mountains. At the foot of the mountains lies the White Dragon Pond, which is about several acres wide and has thousands of mu of good farmland. An earth dam is built to hold the water, facing the great river. When the water overflows, the sluice is opened to release it. During the rain, two dragons fight, appearing like small snakes. Sometimes a section of a large tree, covered in moss, is seen standing upright and moving, often breaking through the dam. One day, while the farmers were planting rice in the fine rain, fish of various sizes flew in pairs, forming a procession, accompanied by a woman in red clothing holding a fan and leading them into the pond, and they disappeared immediately. It is said that the dragon princess returned home.

A family of non-Han ethnicity, known as the Nong clan, was about to end their day when suddenly a man dressed in mourning clothes appeared, claiming to seek lodging for the night. When asked what he needed, he requested a single room for sleeping and a large jar filled with clear water. The host suspected the guest wanted to bathe and agreed to his request, also offering to prepare food and drink. The guest said, "That is not necessary. There is only one favor I ask, and I will reward you greatly." The host asked, "What is it?" The guest replied, "Behind the dragon pool here is a large tree. You should go and cut it down. When it is about to fall, first tie it up with a strong rope. Wait until two sheep fight in the pool. Then cut the rope and let the tree fall." The host agreed. At dawn, he went to cut the tree and indeed saw the water in the pool boiling like waves, with a black and a white sheep fighting. Thinking it was the right time, he cut the rope and let the tree fall. The black sheep jumped out, and the water calmed down. He hurried back to tell the guest and ask for his reward, but the guest had already disappeared. He asked his wife, who said, "The guest is still in the room and has not left." They searched the room together. Suspecting the guest was in the jar, they opened it and found it filled with gold. They then realized the guest was a white dragon transformed to help resolve a dispute over the pool. As a result, the pool became known as the White Dragon Pool, and the Nong family has been known as the richest family ever since.

The God of Fleeting Marriages, Jia Zhengjing, was from the region of Qianzhong. He married Tao Shi, who was quite beautiful. On the Qingming Festival, when they went to visit their ancestors' graves together, they were halfway there when suddenly a whirlwind blocked their path. Suspecting it might be a ghost or spirit seeking sustenance, Jia arranged offerings, poured out wine, and prayed, saying, "In such haste, we have nothing better to offer but a cup of turbid wine; please do not find it unclean." After the offering, they continued to the gravesite to pay their respects and returned home. The following spring, Jia left his wife to travel far away. One evening, as dusk approached, he realized that the nearest inn was still far off, and he was deeply afraid of the wilderness with no place to rest. Suddenly, a man dressed in blue stood by the roadside and asked, "Are you Mr. Jia? I have been waiting for you here for a long time, as commanded by my master." Jia asked, "Who are you?" The man replied, "You will know when you arrive." Pointing to a place with lights in the distance, he indicated that it was the village. Jia secretly rejoiced and followed him. After walking about a mile, the host was already at the door to greet him. Dressed in scholar's robes and wearing a scholar's cap, the host appeared to be a refined gentleman. The buildings were grand, adorned with gold and jade. Jia greeted and asked, "In the darkness of the night, I became lost and was suddenly honored with your summons. I have never met you before, so I do not understand how you could have known me in advance, and why you would trouble yourself to welcome me." The host replied, "Last year on the Qingming Festival, you and your wife visited the gravesite, and the whirlwind that blocked your path was me." Jia said, "So you are a god?" The host answered, "No, I am a local immortal." When Jia asked about his duties, the host said, "It is shameful to admit, but I oversee the fleeting and transient relationships among people."

"Jia remarked, 'I am quite sentimental, and I dare to ask you to check if there will be any encounters in this life.' The deity took the record and reviewed it, smiling and saying, 'How strange! You have no share in this life, but your esteemed wife has a very good match.' Jia unconsciously broke out in a sweat, thinking to himself that his wife was young and beautiful; if this were to happen, it would be a lifelong shame. He then asked to have it eliminated. The deity said, 'This is a predestined fate; how could I change it?' Jia pleaded again, and the deity looked up to the sky in thought, after a long while saying, 'Good! Good! Fortunately, your esteemed wife has met an ordinary and lowly man, whose greed for money surpasses his desire for beauty. You should quickly return home, and you can avoid the disgrace in the bedroom, only losing some money.' Jia counted the days of the journey, realizing he had already been away for four days, fearing he might not make it back in time. He also thought about gaining a small profit by fighting over flies, which could cause his wife to lose her virtue, and this was absolutely unacceptable. Thus, he left the deity and returned home, traveling day and night."

**Translation:** Only forty miles from home, a sudden heavy rain poured down, and he was thus unable to proceed. The next morning upon entering the door, he saw that the wall of the bedroom had collapsed. A single young man was living close by. Recalling the words of a divine being, he unconsciously sighed with regret. His wife asked, "Why are you sighing?" He replied, "The wall has collapsed, and the rooms are now connected. Both of us are young and alone, and the matter is still something that can be spoken of? Yet you ask me about it!" His wife said, "Is this what troubles you? Such things have happened in the city, fortunately, the loss of ten gold pieces prevented it." The merchant inquired about the reason, and she said, "After the wall collapsed, the young man indeed came to make advances. I fled to a neighbor's house, but unexpectedly, the gold hidden in my pillow was stolen. Now he fears your return and has already fled far away." When asked where the gold came from, it turned out to be something owed by a certain family. The merchant reported the matter to the authorities, who captured the young man and flogged him, but the gold was ultimately unrecoverable. This matter was told to me by Cheng Xingfeng. ○ A Ghost of a Hanged Man Clears His Name In Xin'an, Zhao Tianru was teaching a student named Huang. During a scorching summer, fearing the heat, he could not sleep at night and asked his host to change his bedroom. The host pointed out several places, but none were satisfactory. Only one room in a courtyard had many flower trees, and a gentle breeze blew through it, which Zhao liked. Huang seemed reluctant. Zhao suspected that the room was too close to the inner quarters, but Huang said, "No, there are ghosts and spirits above, so I dared not let the master stay there." Zhao said, "It's no problem." He then moved his bed there and lit a candle to wait.

Midnight, a sudden sound came from the beams above. Upon looking up, two pairs of bow-shaped shoes were seen descending, followed by a woman in her twenties, who leaned on the railing to gaze at the moon and arranged her makeup box as if preparing to groom herself. She then proceeded to the side building, lifted the roof tiles of several gutters, and retrieved six bundles of white silver, spreading them out on the table and examining them with a sigh. Afterward, she repackaged the silver, hid it back in the gutter, covered it as before, and turned to Zhao's bed, about to lift the curtain. Zhao got out of bed and shouted to chase her away, and she fled all the way downstairs. She entered the back garden's bamboo grove and disappeared. Upon peering inside, there was a newly placed coffin, and it was realized that this was the ghost. The next day, when meeting the host, Zhao asked, "Could the ghost in the back garden have been someone who hanged themselves? Who in your household?" Huang could not help but cry, saying, "The deceased was my beloved concubine, Zhang, who was most intelligent and skilled, responsible for managing silver and money. One day, she collected 300 taels of rent from a certain place, but shortly after handing it over, it was nowhere to be found when I urgently needed it. In a moment of anger, I cursed her with slanderous words. How could I have known she would be so enraged and take her own life?" Zhao said, "This was your fault for being overly harsh. But can the matter be fully clarified?" Huang replied, "Not yet." Zhao asked, "Does he have a son?" Huang replied, "The one who now studies under my tutelage is his." Zhao said, "Please clear his name for me." Zhao pulled Huang up the stairs, lifted the roof tiles to retrieve the gold, and indeed it was the original.

That night, the ghost descended again, acting as if combing and washing, took a brush and wrote a poem on the wall, bowed twice before the bed, and then left. The poem read: "A little maid stole the gold and ran away, secretly hiding it in the gutter on the roof. Today the injustice is finally cleared, and my resentment is also completely resolved." From then on, the building became quiet. ○ Two children holding a staff, Jin Yi, who passed the imperial examination in the first year of Shunzhi, sought descendants at Lingyan. He dreamed that a Zen monk pointed to the two children holding a staff and said they would be his sons. Therefore, he named his eldest son Chen Xi, who later became the governor of Yunnan and Guizhou. In his old age, he once said, "I still should have another son." After a long time, he dreamed that in the main hall, a bed of brocade was exposed, with a dragon coiled within. Just then, a man named Cao, a tax collector, came to deliver rent, bringing his wife with him. She was just over ten years old, wearing old brocade clothes and laughing. The official was greatly shocked and decided to keep her and take her in, and she gave birth to Wen Su Gong. ○ The Zhao family for three generations was a deity. Zhao Gongyi of Changzhou, a famous minister during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, was well-known to everyone. After his death, a man surnamed Guo from Suzhou, who had known him in life, later traveled by boat on Dongting Lake. As dusk approached, he saw a large ship coming with the wind, its flags and lamps all inscribed with the words "Lake Guang City God's Office," which he found strange. When he got closer and looked, he saw that the official was sitting upright in the boat, handling official business.

Another Mr. Lu's son, Jing, was skilled in the art of Cheli. Once, he knelt in front of the altar until he reached the gate of the Second Heaven, where he saw that Mr. [the subject of the text] was also at the gate of the Second Heaven presenting matters. His son, the Vice Minister, served in the army in Su Zhou as a descendant of a high-ranking official. After the Minister of Loyalty and Valor passed away, he was granted permission to attend the funeral. Overcome with grief, the Vice Minister fell ill. While ill, he often said with astonishment, "The floor is covered in vomit, making it difficult for people to bear. Why am I in this position?" Others asked about his position, and he replied, "The Department of Phlegm and Fire." The family did not know what kind of deity the Department of Phlegm and Fire was. The next day, they prayed at the Dongyue Temple, and indeed, there were statues of the Department of Phlegm and Fire in the side halls. As his illness worsened, people saw the lantern of the Department of Phlegm and Fire enter the room, and he then passed away. After the Vice Minister passed away, more than a year later, the aunt, surnamed Hong, fell ill and became unconscious. In a daze, she arrived at an office and saw Mr. [the subject of the text] coming out from inside. She was surprised and asked, "Why are you here, sister?" He invited her in and talked about family matters in great detail. The aunt asked, "What position do you hold now?" He said, "I am the Inspector of the Sea Route. My duties are heavy, and I must leave immediately, so I cannot keep you." He also said, "Your sister-in-law will not be here for long, and there are many matters at home. You should entrust the two nephews to be cautious." He sent two attendants with incense to escort her back. When she woke up, there was still a lingering fragrance in the room. Not long after, the family members started a lawsuit over the succession, causing unrest for a whole year. Not long after, his sister-in-law, Lady Huang, passed away.

Zhang Shaoyi, the Inspector of Guilin, observed that the city god of Guilin was revered as a deity. Longzhou, Mr. Gu, prayed to the city god because his father had been ill for a long time and wished to take his place. One day, he dreamed that the city god sent a subordinate to summon him to the front of the office, but he could not enter immediately. He saw a sedan chair coming from afar and stood aside to wait, which turned out to be his teacher. The teacher emerged from the sedan chair, held his hand, and comforted him, saying, "I have already become the land deity of a certain place. Why are you here?" Mr. Gu explained his situation, and the teacher said, "This is great filial piety. I will speak on your behalf." After a while, the teacher emerged and said, "The city god has other matters today. We will reschedule." Mr. Gu then woke up. The next day, the same subordinate summoned him again. Upon arrival, the city god summoned him inside and asked about his father's condition. Mr. Gu replied, "He is as thin as a skeleton." The city god became very angry and ordered the subordinate to beat him. Mr. Gu did not understand and cried out in innocence. Soon after, a note was brought out from inside the temple. The city god read it and his expression cleared. He said, "Your father set up a pharmacy. In a certain year, there was a major epidemic. He did not seek a way to obtain medicine, and his merit and virtue were great. Moreover, I pity your filial piety. You can extend your life by a period of twelve years." Mr. Gu thanked him and left. He asked the people around him, "Why was the city god angry?" They explained, "Among beasts, only the dhole is the thinnest, and people often mistakenly write it as '柴.' The city god initially misunderstood and thought you were comparing your father to a beast, which is why he was angry. Thanks to a guest official who clarified the matter, the city god's anger was resolved."

At the office, the people I saw were all local elders who had died by legal punishment. One was in chains, and one was sent for exile to a distant place. I did not recognize them and asked, "Who is this former governor of a certain prefecture, who was taken by the city god of Guilin after being sued by his subjects?" I asked, "Who is Zhang Gong?" He replied, "I have also forgotten his name. He once served as the grain storage commissioner in Yunnan and is now the uncle of Governor Bi of Henan." Zhang Fengsun, courtesy name Shao Yi, was from Changzhou. He and I both passed the prestigious imperial examination for literary talent. In his youth, he was known as "Zhang Sanzi," which referred to him as a "son" in three senses: filial piety, moral integrity, and literary talent. Throughout his life, he was known for his great virtues, which is why he became a deity. However, in the afterlife, they do not know his name, but only boast about his relatives' positions. It is no wonder people like to talk about those who are famous. During the Wanguan Rebellion in Shandong, the most tragic event occurred in Lincheng, where the bodies of men and women filled the river, some even rising above the banks by several feet. After the rebels were defeated, the sluice gates were opened, and the bodies were allowed to float downstream. Some ruffians stole and stripped the clothing from the bodies, leaving many naked. Suddenly, a female corpse, about seventeen or eighteen years old, floated face-up on the water, drifting to the side of the sluice gate, where her left foot caught on the gate and stopped her. Soon after, a male corpse, of similar age, floated downstream naked. As it approached the sluice gate, it suddenly jumped out of the water and embraced the female corpse, their necks and thighs intertwined. The crowd tried to pry them apart with poles, but could not separate them despite their best efforts. Shortly after, they were swept away by the current, and it was impossible to tell who they were.

In the evening, Li the scholar, who was staying at a village school, became lost while walking. Far in the distance, he saw lights among the trees and headed towards them. He saw a thatched cottage, and faintly heard the sound of someone reading. He knocked on the door, and the owner came out to greet him. The owner was about forty years old and invited Li inside. The owner introduced himself as Mr. Ge, who always loved reading and was tired of the noisy and chaotic city life, so he lived in this remote place. He also mentioned that his wife was at home without food and was being pressured by her mother to remarry, and she would throw herself into the river the next day. Only Li could save her, and he hoped Li would help. As he spoke, tears fell from his eyes, and Li agreed. Li then stayed overnight at the cottage, and the bedding was clean and neat. The next morning, Li found himself lying on a tomb and there was no cottage. He was extremely frightened and hurried back. On the way, he met a woman dressed in green, who was walking and crying, and was about to throw herself into the water. Li stopped her and asked why she was doing this. She revealed that she was Mr. Ge's wife, a widow without food, and her parents wanted to force her to remarry, so she was seeking death. Li, seeing that the cottage was not far away, invited her to come back with him. He and the old woman discussed the strange events, and Li raised her as his own daughter. Li, who was over fifty, suddenly gave birth to a son. Looking at the boy's eyebrows and eyes, he resembled the man he had met. Playfully, Li called him "Mr. Ge," and the boy would always place his tongue in his chest.

Tianhou Lin Yuanfeng said: Tianhou Shengmu, my twenty-eighth-generation paternal aunt, passed away unmarried, and her spiritual manifestations are most prominent. In the ocean and on boats, one must worship her devoutly. When encountering unpredictable winds and waves, calling upon her brings immediate response. There are three horsemen, one painted with a crown, flowing robes, and holding a jade tablet; one painted in regular attire; and one painted with loose hair, bare feet, and standing with a sword. Whenever in danger, if the image with the crown is burned, it responds immediately; if the image in regular attire is burned, it responds without fail; if burning reaches the image with loose hair and a sword and still does not respond, the boat cannot be saved. Sometimes when the wind and waves are dark and unclear, one does not know which way to go, devoutly praying and calling upon her, and a red light appears on the water. Following the light, one always reaches safety. Sometimes she is seen standing at the edge of the clouds, wielding a sword to divide the wind, splitting it into north and south. On the ship, a stick must be placed before the god's seat. Whenever a group of dragons floats on the sea, the wind and waves are about to rise. Burning paper, wool, etc., does not work. Then the person called the 'stick master' is ordered to burn incense and summon the stick, dancing it once around the water, and the dragons will lower their tails and descend, none daring to disobey. If the incense ash in the stove rises without reason, or if the incense line disperses into the air, the ship is certainly in danger.

A Relative's Father from My Clan recounted that when he was young, he witnessed soldiers from the Zhang region performing a ritual sacrifice in the training ground during a military campaign to Taiwan. He accompanied his father to observe, and they saw a figure seated at the top of a banner, with a plump appearance but a very short stature. He quickly called his father to look at it, but by the time they arrived, it had already vanished. ○ A Young Girl from the Yin Family, who lived in front of the Yin residence in Wu Prefecture, had a sister who was only twelve years old. It was the Mid-Autumn Festival, and the family was just beginning to drink together when they heard of a neighbor's wife being disrespectful to her mother-in-law, with harsh and abusive words. The sister suddenly turned pale and stood up, holding a knife and going directly into the woman's home, destroying her tables and chairs, and grabbing the woman with the intent to stab her. Family members rushed to save her, but the girl's strength was very powerful, and it took five or six people to hold her back and finally restrain her. They took her back and asked her why, but she still stubbornly refused, angrily yelling, and shouted loudly, "I must kill this woman to avenge my mother." The family forced her to lie down, and then she fell asleep snoring. When she woke up and was questioned, she blushed, sweated, and wept, not knowing the reason for her actions. ○ A Tiger Throwing Itself into the River In the western part of Shaoxing, there was a deep stream. A child was playing by the stream when he saw a tiger approaching. The child dove into the water, swimming in and out, and cautiously observing. The tiger sat on the bank, staring for a long time, with an intense and impatient state, its saliva dripping from its mouth. Suddenly, the tiger leaped up to attack the child, falling into the water. Furious and rapidly moving, the stream boiled, and the tiger repeatedly leaped and fell, but ultimately failed to rise. The child escaped, and the tiger drowned.

○ Zhu Zhujun, a scholar, served as the provincial education supervisor in Anhui. He dreamed that the emperor summoned him to resume his position as the god of Mount Wuyi. Zhu Zhujun wept and declined, citing that his collection of writings was not yet complete. The emperor agreed. Upon waking, he recounted this dream to Lin Mengli, the magistrate of Guichi. Those who heard it found it strange. Later, when he inspected schools in Fujian, he visited the temple of Mount Wuyi. The layout and arrangement of the temple matched exactly what he had seen in his dream, which further surprised him. After completing his term, he returned to the capital and died peacefully without any illness. According to historical records, Yang Wen, a prominent figure, was born with purple hair covering his body, reaching a length of one foot. He called himself the god of Mount Wuyi, which is similar to Zhu Zhujun's story. ○ Jiuhua Mountain is the most famous for its divine and mysterious qualities. It is said that during the late Ming period, Hai Gong Gangfeng, while visiting the mountain during a rainstorm, was told by his companions that his leather boots were made from cowhide, which is non-vegetarian and thus inappropriate for wearing. He then changed to straw sandals and joined the others in worshiping the deity. He pointed to the temple drum and asked the deity, "This is also made from leather, is it not non-vegetarian?" As soon as he finished speaking, a thunderbolt struck the temple, shattering the drum. From then on, no one dared to use leather for temple drums, and they were replaced with cloth. There was a man named Gu from Jiangnan, who had one son and one daughter. Both children were grown up, but his wife had passed away, leaving him as a widowed old man. He arranged a marriage for his son with a seventeen-year-old girl from a farming family named Jiang. Jiang was kind and filial, and Gu loved her dearly. Shortly after, Gu fell ill, and his son was away. Jiang heard his moaning and informed Gu that she would seek a doctor. Gu replied, "I have a foot ailment, but it will be fine if it is kept warm." Jiang said, "If that is the case, it is not difficult." She then held Gu's foot and slept with him, demonstrating her filial piety without understanding the implications of their close physical contact.

In the following spring, the son returned home, passing by his sister's house on the way. His sister informed him about his wife's filial piety. He could not help but be suspicious, but it was difficult for him to speak. So at night, he took his bedding to another room and did not sleep with his wife. His wife was surprised and suspicious, and she asked her husband. He said, "Have you heard of an old man and his wife sleeping together?" Jiang finally realized and said, "I pitied my father-in-law's old age and illness, so I actually slept with him. Only heaven and Buddha know my heart." His son smiled but did not answer. One day, he heard a neighbor's old woman beating a drum and reciting Buddhist chants. He went out to ask, "What are you doing?" She replied, "I am going to pay homage to Mount Jiuhua." Jiang immediately joined her companions to go along. After burning incense and kneeling to worship, she saw the cliffs and precipices of the incense burner peak across the river. She asked, "What is its name?" The old woman said, "This place is called Longkou Xiang. If one's heart and actions cannot be self-evident, one can go to ask the ghosts and gods for proof." Jiang heard and was very happy, and she took the incense and went forward. The old monk stopped her and said, "I have been a novice monk since I was young and am now old. I have never seen anyone dare to climb. Especially you, with your delicate steps, how can you take such a risk?" Jiang did not listen and went straight to the place. Those who watched were heartbroken. Indeed, she fell halfway up the mountain, and everyone was sorry that she had turned into dust.

A neighbor woman returned home and urgently informed her husband. The husband was surprised by the mistake and said, "My daughter-in-law returned home yesterday." He led the neighbor woman inside and indeed saw Jiang with closed eyes sitting cross-legged on a meditation cushion. The neighbor woman exclaimed, "This is a living Buddha; why go to Jiuhua Mountain for worship?" They all chanted Buddhist scriptures and paid homage to her. Jiang then opened her eyes and stood up. They all inspected the meditation cushion and found four characters "Jiuhua Mountain" on it. They all asked the husband, "When did your daughter-in-law return home?" The husband replied, "Yesterday I heard a sound in the courtyard and suspected it was a thief. I went with my son to check, and then my daughter-in-law flew down. Her eyes were closed as if dead, and she was barely breathing, so we carried her into the room. She wanted to express her heart, so she went with anger and did not consider her life and death. Unfortunately, the mountain was very high, and her feet were soft, so she fell. She also did not know how she returned home." The neighbor woman then told the husband and his son what had happened. The couple embraced and cried, and people far and near were amazed. Afterward, those who went to Jiuhua Mountain first came to pay respects to Jiang.

The Clerk Zhang The Clerk Zhang was an honest and impartial official in charge of drafting documents at the Governor's Office in Yunnan. He was trusted by many governors he had served under. One night, early in the morning, he opened the door to find a hanged body suspended. Upon closer inspection, he recognized it as Mr. Jia, who had sought his favor in a legal dispute and whom he had previously agreed to support. He then closed the door and sat quietly, waiting for news from outside. As the morning sun rose, he opened the door again, and the hanged body was gone. He secretly rejoiced. At noon, he suddenly heard that the county magistrate had come out to the city to investigate, asking who the deceased was, and it turned out to be the hanged body of Mr. Jia at the door. At first, he was shocked, then suspicious, but ultimately could not understand the reason for it.

Several months later, the nobleman encountered a vegetable vendor named Zhao in the market, who asked, "On a certain morning of a certain month, did you see the hanged person and startle?" Upon hearing this, the nobleman invited Zhao into his room and treated him to wine and food, asking how he knew about it. Zhao replied, "It was I who carried the body away; how could I not know?" The nobleman said, "We do not know each other, so why would you carry the body? Moreover, you carried the body very early, before the city gates and barriers were open; how did you manage?" Zhao explained, "I do not understand why either. That day, at the fifth drumbeat, I was selling vegetables when I met a friend on the way. He called me here and said, 'Carry this body to a certain place, and you will receive great profit, better than selling vegetables.' I was concerned that the city gates and barriers were not yet open, but my friend said, 'No harm, just follow me.' I followed him, and when the gates and barriers opened, we reached the city." The nobleman asked, "What is the name of your friend?" Zhao replied, "I recognized the person but did not ask their name; they are also someone I know from the market. They borrowed a cigarette holder from me, and I have not seen it returned since." The nobleman paid Zhao a hundred gold coins as a reward and instructed him not to spread the word, then they parted ways.

One day, Zhao Xian entered the city god temple and saw a clay figure hanging a smoking pipe in one of the ten courts, which looked very much like his own. Upon closer inspection, he confirmed it was indeed his, and so he took it down, jokingly remarking, "Why have you been away for so long?" The next day, while selling vegetables in the market, Zhao Xian encountered a friend who scolded him, saying, "Given your character, it's very difficult to get along with you. For such a small matter as a smoking pipe, why did you laugh at me in front of everyone?" Zhao Xian tried to explain his absence and asked for his friend's name, but just then, someone who had called out to buy vegetables arrived. In the moment it took to turn his head, his friend disappeared without a trace. In Lin'an, Zhang Da and Li Er were inseparable friends. Although Li Er was often in poverty, he had a nature that was not careless, which is why Zhang Da respected him. One day, Li Er told Zhang Da about his financial difficulties, and Zhang Da, who had accumulated several hundred pieces of gold, gave it all to Li Er, agreeing to share the profits equally. Unbeknownst to Zhang Da, Li Er lost all his capital within a few years and returned home, staying indoors and not seeing Zhang Da. Zhang Da waited patiently, but after a long time, during the preparation for his daughter's wedding, he went to Li Er's house to ask about him. Li Er ignored Zhang Da, and Zhang Da became angry, leading to a heated argument. Passersby gathered to watch. When asked, Zhang Da said Li Er was untrustworthy, while Li Er said Zhang Da deceived him. Neither party had a clear basis, making it difficult to determine right from wrong. Li Er persisted in his argument, and Zhang Da became even more angry, saying, "If you dare to go to the city god temple tomorrow to swear and touch the money, I will give up." Li Er responded carelessly, as the neighbors believed in ghosts and spirits, and it was said that the city god was the most powerful. In front of the city god, a pot of boiling oil was placed with money inside. The hand of a just person would not be burned, but if not, it would definitely burn. Zhang Da used this to pressure Li Er.

The next day, Zhang Guo came to pursue Li, but Li was not afraid. Together, they went to the temple, where they struck the bells and drums, recounted the entire story, and then set up a cauldron of boiling oil. They threw a coin into the oil and had someone touch it. Li managed to retrieve the coin without any harm to his hand, and thus the crowd condemned Zhang, who could no longer defend himself. Later, Li engaged in another business and returned home with full loads after several years. He then calculated the principal and interest of Zhang's loan and returned everything to Chu, personally visiting Zhang's door. Zhang said, "Our relationship has been severed, and morally, I cannot accept the money." Li replied, "In fact, I borrowed your property. How could I dare to betray your kindness? I will wait until the next life to repay you as a cow or horse!" Zhang refused repeatedly, but Zhang ultimately refused to accept the money. The villagers then decided to use the money to replace the decaying wooden bridge in front of the temple with a stone one. They also asked Li, "Since you were dishonest before, how could you swear an oath?" Li laughed and said, "At that time, I was not dishonest; I was actually afraid that once I admitted it, I would have to return the original item, which would be impossible to repay even if I were to break my bones. That's why I first went to the temple to pray for the gods' silent protection, planning to repay Zhang when I became wealthy. I didn't expect the gods to be so effective." The crowd heard this and all laughed, saying, "Is the City God accepting your bribe?" After the bridge was completed, it remained unnamed. They then named the bridge "Bridge of Accepting Bribe."

Cao Gong dreamed that Cao Quan, the scholar-official from Haiyang, was selected for a county in Guangxi. Friends and relatives came to congratulate him, but Cao Gong wished to decline the appointment due to illness, saying: "In my youth, I had an unusual dream: at what time would I enter the academy, marry, have children, and be selected as a successful candidate for a county in western Guangxi, only to be killed by two white-armored generals. Upon careful reflection, every detail of the dream has come true, not differing by a single hair. Now, the selected position happens to match my dream, but the area is inhabited by many Miao and Yi people, whose wild and unpredictable nature is difficult to gauge. Is this not an early sign? Should I not seek good fortune and avoid misfortune?" Some argued that dreams are not reliable indicators, while others advised him to act according to circumstances and decide whether to advance or retreat after a year or half a year. Cao Gong, having no choice, set out on the journey. Upon arriving at the county, he found the people simple and honest, and the officials upright and modest, and he felt very at ease.

Several years later, someone suddenly presented a request to open a silver mine. The official submitted a detailed report. He received an imperial order to entrust the task of procurement to him. The official personally went to the mine site to inspect the excavation. When he reached the mine, he saw two white vapor trails, like a rainbow, directly in front of him. The official was startled and fell. He returned to the inn and died at midnight. His family only then realized it was a sign of the white armor. As for the difficulty of dealing with supernatural phenomena versus dealing with people, Liu, the magistrate of Hanyang, was upright and stubborn. He dealt with the exorcist cults too strictly. There were cunning people who appealed to the governor-general. The governor-general warned him, but Liu spoke against the warning. The governor-general was angry and said, "If you truly have talent, there is a case from Mianyang Prefecture. Can you handle it?" Liu nodded. Before this, in Mianyang, there was a girl named Jin Gui who was betrothed to Huang's family. On the wedding day, the wedding sedan arrived at her home, but two brides came out, dressed identically, with the same accessories and demeanor. Therefore, they did not dare to proceed with the ceremony and returned both brides to Jin. Jin's parents could not distinguish between them, so both families sued the court, claiming that the two brides were indistinguishable. The case went from the prefecture to the governor-general, but it remained unresolved for half a year. Therefore, the governor-general gave the case to Liu. Liu requested to bring the case to the governor-general's office for trial and asked to borrow the governor-general's seal for the trial. The governor-general agreed.

At the time of the trial, the magistrate called the two women separately and interrogated them carefully, along with their parents' birth years and months, property, and arrangements, questioning them one by one and verifying their statements, which were consistent. The magistrate then called the two women to the front of the case and said, "Seeing that you two are originally twin sisters, if both are to be given to the Huang family, your parents might not agree. I have specially set up a magpie bridge here, those who can walk across will be united, those who cannot will be separated." He then laid out white cloth like a bridge, from the ceremonial gate directly to his seat, and ordered the two women to walk on the cloth. One woman could not, her tears flowing, while the other was delighted and showed it on her face. The magistrate scolded the woman who cried and ordered her to be driven out of the office, calling the happy woman to step onto the cloth. This woman walked as if on level ground, stepping up to the magistrate's presence. The magistrate secretly held the court seal and struck down from above; both sides were covered with a net, revealing her as a fox, which he cast into the river. Thus, the case was concluded. The governor was greatly pleased and presented a report to promote him to the position of Magistrate of Hanyang Prefecture, from then on, far and near sang praises of the Dragon Diagram's reappearance.

A tea merchant from Hanyang, carrying heavy wealth, was returning home when he was pursued by bandits on the way. He ran to Hanchuan and sought help from the innkeeper. The innkeeper, after some hesitation, said, "If it is truly like this, then this place is not suitable for you to stay. You can quickly go to the home of a certain scholar-official, and you will be safe." He led the merchant to the scholar-official's house. The scholar-official's brothers prepared food and wine, and made up the bed. They instructed him, "If there is any activity at night, just sleep peacefully, do not lightly go out to see." The merchant slept, while the brothers held candles to wait for the bandits. The bandits indeed came, and they fought each other; the scholar-official killed four of them, and the remaining three bandits escaped over the wall. At dawn, they called the merchant up, and went to the county office to report. The county officials arrived early, and took the scholar-official's brothers away. It was because the cunning bandits pretended to be the tea merchant, and first planned to rob and kill him, so they went to the county office at night to beat the drum and seek help. Therefore, Magistrate Liu sent officials to nearby places to arrest the scholar-official's brothers and question them. The scholar-official's brothers recounted the whole story, and requested to release one person to protect their family. The magistrate did not agree, and they were all put in prison. The bandits returned to the scholar-official's house, and killed all the family members before escaping. By the time the magistrate realized, they hurriedly released them, but it was too late. Alas! The magistrate could judge the fox, but he could not escape being deceived by the bandits. Is it not that dealing with monsters is easier than dealing with people?

At the Vubopo Beach, a place of strategic importance for entering Guangzhou, lies a temple dedicated to the Han Vubopo General. In a certain year, a guest who had been collecting debts returned to this spot and anchored his boat there. That night, several boatmen came with knives and entered the boat, saying, "Your life will end here. We are bandits. Come out to die, or we will not let your blood stain the cabin, and you will also need to clean it!" The guest pleaded, "All my possessions will be handed over to you. If you allow me to die with my body intact, I will have no regrets, and I will pay you four hundred gold coins as a reward." The bandits laughed and said, "All you have is in our hands. How could there be another four hundred gold coins?" The guest replied, "You only know about the things in the boat. Do you not know about the rest?" He then showed them a document and said, "This sum is still kept in a certain shop. If you take the document there, you can get it. But if I die while sober, it is very embarrassing. Please allow me to drink until I am drunk, and let me end up wrapped in a torn mat. Would that be acceptable?" Moved by his sincerity, the bandits indeed gave him enough to drink, tied him up, and threw him into the river. Just as he was drowning, a dog jumped in and followed him. Both were carried downstream and hit the shore. The dog stood up and scratched at the temple door. A monk asked who it was, but the dog did not respond. When the monk opened the gate, he saw the dog enter, completely wet and muddy, holding onto his robe without letting go, as if leading him somewhere. They followed the dog to the riverbank and saw the wrapped body. Both wanted to leave, but the dog again made a blocking gesture. The monk understood its intention and carried the body to the temple. He touched the body, and the smell of alcohol was strong, and it still had breath. He unbound the guest, examined the mat and found tooth marks, realizing that the dog had bitten through the ropes. He then gave the guest tea and soup and lay down with him. This translation aims to capture the essence of the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English. It maintains the historical and cultural context, handles idiomatic expressions and syntactic ambiguities appropriately, and uses consistent terminology for proper nouns and key terms.

The next morning, the guest woke up and said, "The thief has taken the water route. We will report to the authorities by land and arrive before the thief." He suspected that the thief would go to a certain place with the bond. The monk agreed and went with him. Indeed, the thief did not arrive, so he informed the innkeeper of the reason and warned him not to reveal it. Soon after, the thief arrived with the bond in hand. The innkeeper pretended to be servile and sent the guest to report to the authorities, and they were all captured. The guest returned home with the dog, lived out his days at home, and did not venture out again, writing a book titled *The Story of the Righteous Dog*. --- Mr. Wang Qian Guang of Floating Sea was a scholar from Wenzhou Prefecture. He came from a poor family and could not support himself, so he lived as a guest in a family that dealt with foreign trade. He frequently saw those who went abroad making enormous profits, so Qian Guang also gathered funds and went with them. When he first arrived in Japan, he made profits many times over. Later, he went again, with many people and goods. A typhoon suddenly struck, and they were blown off course and did not know where they were. When they saw a mountain, they rushed to anchor there, but struck a reef and the ship sank. More than half drowned, and over thirty people climbed onto the shore. The mountain had no resources, and no one had ever been there before. Even if they were not buried at the bottom of the sea, they could not escape becoming ghosts of the mountains. Everyone wept bitterly. They traveled during the day and hid at night, collecting seeds and fruits of plants just to stave off hunger. When it rained and the weather was dark and gloomy, mountain demons and tree spirits came to mock and harass people, and another seven or eight out of ten died.

One day, he walked into an empty valley, where there was a stone cave spacious enough to be like a room, providing shelter from wind and rain. Near the cave, there was a fragrant plant; digging up its roots and eating them, his hunger and thirst were suddenly relieved, and his spirit and energy were refreshed. An expert remarked, "This is ginseng." For more than three months, everyone ate this plant, and when they looked at each other, they saw their complexions and luster as if they were children. He often climbed mountains to look at the sea. Suddenly, there were dozens of small boats; seeing people on the mountain, they anchored and came to ask, learning they were Chinese, then loaded them on board and took them away. They were patrolmen from the outskirts of Korea. Reporting to the king, he was summoned to meet. The king asked about their backgrounds; Hui Guang said he was a scholar. The king laughed and said, "Isn't it that the way of righteousness cannot be practiced, so one floats on a raft in the sea?" He then used "Floating on the Sea" as the topic and asked Hui Guang to compose a poem. Hui Guang picked up the brush and completed it immediately, saying, "Long confined to the life of a scholar, riding a raft to learn about the stars. Not because of the dangers of wind and waves, how could one reach the king's court?" The king praised it, treating him with proper etiquette, often summoning him to meet and repeatedly expressing the wish to return home. After another three years, they finally prepared a boat, sending Hui Guang and the others back home, with the king giving them generous gifts. While in that country, Hui Guang met various officials, composing poems and hosting grand gatherings, all of whom were invited, and he received many gifts and饯before his departure.

By the time he reached home, it had been more than five years. Before that, when Qian Guang was in Korea, one night he dreamed of coming to his home. He saw a large number of monks setting up a memorial service. His wife was crying very sadly, and there was a son wearing mourning clothes present. Qian Guang also cried and woke up. He reflected that after several years without returning, his family must have thought he was dead and set up a memorial. But since he had no son, the one wearing the mourning clothes must be someone else, truly an enigma in the dream, which only moved him to tears. After another year, he arrived home, and the altar was still set up as before, with the mourning clothes placed beside it. The husband and wife held each other, filled with both sorrow and joy. He asked his wife, who had performed Buddhist rituals to summon his spirit, on the very night he had the dream. He asked, "What kind of mourning clothes were those?" She replied, "They were the mourning clothes worn by a nephew who had taken over the family line." He said that he had also seen this person in his dream. He became even more sad.

A Headless Island According to the account of Xingtian, there was an island to which he once drifted. On this island, there were a thousand people, all fat and short, without heads. They had their nipples as eyes, which shone and seemed ready to move; they used their navels as mouths, taking food and sucking it down. Their sounds were hoarse and indistinguishable. When they saw Xingtian with a head, they were all amazed. The men and women crowded around to look, and from their navels, they each extended a tongue about three inches long, competing to lick Xingtian. Xingtian ran to the top of a hill and threw stones at them, causing them to disperse. A knowledgeable person said, "These are the Xingtian figures described in the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas). They were executed by Yu the Great, but their bodies did not decay, and they could still wield axes and shields in dance." I have read that in Yan Shigu's *Deng Ci Si Bei* (Inscription on the Temple of Equal Compassion), the name is written as "Xing Tian." Therefore, the current name "Xingtian" might be a transcription error. Additionally, Xu Yingqiu's *Tan Hui* (Collection of Chats) records that there was a headless person who wove straw sandals. This person was likely a soldier who died in battle but returned to life, appearing as if he had been reborn. His wife and children fed him by inserting food into his throat. If he wanted to eat, they would write the character "饥" (hungry); if he did not want to eat, they would write the character "饱" (full). This state lasted for twenty years before he finally died. There was also a general named Jia Yong who was beheaded, but he held his head and returned to his camp. Standing outside the tent, he asked, "Is it better to have a head or not to have a head?" Someone inside the tent replied, "It is better to have a head." Jia Yong said, "Not so, it is also good to be headless." Are these also examples of Xingtian?

○万年松 In the Phoenix Mountain of Xiangshan County, Guangdong Province, there are several pine trees known as the "ten-thousand-year pines." Westerners climbed ladders to try to take them, but the pines would suddenly move up and down, following the movement of the ladders. The Westerners became angry and shot them with bird guns, firing dozens of shots, but they could not hit the pines. The pines remain as green and lush as ever to this day. ○虹桥板 In the cave of the Great Treasure Peak in Wuyi Mountain, Fujian Province, there are hundreds of large pieces of wood arranged horizontally and diagonally. These pieces of wood have not decayed or fallen for thousands of years, and their color is like that of old camphor wood. Zhu Xi said: "This was a place where people lived during the time of Emperor Yao to avoid floods, and after the water receded, the wood remained." However, the wood does not appear to have been shaped by an axe, as the cave is surrounded by many waters, arranged like a wood merchant's display. The rapids below the mountain are so swift that boats cannot dock. I personally saw this in Wuyi Mountain. Later, when I arrived in Hangzhou, I also saw a piece of "rainbow bridge board" in the collection of Sun Jinggao, which had a slight fragrance, fine and smooth texture, and a poem engraved on it by Liang Shanbo, the lecturer. ○天上过船 On the 14th day of the fifth month in the 50th year of the Qianlong era, a sudden wind and thunderstorm occurred. A passenger boat on the Yangtze River near Yizheng County was blown into the air and landed on a beach in Hongze Lake. The six passengers and all the utensils and dishes on board were undamaged. According to people from Yangzhou, they personally saw a boat pass through the clouds that day, initially thinking it was a large bird. ●卷二○鬼状 The county of Xiangfu in Henan Province is the most bustling and busy. All cases submitted by various prefectures and counties to the court for review are often entrusted to it. Although it regularly accepts self-prosecuted lawsuits, there is no set date for the trial, which often leads to delays.

The High Official Lord Bao was diligent in his official duties. One evening, he received several petitions but did not have time to read them thoroughly, so he quickly passed them on to his advisors. The next day, one of his advisors asked him, "Is it possible to investigate a murder case in a certain place?" The official replied, "I have not seen the petition, how could I know about this?" He then examined the petition and found it was about a case of wife killing her husband. The petition included the name of the lover, who claimed to be blind, and stated that the husband was killed somewhere, and the case had been pending for sixteen years. The official was startled and said, "A case pending for sixteen years is quite unusual." He quickly approved all other petitions but held back this one. The next day, when the official personally questioned those who brought petitions, there was no blind person present. Later, when he checked again, the petition from the blind person was still there. The official asked his clerks, "Do any of you know someone named Duan Shun?" One clerk replied, "Yes, he works as a cook in the provincial governor's kitchen." The official went to the governor to request the arrest of the criminal, and the governor handed the case over to the official, who interrogated Duan Shun, who admitted to the crime without reservation. ### Background and Explanation - **令尹鲍公** - The high-ranking official, likely a provincial governor or prime minister. - **勤于堂事** - Indicates the official's diligence in administrative tasks. - **收呈状** - The process of receiving and processing petitions. - **未及细阅** - Reflects the official's busy schedule. - **幕友** - Advisors or private secretaries who assist the official. - **批发** - Quick approval or passing on of petitions without detailed review. - **案悬十六年** - The long duration of the case, which is unusual and noteworthy. - **刘顺** - The criminal, a fictional character representing a common rascal. - **汴城** - Likely Bianjing (modern-day Kaifeng), an important city during the Song dynasty. - **臬司** - The provincial governor or chief magistrate, a high-ranking official. This translation aims to preserve the original meaning and cultural context while making the text accessible to English readers.

The official, Gong Lian, discovered the true situation and immediately went to dig and verify. The body had not decayed, and the wounds were still clearly visible. Thus, Liu's husband and wife were both executed. ○ The Four Characters to Drive Away the Fox During his tenure as the magistrate of Yucheng, Zhou Gong Shixin encountered a problem at Gengjiazhuang, where Liu Huamin's family was plagued by a fox. Despite numerous attempts to drive it away, all methods failed. They then appealed to the official, who issued a document to the city god. The official agreed to their request, and the fox, appearing in the air, shouted, "You seek the city god, but what can the city god do to me?" The fox's mischief became even worse. The official said that the deity could not control it, and it was extremely difficult to exert force. His friend Shen Songtao said, "I was in Xixian, where a wealthy and influential man's son had just completed his wedding. Under the strict supervision of his father, he feared being attached to his new bride, so he was urged to study far away under a teacher. His father also scolded him, saying, 'You cannot return home without reason.' The son, intimate and close in his early marriage, could not help but have wild thoughts. One day, while sitting alone in his study, he saw a beautiful woman behind the wall, showing half her body, with a lovely gaze flowing. He flirted with her, and she smiled and descended. Just as he was about to move a table and ladder to reach her, he saw a golden-armored deity standing on the wall, holding two red flags. One flag was inscribed with 'Right Door,' and the other with 'Right Night.' The deity beckoned to the woman, who vanished and disappeared. Now, let's try writing these four characters on paper. What do you think? He then cut two pieces of yellow paper, ground cinnabar to write on them, and ordered Liu to take them back and paste them between the door and window. That night, the fox came and stepped back, saying, 'The guardian of the door and night is here. I will let you go for now, but I will come back in three years.' From then on, it was silent. The official handled the matter and ascended and left, not knowing what would happen afterward.

At that time, Jiang, who was well-versed in the story, heard that in the temple of Guilin in Shaoxing, three nuns were also entangled by evil spirits. Jiang then taught them to use cinnabar to write the characters "Right Gate" and "Right Night" according to the method and paste them on the building. The windows opened by themselves without wind, and on the upper floor, foxes scratched and scurried all night, making sounds like iron armor. They stopped only at dawn, and the foxes all fled away. I consider the four characters to be quite plain and not found in any classic, yet they could subdue the foxes like this, so I record it. ○ A Female Ghost Guarding Wealth Awaiting Her Husband In Anyang County, there was a man named Yang who opened a guesthouse. He had a daughter who married a man named Deng from Tangyin County, who was engaged in peddling and was poor. Yang's wife, Du, often provided money and goods to him. Yang stored several tens of taels of white gold in a box, and the wife thought to steal a little bit to give to her husband as capital, but did not find an opportunity. One day, a neighbor invited Yang to drink, and the wife took advantage of her husband leaving, then opened the box, tried several keys, and the lock finally opened. She took out the gold just as Yang suddenly returned. The wife hastily put the gold in her bosom, closed the box and locked it before getting up. But the gold was in her hand, and she had nowhere to hide it, so she went to bury it in the garden soil. At night, Yang opened the box and did not see the gold, knowing it was stolen by his wife. He suspected she gave it to her lover and scolded her in various ways. The wife was extremely angry, waited until her husband was asleep, and hanged herself to death. After her death, the ghost often caused trouble. Yang could not live in peace, so he sold the house and moved far away.

Before the woman's death, Deng had taken his wife to Hubei to live with his uncle. The uncle ran a soy sauce factory and, being over sixty years old and without a son, was very pleased to see his nephew and treated him as his own son. Thus, Deng and his wife enjoyed a life of ease and comfort. Several years later, Yang's daughter, missing her parents, asked her husband to visit them. Deng went, and the old house remained the same, but the owner was different. It was already dusk, and Deng was tired from his journey, wanting to stay at the house. The host politely declined, saying, "The guest rooms are already full, and there is no place to stay. There are only two rooms in the back hall, which are said to be haunted by ghosts that can harm travelers. They are still locked and closed, and no one stays there." Deng replied, "This house used to belong to my father-in-law, and it is a place I am very familiar with. How could there be ghosts? Even if there are, to stay for one night, surely it would be harmless." The host agreed, moved the lamp, opened the door, set up a bed, and swept the dust. Deng unfolded the quilt, removed his shoes, and lay down in his clothes. In the middle of the night, he heard a faint crying sound from the west corner of the hall. He quickly got up and saw a female ghost with disheveled hair and a dirty face, leaning forward to attack him. Deng hurriedly ran, and fortunately, there was a small table in the hall that he used to shield himself. The ghost moved east as he moved west, and the ghost moved south as he moved north. He was terrified and wanted to scream, but he couldn't make a sound. Seeing the moonlight as bright as day in the courtyard, he ran and stood in the moonlight. The ghost followed, but did not dare to attack, only staring at him intently with their eyes. As the moon moved one inch, he stepped back one inch, and the ghost approached one inch. As the moon moved one foot, he stepped back one foot, and the ghost approached one foot. As the moon rose to the wall of the courtyard, Deng stood leaning against the wall.

In a short while, the moon moved to his knee, and a ghost crouched down to pull his foot. Deng sighed, "Who would have thought that Deng would die here!" The ghost, upon hearing this, quickly released his hand and asked, "Who are you?" Deng replied, "I am Deng from Tangyin." The ghost said, "You are my son-in-law, why didn't you say so earlier, almost killing you by mistake?" It then explained the reason for its death and the location where the gold was buried. "While it is still dark and no one knows, quickly take the gold and leave. I was causing mischief to guard this wealth for you. Today, my heart's matter is settled, and I will no longer cause mischief." The ghost then moved to the west corner of the hall and disappeared. Deng went to dig up the ground and indeed found the gold. He took it back and, with the added wealth, became even more prosperous. ○ A Zombie Feeding on Human Blood In Wujiang, there was a scholar named Liu who taught at Jiang's house in Yuanhe County. During the Qingming Festival, he temporarily returned home to sweep the graves. After completing his affairs, he was about to return to the academy. He told his wife, "Tomorrow, I will go to a certain place to visit friends, then take a boat to Changmen. You must rise early to prepare breakfast." His wife followed his instructions and rose at cockcrow to prepare. Liu lived in the countryside, his house facing the river and backed by a mountain. His wife washed rice in the river and picked vegetables in the garden. Everything was ready, but the sky was already light and his husband did not get up. She entered the room to urge him, repeatedly calling out with no response. She lifted the curtain to look and saw her husband lying on the bed with his headless neck and no blood. Greatly frightened, she called the neighbors to come and see. The crowd suspected the wife of having killed the husband and reported it to the authorities. The official came to inspect, ordered to temporarily collect the body, arrested the wife and interrogated her, but finally found no concrete evidence. The wife was placed in prison, and the case remained unresolved for months.

After the neighbors went up the mountain to gather firewood, they discovered a collapsed tomb with an exposed coffin. The coffin was intact, but the lid was slightly open, leading them to suspect that the grave had been disturbed by grave robbers. They called for others to investigate and found that the corpse had a face that looked as if it were still alive, with white hair covering its entire body, and its hands were holding someone's head. Upon closer examination, they recognized the corpse as Liu Xiucai, and they reported the matter to the authorities for an autopsy. The official ordered the removal of the head, but it was tightly held by the corpse, and the combined strength of several people could not pry it open. The official then ordered the executioner to cut off the zombie's arm, and blood flowed profusely. However, Liu Xiucai's head had no blood, as it seemed that the zombie had absorbed all of it. The official then ordered the body to be burned, and the woman was released from prison. The case was concluded. --- ### Mouse Ghost Cui, from Hanyang, was a wealthy man who was chosen as the magistrate of Yunnan. He brought his family to the post and left an old servant to guard the gate, sealing everything after the hall and leaving. Several years later, he resigned from office and returned home. He stayed for only a few days when the family collectively reported that there was a strange occurrence on the Buddha pavilion every night. Cui, who was usually brave, moved his bed to stay downstairs, thinking to observe the strange occurrence. At the beginning of the night, he extinguished the candle and went to bed. Immediately, he heard sounds of knocking on the table, hitting the chair, and walking around the building, as if officials were leaving with servants dragging boards. A short while later, the sounds gradually descended the stairs, and one step of the ladder was descended, as if a hammer was hitting the ladder. Cui was extremely frightened and clapped the bed and shouted loudly, as if someone was dragging a hammer back up the stairs. The family gathered together and lit the stairs with a torch to investigate. Nothing was found, and they further believed that it was either a demon or a ghost. They invited a shaman to pray, but it was ineffective, and the entire county spread the rumor that Cui's family had a ghost.

Cui Xiu had a troupe of actors in the pear garden, among whom were several bold individuals who wished to see a ghost. At night, they painted their faces and changed their clothes, with one person dressed as the God of Exorcism and another as General Zhou standing by his side, lighting candles to wait. Suddenly, a mouse scurried down from the top of the shrine, its tail as large as a cudgel. The two men quickly chased after it. The mouse, due to its large tail, moved sluggishly and was soon caught. Upon closer inspection, its tail was found to be a mass of accumulated dust, weighing several pounds. Cui Xiu suddenly realized, "Years ago, this mouse stole lamp oil, and I secretly caught its tail from behind. The mouse struggled to escape, leaving its tail behind, covered in grease and blood. Over time, the dust accumulated, causing this condition, dragging on the ground with a sound. It's laughable that the prayers over the past few months were in vain, seeing only ghosts." ○ A Woman Possessed by a Turtle Spirit A woman from Wu County, named Sun Xiangquan, married a scholar from the same county. After eating a turtle, she developed a strange illness: when happy, she would dress up beautifully and dance in various ways; when angry, she would throw pots and bowls and curse without restraint. Sometimes she would not eat for two or three days, or one meal could feed several people. Gradually, she became emaciated.

The girl was deeply loved by her grandmother and was therefore brought back to care for her illness, but all rituals, prayers, and medical treatments were ineffective. After several days, her illness would temporarily subside, and during those times she would be as healthy as usual. Family members asked about her condition, and the girl said, "I first saw a man wearing a black cap and green robe blowing air towards my face, and then I could not control my body. All my words and actions were done by the green-robed man." When asked, "Why do you eat more than several people?" she replied, "It's not me who is eating. A blue-clothed person and two black-clothed people approached the green-robed man for food, and they used me to eat and drink to satisfy them. When the green-robed man left, he would always stretch his neck, lick his tongue three times, and leap three times with his feet, but I don't know why." At that time, Xiangquan was a guest in the entourage of Governor Bi of Henan. The girl's family sent a swift messenger to inform him of her illness. Xiangquan immediately gathered his belongings and returned, taking the girl to seek refuge in the Yuanmiao Temple in the hall of the True Man in the Straw Raincoat. The supernatural affliction continued as before. Xiangquan thought that taking the girl far away might help, so he rented a boat and planned to go to Yangzhou. Gu Qingsha, an observer from Wuxi and a close friend of Xiangquan, heard about the matter and welcomed them to his home. The supernatural being also followed them there. The observer spoke solemnly and earnestly, hoping to persuade the girl with righteous reasoning. The girl covered her ears and said, "Don't let the stale talk of迂儒污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污污

As they approached Zhenjiang, the girl suddenly said, "If they were to go to Yangzhou, we fear the river god, Jiang Shen, and cannot cross the river. What shall we do?" Xu replied, "I have a plan. We need not wait until the appointed day; we can marry her now." The girl immediately collapsed and cried out in pain, suffering from severe abdominal pain as if wanting to die. Sun feared that the girl would die and allowed her to return by boat to her hometown. Her abdominal pain suddenly stopped. By the appointed day, the family was in great fear, worried that something terrible might happen to the girl, but she was miraculously unharmed. Sun then sent a letter to Governor Bi, requesting the help of Master Zhang from Longhushan to exorcise the evil. Upon receiving the letter, Master Zhang dispatched Judge Zou. They set up a platform and performed rituals for three days and nights, after which the girl's illness was cured. Sun asked, "What was the nature of this evil?" Judge Zou replied, "The one in green robes was a turtle, the one in blue robes was a shrimp, and the one in black robes was a tortoise. Their lair was under the pavilion in the center of Shihu Lake. Because your son-in-law's family had killed too many of their descendants, they led their kind to seek revenge. We just sent the Six Celestial Spirits to capture them all, so your daughter is safe now." I have learned that the river god is named Qixiang, as recorded in the *Book of Curiosities*. ○ A strange event involving thunder occurred at Guazhu in Jintan. There was a man whose son was betrothed to a family at a young age. Shortly after, the man died, and his wife vowed to raise the orphan despite frequent famines. When the son grew up, he could not perform the wedding rites, so he instructed the matchmaker to break the engagement and find another suitor. The couple consulted the girl, but she was resolute and did not change her mind. The matchmaker returned with the message, leaving the mother and son at a loss for what to do.

After a long time, the mother called out to her son, saying, "For the past ten years, enduring hunger and cold, I have harbored no other thoughts than hoping you would establish a family, continuing your father's lineage. Now living alone together, even a hundred years would not help. I have already arranged to remarry with a certain surname, receiving some money to marry you off and some to pay off old debts. Now the money is all on the bed; you can see it." The son remained silent and could not speak. The mother wept and said, "Go quickly to the matchmaker and tell him; I will wait here until you and your wife are married before leaving." The son grieved deeply but did not respond, and the mother urged him repeatedly until he went. At that time, there were bandits in the nearby gambling hall who were eavesdropping. They took advantage of the son's nighttime departure, burrowed through the wall, and stole the money. The mother woke up in the morning to find the money missing and then hanged herself. The next day, the son came with the matchmaker. Opening the door, he did not see his mother and found it strange. He made the matchmaker sit in the guesthouse and entered the house himself. Seeing that his mother was already dead, he grieved deeply and also hanged himself. The matchmaker found it strange that he had not come out for so long, called out to him but got no response, and peeked into his bedroom. Both mother and son were found hanging, and the neighbors were shocked and cried out. All the neighbors gathered, but none could understand the reason. The matchmaker then ran to inform the girl's parents. Hearing the news, the girl also hanged herself. It was mid-winter, and the sky suddenly became overcast and dark. Lightning and thunder struck together, killing seven of the gamblers. The boy and girl both had their necks broken but then revived, while only the mother, despite being rescued, did not wake up.

Those who heard of the matter at the time all sighed and said: "Chastity, loyalty, filial piety, and virtue—these three virtues were all present in one family, yet they all died at the same time, not by their own will. As if no one were to defend or clear their names, the thunder would defend them, this is indeed strange. As for the two individuals, a man and a woman, who were saved, they were made to marry properly, while the chaste mother was left to wander aimlessly and not return. Those who acted to preserve them in a roundabout way were so, who would say that the thunder is without knowledge?" --- Regarding the dream of Scholar Cao: Scholar Cao, whose courtesy name was Fuqing, was in his early twenties when he fell ill in the winter month and lay bedridden for five or six days. One day, he dreamed that he was on the west horizontal street of the city, and someone called out his name from behind. He turned to look but did not recognize the person. He asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am here to summon you by the command of the City God." Scholar Cao asked, "What business do you have with me?" The person said, "You will know when you go." Just then, his uncle, Scholar Cao Yongzhang, arrived and spoke to the official, saying, "Let me accompany my nephew. What do you think?" The official nodded in agreement. On the way, Scholar Cao asked the official, "I have recently heard that the City God is no longer Yang Gong. Who is in charge now?" The official replied, "It is Duke Yuan of the Eastern Han Dynasty." They then parted ways. Scholar Cao Yongzhang accompanied Scholar Cao Fuqing, walking swiftly. The moonlight on the streets was very bright, but they felt a chill in the air. The houses and doors on both sides were all closed, and on the door lintels, there were several strings of paper money. For several miles, the scene was the same.

Soon Yongzhang arrived at a vast wilderness, far off saw a high wall like a city, directly to the south there were double doors. He knocked on it, but there was no response inside. He opened the door and entered, and was ordered to walk along the eastern corridor. A little further, he did not know where he was. Feeling tired, he wanted to rest a bit, and leaned against the door of a gate. He saw several dozen people in front of a room, some had ropes tied to their feet, others had ropes tied around their necks, sitting and standing were not uniform; half of the room behind was filled with sheep and pigs. Unwillingly, he sat outside the railing. Suddenly, all the prisoners extended one hand out of the door as if searching for something, the sheep and pigs all came to smell his clothes and bite his feet. He was very embarrassed and frightened, and a bystander shouted, "Do not be rude! What you need will be given to you immediately." After a while, officials came to summon him, showing him a ticket, and he suddenly realized he was in his previous life, and said, "Your father and I were involved in a contract, that person was untrustworthy, so I am here to testify, do not be afraid." When he reached the office door, a clerk came with a book, looking as if he was asking for regulations. One of the prisoners said, "Yes, yes, I will get it from my house later." Then they stopped. Suddenly, someone appeared in a short robe and bare feet, looking around as if investigating official business. The officials shouted at him, and he quickly hid. Only a wisp of black smoke on the wall could be seen, dispersing slowly.

Soon after, I heard that the official had taken his seat to interrogate a suspect, using severe punishments and torture, the sounds of which were very harsh. After a short while, someone came out and said, "There is no need to appear in court; he has revealed the truth." I saw a prisoner being led out: his hair was disheveled, covering his forehead, one hand on his chest, and the other on his back, with a rope through his chest, his hands bound in front and behind, walking tiredly and slantingly, indicating that he was a captured prisoner. Everyone scattered before the office, and there was no one's trace, silent and empty. I peeked inside, and saw a hall with three rooms, with side corridors lined with sedan chairs, banners, staffs, and ceremonial implements, all resembling a human world's office. Stepping forward a few steps, my maternal uncle Zhou Zijian was already there, saying, "Have you come to testify?" We comforted each other, as it was said that the old man was a debt collector from a past life. At that time, the old man's second brother had just died, and I mentioned it, to which the old man wept and said, "He is also here, but I cannot bear to see him."
