Angered by their offensive words, he sat down and humiliated them. Wang Wanli was very angry and drove away the spirits to scare Wang Bi.

Bi was sitting late one night when he suddenly heard a mournful wail outside his window. He opened the door and looked out, but the courtyard was empty under the bright moonlight. The next day, a ghost appeared at his door, weeping and claiming innocence. Bi then said, "Did I poison you? If it wasn't me, I will clear your name." The ghost replied, "I have seen many people, but only you can be trusted. That's why I came to you, not out of malice. If you truly clear my name, you should gather ten people as witnesses." Bi followed the ghost's advice. The ghost continued, "I am the daughter of the Zhou family, living in Heihe, Fengzhou, Datong. My father is Heqing, and my mother is Zhang. I was born when the moon was in the position of Geng, so my nickname is Yuexi. At sixteen, my mother fell ill, and my father summoned Wang Wanli to diagnose her, which is how I met him. A hundred and five days after my mother's death, my father slept during the day while my brother was away gathering firewood. I happened to walk along the wall, and Wang used a curse based on my birth date to trap me. I fell unconscious and could not speak. Wang carried me to the willow forest, tied me to a tree, first shaved my hair and wrapped it with colorful threads; then he cut out my heart, liver, and other organs, including my eyes, tongue, ears, nose, and fingers, ground them into powder, and placed them in gourds. He then made a paper figure and used a curse to enslave me. Whenever I was slightly lazy in my duties, he would prick me with needles, causing unbearable pain. Yesterday, I was offended by you, so I sent myself to report to you. I couldn't bear it. If you can pity me, don't let me carry this grudge to the netherworld. I swear to become your son. The men present here must keep this secret; if it is revealed, disaster will follow." After finishing, the ghost wept even more bitterly. Bi and the ten others all shed tears, wrote down Yuexi's words, signed their names, and secretly reported the matter to the county.

The county re-examined the case as it was at first, and urgently arrested Wanli and his uncle for interrogation. At first, they resisted, and Moon West argued with them, the arguments being very bitter. Moon West then requested to search their traveling bags, and they obtained talismans, seals, measuring sticks, long needles, short nails, and other items. Wanli then confessed, saying, "Wanli is a man from Luling. He sold his skills to Xingyuan, where he met Liu the Taoist Master, who taught him the method of capturing life, which was roughly as Moon West said. Wanli did not believe it, so Liu took out a multi-colored silk cloth from his bag, inside which was hair like a bullet. Liu pointed and said, 'This is Li Yannu from Xianning, whom I have captured. If you can pay me 750,000 strings of cash, I will make her serve you.' Wanli happily agreed. Liu performed a ritual, burned talismans, and invoked the spirits. Yannu spoke from the air, 'What does my master command me to do?' Liu replied, 'You should follow Mr. Wang. He is a benevolent man, and it will not be difficult.' Wanli paid the money as agreed and fully learned the method. Later, passing through Fangzhou, he met a certain Mr. Kuang, and their conversation was in agreement. He also obtained a certain Geng Wantan, whom he also kept as a servant, and the amount of money he paid was as Liu had said. Liu warned Wanli to never eat beef, dog meat, or beefsteak. If he forgot and ate beefsteak, the matter would fail, and there would be nothing more to say. The county sent a document to Fengzhou, summoning Heqing as a witness. When Heqing arrived, he was somewhat suspicious and mingled among the crowd. Biyang asked, 'Who is your father?' Yue Xi called out from a gap in the wall, 'The one in black clothing and a reed hat.' Heqing wept, and Yue Xi also wept. After the weeping, they recounted family matters and comforted each other as if it were their ordinary life. The officials prepared a complete case file and submitted it to the higher authorities, who were about to decide on the punishment, but Wanli died in prison."

At first, Bi returned from the county office, and his family and guests were drinking and enjoying themselves. Suddenly, they heard crying sounds, and Bi inquired about it. The ghost said, "I am the naughty boy Geng and the deceased Li Yannu. The injustice of Yue Xi has been redressed, but will the old man not pity us two?" Bi was puzzled by this, and the naughty boy said, "Yue Xi and the old man made an agreement to be father and son, am I not the old man's child? Why is our treatment so unequal?" Bi had no choice but to go to the county office again and file a petition. The official arrested the father of the naughty boy, Debao, and the father of the deceased, Fubao, to appear, and what they said was all verified. From then on, the three ghosts stayed at Bi's home, following him during the day and sleeping with him at night, although they were not visible, their sounds were clear and distinct. Bi asked calmly, "There should be a god at the door, how did you enter?" Yue Xi said, "There is none, I only see paintings hung on the door." Bi said, "I want to burn paper money for you, what do you think?" The ghost replied, "It is unnecessary." Bi then asked, "Can your spirit and energy remain in the world for a long time?" The ghost replied, "When their time comes, they will disperse."

The mischievous child was skilled in singing and, when meeting with a drink, would sing a Han Dynasty tune from Shanxi to celebrate someone's longevity. When Bi continuously poured wine to the ground, the child would become drunk and lose his composure. Once, a guest played a prank by substituting vinegar for the wine, and the child angrily exclaimed, "It almost stung my throat and lips! What kind of little brat are you, playing such a mean trick?" He continued to complain about the guest's private affairs, which made the guest feel ashamed and flee. Yue Xi was especially known for his cunning and wisdom, often teasing with the sons of Bi, whose words were often humorous. Sometimes, when the sons of Bi lost the argument, Yue Xi would hit towards the noisy place, and he would laugh and say, "Ghosts are invisible, why bother, brother? It only shows his lack of wisdom." After eight months, the playful interactions finally came to an end, and silence prevailed.

The deity requested the destruction of someone named "戚南元" who was appointed as the magistrate of Guian. There is a shrine dedicated to the deity, which is very powerful and the temple is exceptionally magnificent. One day, when passing by, it coincided with the festival period, and thousands of people gathered. "戚" reported to the deity, saying, "It has been a long time without rain. If the ritual can bring rain, it would be good; otherwise, the temple will be destroyed, and there will be no pardon for the sin." They carried a wooden puppet to the bridge, but it did not rain, so they sank it in the water. Several days later, while the boat was traveling, the wooden puppet suddenly jumped out of the water and into the boat. The servant turned pale and ran to report, saying, "The deity is coming! The deity is coming!" "戚" laughed and said, "This is the deity requesting destruction." He ordered the boat to be tied to the side. Looking at the shore, there was a community shrine. He sent a clever servant to change clothes and enter the shrine, instructing him to wait for the person from the water to emerge and bring them in chains. Later, it turned out to be true, as the people involved in the festival had bribed someone to do this. They then burned the wooden puppet and punished the person who acted falsely. In the year 丙辰 of the Qianlong era, I passed through Quanzhou in Guangxi and saw a chest on a sheer cliff, which looked like wood but was not, and had no lid. The boatman said it was where Zhuge Liang had hidden his military books. In the year 甲辰, I passed through Quanzhou again, which had been fifty years, and looked up to examine it closely; it was undamaged, and I wondered how there could be such an immortal wood in the world. Later, when the Governor of Guangxi, Mr. Qi, passed through the area, he used a telescope to measure it and confirmed that it was indeed a wooden chest, not a stone chest. The river below was swift, and it was difficult to stop the boat for long, and I still remained doubtful in my heart.

After reading *Yongzhuang Xiaopin*, it is recorded that during the reign of Emperor Jiajing, the emperor often sent Magistrate Jiang from Nanchang to retrieve military texts. Jiang built a cloud ladder and recruited strong soldiers to climb it. They found a coffin, about a foot thick, yellow-black in color, with a lid on top. When they opened it, they discovered white bones inside, a skull as large as a cart wheel, two teeth over a foot long, sharp as a knife. They took the bones back to the emperor. The magistrate reported the truth and buried the bones on the side of a mountain. That night, Jiang dreamed of a man with a tiger head, over ten feet tall, who burst through the door, glared angrily, and said, "I am the third son of the water god Wuzhiqi, named Bēnyún. I can enter and exit the clouds and winds, and swallow and bite tigers and leopards. When Yu was controlling the floods, my father and I fought him in battle. I was defeated and hid in the mountains and lakes. Bo Yi came to set fire, nearly burning me, and I bit his finger and escaped. Yu the Great was greatly angered and ordered the celestial general Gengchen to use the Divine Xao Sword to cut me down and throw my body into the river. At that time, my father was still alive and ordered the water spirits to fetch a piece of waterlogged wood for a coffin and bury me here. When the劫is full, I will still want to come back to earth to seek revenge. You have ordered a certain soldier to open the coffin and mutilate my body. But you are a noble person, sent by the Son of Heaven, and I cannot harm you. I will take the life of the soldier who broke the coffin!" After speaking, he left. The next day, the soldier indeed died suddenly.

I investigate that yinchen wood is the wood that existed before the chaos of the early universe, having endured the ashes of great disasters, it remains unbroken for ten thousand years, thus standing majestically and imperishable for thousands of years. It was taken by Inspector Jiang, hence it is still exposed today. In the year Bingwu, I traveled to Wuyi Mountain and saw the natural bridge of the caves in Mount Dazang standing tall and dense, regretting the absence of someone like Inspector Jiang to build a ladder to the clouds and explore it further. ● Chapter 40: The Formula for the Emperor's Flowing Plaster, Yan Ji was skilled in divination and exorcism, and his medium each year must be a deity. In the year Wu Zi, the medium was Chen Zhenren, also known as Beard Elder, who was adept at discussing matters with others. One day, the group discussed why drowned ghosts always carry the smell of sheep dung. Chen said: "When a soul enters the ground, it becomes smelly upon contact with water. Rivers are filled with silt and accumulated filth, and when the soul is immersed in it for seven days, it develops the smell of sheep dung. Ghosts from rivers who carry the smell of sheep dung cannot harm people, but after five years without this smell, they can cause disasters to people."

Ghosts of Fire Victims It is said that the ghosts of those who died in fires have incomplete limbs and must find companions to merge with in order to take form, sometimes merging two or three, but not necessarily equally. The rules for merging are: the old do not merge with the young, and men do not merge with women. Plants and Trees Becoming Spirits It is also said that for plants and trees to become spirits, they must receive the essence of the moonlight, but only on the night of the Gengshen day. The moonlight on that night contains the emperor's flowing juice, which appears as countless olives with golden threads, hanging down in a string. When plants and trees receive this essence, they can become spirits. Fox spirits and ghosts, when they consume it, can display supernatural powers. Plants and trees have nature but no life, while the flowing juice has nature and can supplement life. Fox spirits and ghosts already have life, so consuming the essence greatly benefits them. The Method of Summoning the Deceased Chen Yikui, a practitioner of the "讨亡术" ritual, was skilled in this method. If someone died with unfinished matters, their descendants could not inquire about them without performing the ritual. They must offer four taels of gold to Chen to perform the ritual. The ritual involved selecting a child over six years old who was familiar with the deceased. The child was instructed to close his eyes and sit in a cross-legged position. Chen wrote a talisman on the child's head, which contained the phrase "果斋寝К八埃台戾." At that time, the child's family was ordered to burn paper horses outside the door. After writing the talisman, the child fell asleep with closed eyes. The child then saw the local land god carrying a package, leading a horse and commanding to ride, and together they went to the underworld to find the deceased. After inquiring about the unfinished matters of the deceased's life, the child woke up. This ritual was especially popular in Hangzhou.

A local official, known as the "land god," was believed to be the spirit of Xiao He, a famous chancellor from the Han dynasty. One day, while performing a ritual, a young boy suddenly opened his eyes wide and shouted, "I am the Han chancellor Xiao He. What kind of person is Chen Yikui, daring to use evil arts to drive me around as a child's packhorse and mount! Because you recited the *Taisang Xuanjing* to teach me, I dare not disobey. If you dare to do so again, I will report this to the Supreme Being, and I will be secretly punished." Chen Yikui, greedy for profit, did not change his ways. One day, as Chen performed another ritual, the land god led the boy through the City of Wrongful Deaths, where disembodied limbs and fierce-faced evil spirits, holding heads and throwing bones, filled the area in front of the boy's horse. The boy was shocked and woke up, after which he no longer followed Chen's rituals. Chen, having no choice, taught the boy sword techniques, instructing him to hold a sword and recite the previous text. The land god then led the boy to the same place, and the boy, following instructions, began to dance with the sword, chopping and killing several spirits. The spirits shouted in alarm. Suddenly, they saw a flash of golden light in the sky and exclaimed, "Lord Guan has descended!" They saw the land god bowing in front of the emperor's horse, muttering incoherently. Soon, the land god led the boy's horse to the emperor's presence, where the emperor said, "I consider Chen Yikui, this old servant, to be following the teachings of the Supreme Taoist. Therefore, I cannot immediately destroy his rituals. You may convey this message to him: if he dares to perform these rituals again, I will decapitate him." The emperor then ordered Zhou Cang to strike the boy once with the back of the Green Dragon Sword. The boy shouted loudly and woke up, thereafter abandoning his intention and no longer following Chen's rituals.

Chen, having become increasingly poor over time, had no means of sustenance and continued to steal from other places to practice his craft. That autumn, he dreamed of reaching the Black Pavilion Bay outside Qiantang Gate, where he saw a wooden placard listing his crimes, and it was written that he would be executed on the 13th day of the ninth month as the demon Chen. After waking up, he paid little attention to it and gradually told his dream to others. On the appointed day, someone curious wanted to verify his words and went to Chen's home. They saw Chen dressed in Daoist robes, his body covered in talismans, and reciting scriptures, as if he had a method of exorcism. After a long while, he suddenly shouted, "Beheaded! Beheaded!" The crowd asked, "You can still speak, why say you were killed?" He replied, "Fortunately, I have many souls, so I cannot be killed, but I cannot live much longer." Not long after, he died of illness. Upon examination, his neck had good skin and flesh, but the inner bone was already broken.

In Zhushan County, Hubei Province, there is a heretical sect led by the Zhushan Sect Leader, which is passed down to only one person and specializes in stealing from merchants. The sect has two branches, with the Broken Head Sect Leader being a disciple of the Zhushan Sect Leader. Those who learn this method will be struck by lightning. Those who learn the method must first swear an oath before the founder, willing to forgo human form for seven generations, only then will the method be taught. To avoid thunderbolts, seven used maternity pails are required. On New Year's Eve, one must wear heavy mourning clothes and arrange the silver carried over the past three years on a table. After bowing, one must repeatedly insert oneself into the pails, to suppress the gods. A partner of a wealthy merchant from Jiangxi Province lost 3,000 gold coins at night. In the morning, the boxes were untouched, only the paper wrapping the silver had small holes from insects. Because he remembered a ship passing through Xiangyang, there was an old man who boarded the ship and stayed in the cabin. Every night, he burned a stick of incense, made three bows to the air, and muttered spells. Listening to it, I couldn't understand, suspecting it was the Zhushan heretical sect. Those who know use red paper to wrap the silver and surround it with five grains, so the method cannot be practiced.

Guan Yu's Manifestation in Tongcheng According to Yao Taishi Kong Chen of Tongcheng, he once witnessed a manifestation of Guan Yu at the office of a certain censor in the northern region. At the office, friends and relatives gathered, but the censor's younger relative sat quietly in his study, not coming out. Someone invited him, saying, "A乩仙 is merely a spirit of literature; I am Guan Yu, the Lord of the Three Kingdoms, and by law, I should not descend to the乩坛." The guest asked, "Can Guan Yu be summoned?" The younger relative replied, "Yes, and you can also observe his appearance." He then informed the censor. The censor personally prayed to Guan Yu. The younger relative looked solemn and said, "The gentlemen must fast for three days, choose a clean room with clear windows, and set up incense offerings. The gentlemen should prepare ten large jars filled with clear water in another place, and kneel outside the jars while waiting." The younger relative, dressed entirely in blue, looked up to the sky and wept bitterly, speaking earnestly as if making a plea. Suddenly, a cloud of five colors appeared, and the Lord of the Three Kingdoms, with a long beard and a ceremonial robe, descended from the sky, supported by General Zhou Cang. He sat facing south, and said to the younger relative, "Do not worry, your enemies will be avenged." The younger relative knelt and wept again. General Zhou Cang then carried the Lord of the Three Kingdoms away, and only the auspicious aura remained. The gentlemen were blinded by the dazzling light of the golden armor and could not open their eyes, so they all knelt on the ground, separated by the water jars.

One day, the young man from the family left without saying goodbye. Everyone suspected that a high-ranking official had suddenly fallen ill while traveling, but no one knew the reason for the grudge. ○ The Rat's Bow and the Weasels' Performance Zhou Yangzhong, from Shaoxing, was serving as an official in Anhui Province and brought his nephew to stay in the county seat. There was an empty room with three rooms, which no one had dared to occupy before. Zhou did not believe the rumors and cleaned the room thoroughly, moving into the inner room and lighting a candle to lie down. Suddenly, the door opened by itself, and a white rat stood like a person with its hands clasped. It walked several steps, bowed deeply, and bowed again at the foot of the bed, then leaped onto the bed. Beside it were two weasels, dragging long tails and holding reeds, performing a swordplay act imitating Lu Bu. They seemed to be the slaves of the white rat, seeking favor with the rat king. The white rat crouched at Zhou's feet, slowly crawling up his body, making him feel as soft as tofu, and he felt quite very happy; when it reached his chest, he felt as if a stone was pressing on his body, and he could not move. The rat licked its lips and ate the foam. Gradually, it retreated and crawled down Zhou's feet, bowed once, and left through the door. Zhou remained unharmed.

His nephew was away, and he saw that when the mouse first came, it bowed politely and the door opened; when it left, it bowed again, and the door closed as before. The poem by Han says, "The mouse bows and the door is square," is it truly so? ○ When the Marshal Wen appeared to show his spirit, the magistrate of Yanghu, Pan Benzhi, whose grandfather used to open a money shop, suddenly lost a thousand gold pieces. The magistrate of Renhe, Li Gong Xueli, personally investigated and found six hundred gold pieces in the ash. Li Gong believed it was the clerks' fault and wanted to take them to the county for questioning. The old man said, "These people are all experienced and hardworking, they must not have done it; take my servants to interrogate them instead." The clerks said, "If it were not for the owner's kindness, we would all be punished. However, we should also go to the Marshal's temple to confess our hearts." They began to arrive at the temple gate, and one person inside suddenly closed their eyes and shouted, "Do not hit! Do not hit! I will say, I will say." You can take the four hundred gold pieces in the jar and have your brother carry them to the temple. "The others saw this scene and searched his home together, finding the four hundred gold pieces still in the jar. His brother then carried the four hundred gold pieces on his head to the temple. Li Gong recorded their confession and ruled, "This is a method of the spirit realm, not the official law. Wait until they calm down, then take them to the county for judgment." Soon after, the person had drowned himself. ○ A zombie resisted the thief. A fish vendor at the stone archway on Yangshi Street in Hangzhou, every day at dawn, left the Gen Gate to sell fish and saw faint lights in the forest, with a beautiful woman sitting alone spinning. Every day it was like this, with no one else around, he suspected it was a ghost but was not afraid.

One day, an old man with a white beard spoke to him, saying, "You admire this woman and wish to marry her, do you not? I have a method; if you follow my instructions, the matter can be accomplished. Tomorrow morning, you must carry a ball of rice into her room. When she opens her mouth, stuff it with the rice, carry her back, and do not let her see the light of day. Then she will be no different from a normal person." Following his instructions, he indeed obtained the woman. They lived in a secluded room, and their marriage was very harmonious. After a year, they had a son who could eat and, on rainy days, would go downstairs to cook. Over twenty years, they married a daughter-in-law and had grandchildren. Their family also became moderately prosperous, and they opened a tea house. One day, it was extremely hot, and her eyes glowed like fire. Her wife heard her mother-in-law coming down the stairs but heard no sound. Upon looking, there was a pool of blood, which had transformed into a zombie. Her husband understood the situation and was not overly distressed. He simply bought a coffin to bury her, and she would come out of the coffin at night. Often, when thieves entered the front door, someone would block them; when they entered the back door, someone else would block them. All of this was done by the zombies guarding the house. ○ A Deceased Father Transformed into a Monster: A certain magistrate from the northwest, whose father had died many years ago, suddenly appeared one day riding a horse, looking just as he did in life. He said, "I have become a deity, but I still love you and cannot forget my feelings, so I have come to see you. You can clean a quiet room for me to live in." Although his son was suspicious, the sound of his voice, his appearance, and his mannerisms were indeed those of his father. So he treated him as if he were alive. During the day, he read books, and at night, he either slept or stayed awake. After a while, he also ate normally, and they lived peacefully together.

After a year, Zhang Sanfeng, the Taoist master from Jiangxi, passed through the area, and the governor informed him. Zhang said, "It's a demon. How could a true immortal remain in the city without any extraordinary signs? Can you show yourself to me?" The governor told the man's father, who happily replied, "I was just about to meet the Taoist master." The demon spoke as usual. The Taoist master said, "This demon is beyond my knowledge." He consulted an old Taoist ritual specialist, who said, "We should take advantage of its lack of vigilance to expose it." One day, while the man's father was writing, the ritual specialist suddenly shouted from behind him, and the demon stood like a stunned rooster, motionless. The ritual specialist drew out a *Tianpeng scale* from his sleeve and began measuring it from the head. For every inch measured, it became one inch shorter, until it reached the feet and disappeared, leaving only its clothes and hat, with just one leg bone remaining. The ritual specialist said, "This is the true bone of your late grandfather. A fox burrowed into the bones, and a wild dog carried them out, absorbing the essence of the sun and moon to become this demon. That's why it could speak of things that happened before his death. If it were to mate with a woman and gain yin essence, the disaster would be even greater." The governor wanted to ask for the bone to bury it, but the ritual specialist refused, saying, "Do not bring future calamities." He took the bone away. I have read in *Tanghuai Records* that during the Tang Dynasty, after Li Ba died, he returned home. He arranged his servants and everything was in order, but he lived alone in a room and did not appear to anyone. One day, his descendants forced him to show himself, and he transformed into a demon with a green face and fangs, his head as large as a carriage wheel, and his eyes like wild fire. His descendants fled in terror, and Li Ba never appeared again.

**The Dry Deer** The dry deer is not a human, but a type of zombie. In Yunnan, there are many mines rich in five metals, and miners who get trapped by the earth and cannot escape, sometimes for decades or even centuries, are nourished by the earth and metal qi, so their bodies do not decay. Though they are not dead, in reality, they are dead. Miners often light a lamp on their foreheads to navigate the darkness underground as they dig through the earth. When they encounter a dry deer, the creature is very pleased and tells the miner they are cold and asks for tobacco to smoke. When given tobacco, the dry deer inhales it immediately and finishes it. It then kneels and begs the miner to take it away. The miner says, "I came here for gold and silver, and there is no reason to leave empty-handed. Do you know where the gold veins are?" If the dry deer leads the way, the mine will be rich in gold and silver. Before leaving, the miner deceives the dry deer by saying, "I will go out first, and I will use a basket to bring you out of the cave." They tie a bamboo basket to a rope, pull the dry deer up into the air, and then cut the rope, causing the dry deer to fall and die. There was once a kind-hearted mine manager who felt pity for the dry deer and pulled up several of them. When exposed to the wind, their clothes and bones turned into water, and the smell was foul. Those who heard of it would all die from plague. Therefore, after this, miners who pulled up dry deer would always cut the rope, fearing that they would die from their foul breath. It is further said that when there are many people and few deer, the crowd would bind the ghost and make it lean against the earthen wall, sealing it all around with mud to make a mound, and placing a lampstand on top, then it would no longer cause harm. If there are few people and many deer, they would be entangled and die without being released. ○ Stone Mr. descended from the bridge of the ford. Stone Mr. operated a rice shop, and his family was always wealthy. Suddenly, he fell ill, and a female ghost possessed him, speaking with a Hangzhou accent. The ghost said that Stone Mr. in his previous life was a neighbor of the female ghost and operated a pawnshop. The father of the female ghost was traveling outside, and they had a moon terrace. The man and woman were mutually fond of each other. One day, while whispering on the moon terrace, the uncle of the female ghost came from outside, and was seen by him. The man fled, and the woman was shamed and humiliated by her uncle, feeling ashamed and committing suicide. The man was shocked and returned, and heard that the woman had died, also falling ill and dying. The man was reborn as a male in the Stone family. The female ghost searched for over thirty years, finally learning that she was in Suzhou, and therefore came searching. The Stone family begged, willingly to serve as an ancestor and be worshipped in the study. Stone Mr. indeed recovered. Soon after, a girl died from smallpox. An old woman saw the girl sitting on the ghost's knee, and the ghost hugged and played with her. Stone Mr. became very angry and cursed the ghost, stopping the ritual offerings. The ghost caused great harm, so they begged for mercy and resumed the rituals as before, and the ghost remained calm. After several years, suddenly one day, the ghost possessed Stone Mr. and said, "I am leaving now, quickly prepare a banquet, a carriage, and a boat." The family asked why, and the ghost said, "The madam of the supervisor came to take me to be reborn in the Zhang family in Yangzhou, the third son is me."

I asked someone to check, and it turned out to be true. ○ Transformations Every year between August and September, stones in the Yutian River turn into jade, and collectors gather them by kicking the stones. Soldiers on both banks of the river pass on a drum signal, and when they see someone bending over to collect the jade, they must present it as an offering; otherwise, they will be punished. Once the jade is collected, it will grow again next year. When there is heavy fog, stones on the mountains transform into mountain jade, and stones in the river transform into water jade. In the Russian Empire, thousands of birds come, and when they encounter heavy fog, they immediately stop moving and transform into gray mice. Other substances transform into tigers and deer, and when two ants fight, they transform into flies and shrimp. Crawling insects transform into dragonflies, and when they are caught, they transform into scorpions out of anger. ○ People turning into trees Foreigners from the Mongolian tribe and the Uyghur people do not commit suicide, as they believe that those who commit suicide will turn into trees, which are easily cut down. Jiang Yunxiang, a magistrate from Qin, said so. ○ Water essence and blue mist On the mountains of Zhangzhou, if there is an upward rush of qi, it indicates that there is water essence below. In the southern part of Yunnan, when there is a loud thunder, blue mist is produced. These phenomena are due to changes over time and are not substances that have existed since the beginning of time. ○ The Land of Futi The people of Futi can travel through empty space, reaching thousands of miles in an instant wherever their thoughts go. A former inspector of Jiangxi once crossed the sea and saw these people. They had handsome appearances, could learn the local language wherever they went, could enter private spaces without being stopped, and never engaged in immoral or thieving behavior.

### Translation #### Knife Wound Medicine During the Five Changes in Gansu, soldiers were killed at Stone Peak Fort, and many died. Many children cut and joined the genitalia of both males and females into one piece and sold it for twelve cash coins. Those who applied the knife wound medicine competed to buy it, but after one night, it became rotten and smelly and could not be used. #### Mediums and Spirits Mediums and spirits vary in intelligence; some are intelligent, while others are foolish. Sometimes, they are asked to write on behalf of others. Zhao Yunsong, in the capital, asked his fellow townsman Wang Dianbang, a scholar, to summon a spirit. Wang Dianbang had a spirit he had long revered, which would come without the need for a talisman, and would write swiftly with meaning. Sometimes, Zhao Yunsong would engage in poetic exchange with the spirit, and just as he thought of a certain word, the spirit would already write it on the divining board, with each word appearing half a moment earlier than Zhao Yunsong. When Zhao Yunsong was in the south of Yunnan under the command of General A, who was known for his bravery, A's son Feng Shenghe could also summon spirits. One night, he invited Zhao Yunsong to watch together, but the divining board moved a lot and could not form words. Zhao Yunsong knew that the spirit was not of high caliber and played with it by passing words. When he thought of a word, the spirit would write it on the divining board; when he deliberately stopped thinking, the spirit could not form words. #### Pulling Out the Ghost's Tongue Jiang Jingwu's servant, A Zhen, was brave and liked drinking. He often followed his master to live in Xizhimen, a place known for having many ghosts, where people dared not live. A Zhen lived there. One night, a ghost with long hair came, and A Zhen was just drunk and did not fear it. The ghost extended its tongue about a meter to scare him. A Zhen stood up, grabbed it with his hand, and pulled out the ghost's tongue, which was cold and soft like cotton. The ghost shouted loudly and left, and A Zhen placed the tongue under the mat. The next morning, he looked at it, and it was just a straw rope. From then on, the ghosts disappeared.

○ Jiang Yingxi, who became a son-in-law in the Wang family of Huating, had his brother-in-law Ji Xun marry in Tongxiang. Shortly after the marriage, several pieces of silverware, including toothpicks, were lost in the household. The items were found at the residence of a servant involved in the wedding preparations, and Jiang Yingxi was about to report the matter to the authorities. That night, the servant and his wife committed suicide together. The servant’s husband was a monk who had abducted a woman (likely the servant’s wife), fearing severe punishment if discovered, they chose to take their own lives. A few days later, a young female servant in Jiang’s household inexplicably attempted suicide but was saved by emergency treatment. Jiang Yingxi went to her and scolded her, saying, “You have committed crimes of adultery, abduction, and theft. Instead of facing official punishment, you have avoided it by taking your own lives, which is a great relief. Why dare you torment an innocent young servant? If the servant does not survive, I will beat and burn your two corpses.” Afterward, the young servant remained well. ○ Fang Gongjian, the Palace Censor from Tongcheng, was in his previous life during the Jiajing era as a Taoist monk at Qingcheng Mountain. He witnessed Yang Shen (Elan) becoming the top scholar in the imperial examination, and his heart was moved. He was then reborn into the Pan family of Yixing. In his youth, as a top scholar, he had a relationship with a Buddhist nun, but they parted ways abruptly. The nun died from grief and depression. Soon after, the nun was reborn as a nobleman, and the Pan family member was reborn as a woman who married the nobleman but became widowed early and remained chaste for over seventy years, thus repaying the karmic debt. Fang Gongjian underwent three reincarnations to become a Palace Censor. He was born with great beauty, with pierced ears, and was given the nickname “Jia Ge” (Big Sister). His father, Fang Gongqian, served as a minister in the Ming dynasty and named his sons with names that began with a literary character and ended with a martial one: Gao Mao, Zhang Yue, and Fang Hengxian, all following this principle. Someone joked with him, saying, “Why not name you ‘Yu Xi Ai Zai,’ which also follows the literary-martial pattern?”

In the fourth year of the Qianlong era, a qilin was born from a cow in the folk of Wuhu. It died after three days. When its belly was opened, there were no intestines, and the interior was solid like a crab. People found this strange. Later, someone said that this event was recorded in the *Nan Xun Sheng Dian*. In Jianzhou, Sichuan Province, there were cats with four ears. Someone from Jianzhou personally reported this to me. The *Nan Shi* (Southern History) records that the king of Pijian had a head that was three feet long and was immortal. Later, reading *Xi Xizhi's Western Regions Record*, it was said that the king of Pijian was born in the second year of Emperor Zhang of Han. In our dynasty, he is called Dongkalisi Hultoto. Emperor Kangxi once sent an envoy to see him. The king had a head shaped like a bucket and a neck like a goose, both about a foot long. He had a strange gaze and incomprehensible speech. His descendants lived and died normally, but he was immortal. The matter is recorded in *Kangxi Tianwen Da Cheng*, and Zhao Yiji provided additional information.

A Strange Bird Phenomenon Involving a Woman from Songjiang and Her Family The aunt of Wang Zhangke, a scholar from Songjiang, and the second daughter of Lin Yinglan, a successful candidate in the imperial examinations, was just reaching the age of marriage when she married into the Li family. One morning, as she was putting on her makeup, a colorful bird flew around the window and perched on the mirror stand, raising its claws to beckon her. The woman became entranced, began chirping like a bird, and people could no longer distinguish her from a bird. Her body became as light as a sparrow, and she could move swiftly between beams and tiles. The bird on the mirror stand had already gone. The family was troubled by this and could not dispel or resolve the situation. They heard that there was a Taoist master in Qionglong Mountain, Suzhou, who could perform magic, so they invited him to come and seek his help. The Taoist master said, "This is a bird-like spirit. I can dispel and treat it. But you need three feet of white cloth to wrap the mirror where the bird stood, and use a fierce fire to burn it. If the mirror turns red but the cloth remains unburned, then it can be treated." Following his instructions, the cloth indeed remained unburned. The Taoist master muttered incantations and after a long time said, "The evil spirit has been captured." He took a clay jar, sealed it, and inscribed characters on it. He instructed the family members, "Do not open it to look inside, and quickly throw it into the river." The woman woke up as if from a dream, spoke normally, and when asked about the reason, she had no memory of what happened. However, someone at home secretly opened the jar to peek inside, and the woman became confused again. Her handmade shoes all had the shape of bird claws.

Again, the Taoist was requested, and he said, "If you do not heed my words, indeed, complications will arise. Fortunately, the lady has good fortune, and this monster has not escaped far. Try it again as before, and a single peony flower must appear on the cloth after burning; then my method will become effective." As he instructed, indeed, a peony appeared on the cloth as if painted. The Taoist again took a porcelain bottle, sealed it, and inscribed a spell, personally throwing it into the river. The woman's illness then healed. Now she has given birth to a child and lives peacefully, with no other illnesses. ○ During the late Ming period, the son of Mr. Zhang from Huanggang, Huguang, was gravely ill and possessed by a ghost. Once one ghost gathered, all ghosts arrived, demanding food and paper money, and they gathered at the door. Just as Mr. Liu Kewei pushed the door open, the ghosts were startled and said, "The ZHUANGYUAN has arrived! We should retreat." One old ghost left, turned back, and said, "A ZHUANGYUAN without a hat to wear, what have I to fear?" The patient suddenly recovered, and everyone did not understand. Later, Liu became the ZHUANGYUAN of this dynasty, and only then could the old ghost's mockery be understood.

### Final Translation **Black Peony** In Huian County, Fujian Province, there is a temple dedicated to the Great King of Green Mountain. Below the temple's steps, all the plants grown are black peonies. When they bloom, hundreds of them open, each flower facing the statue of the Great King. If the statue is moved, the flowers also turn to face it. **Mr. Li's Method of Capturing Fugitives** Mr. Li, a scholar from Fujian, had long experience in the imperial examinations but was very poor and did not rely on teaching or farming. He made a living by capturing fugitives, and his method was remarkably effective. Once, a man named Wang came to him because he had been robbed. Mr. Li recited a spell and placed a mirror on the water, instructing Wang to observe the trail. He told Wang to go to the east gate at a certain time and capture a white-haired, limping person, and the stolen goods would surely be found. Wang thought a limping person could not have stolen anything, and a white-haired person must be old and incapable of being a thief, so he decided to try it. As Mr. Li had said, they caught the thief, and the stolen goods were recovered. The thief was an experienced criminal, around twenty years old, who was worried that the patrol officers would recognize him, so he stole a fake beard from a clown in the theater to disguise himself as an old man. The day before, he went up the mountain and met with rain, injuring his foot, which caused his limp. **Shi Shu Rong** Shi Shu Rong, a student at the Imperial Academy, was known to the Grand Secretary Sun Wending of Fushan. Sun recommended him to be the magistrate of Jianwei in Sichuan, where he served as the acting magistrate of Jiading Prefecture. He was skilled in divination and fortune-telling and was compared to Guan Gongming and Guo Jingchun of his time. One day, while in the Jiading office, he divined his own fate and was shocked by the result: "I will not reach forty, and only at the age of seventy-two will I serve as a prefect!" However, he died shortly after passing forty, and this prediction was entirely inaccurate. Yet, Jiading did indeed change to a prefecture, and this occurred when he would have been seventy-two years old.

### Translation **The Monk Who Swallowed Eggs** A Zen master, traveling for spiritual cultivation, arrived at a village to beg for food. The villagers were all shallow and lacking in virtue, especially the unruly young people. They said to the master, "In our village, we offer wine and meat but not vegetables or bamboo shoots. If you truly go hungry for three days, we will prepare a vegetarian meal for you." After three days, they invited the master to the meal, but it was still a mix of meat and vegetables. They intended to make the master eat whatever he could find, just to laugh at his predicament. The master quickly swallowed several eggs and recited a verse: "The vast universe is contained in one mouth, without skin, blood, or fur. I will take you to the Western Paradise, sparing you from the knife of human slaughter." The villagers looked at each other in shock and then began to respect and support the master in the village. **The Judge of the Hall of Supreme Harmony** Hu Jimo, the magistrate of Zhongwei County in Gansu Province, was a native of Tongzhou, which was directly under the imperial court. He had passed the imperial examination as a孝廉 in the year 戊子 (Wu Zi). He once said that before he took office, he stayed in the capital. One night, he dreamed of a grand entourage, riding a mythical creature with silver antlers, traveling on the wind. He arrived at a place where the hall was very spacious, with the sign "Hall of Supreme Harmony" above it. A throne was set up to the side, and red candles burned on the table, with three plates of clay fruits. Many scribes stood below the steps, holding books and standing respectfully. Before he sat on the throne, he went to a side room and removed his clothes and shoes, replacing them with paper ones, which felt very cold. He stepped out, and the side door was immediately locked. If the door slightly opened at times, he could feel the wind blowing his clothes and shoes, with impurities entering. ---

The official duties to be handled consist only of checking the register and calling names. When calling out names, one might see an old acquaintance about to suffer, and a slight tendency to protect arises; or one might see a beautiful woman, and thoughts are not without movement, at which point a fire suddenly erupts on the dais, and the paper clothing on one's body is completely burned. Startled but calm, the fire extinguishes itself. But those being summoned, both men and women, all have a mass of yellow qi, said to be those undergoing a transformation in the Daoist realm. One day, a child of seven or eight years old was seen, and upon reviewing the register, it was known that their previous life was that of Shao Changgao of Renhe, who also passed the northern examination in the year of Wu Zi, dying shortly after the results were announced, thus this child has been reborn. In this manner, it has lasted for several years, with one being summoned every night, almost like a monk who has taken vows, and the heart is greatly distressed. At the time, there were no descendants, fortunately the father was a scribe for the Dragon King in Hangzhou, seeking exemption for his son in accordance with the example of those without descendants. The Dragon King pleaded on their behalf, and the exemption was granted. In the Hall of Supreme Harmony, there are many secret books from the Heavenly Treasury, unaware, writing them in a dream, as heavy as a thousand jin, only memorizing silently one volume of the *Commentary on the Heart Sutra* and one booklet of the *Lower Grade Precepts of the Yuanjun*, which are the four rules against killing, stealing, lust, and madness. The rules are very detailed, largely resembling the Buddhist monastic precepts. Alas, having served for several years, the master of the Hall of Supreme Harmony has never been seen, and their identity remains unknown.

Hangzhou's Tu Jianan was at the eastern office of Chen Wangzhi, where he personally witnessed the person speaking these words and recorded two books, including a set of rules that he brought back. This matter is very close to what was previously said by someone named Peng. ○ Foxes Carrying Flags, White Deer Holding Umbrellas Hu also said: The officials are all people from the world of the living, some of whom are scholars, others are candidates for office, and some have knowledge. As for the lower-ranking officials, they are often foxes or deer-like creatures. When they come to greet, the ceremony is formal and solemn, with foxes carrying flags and white deer holding umbrellas, some with golden horns and others with silver horns, indicating their rank. After being appointed as instructors and residing in the inner city, they no longer come. Men and women are not allowed to sleep together. If they do, their souls will be disturbed by living beings and cannot enter the city. The inner city is protected by celestial beings, and foxes and deer cannot enter. Later, due to leaking confidential information, they were dismissed from their positions and began to have children. ○ Tiger with Yellow Light Hu also said: A tiger that has yellow light and is undergoing reincarnation was observed by mountain gods and land deities, who reported to the supreme deity. It was known that the tiger was a Daoist who had fallen into a disaster at the 含元殿. Upon death, the soul was not summoned, so the person hanged themselves and was reborn into a tiger's womb. Soon after, the supreme deity decreed that if the tiger harms someone, the blame will fall on the master of the 含元殿 and the judge.

Four characters of "upright and dignified in court" appeared on the leg of Wu Na Sun, courtesy name Jian Shi, a student from Renhe, and the grandson of Mr. Ziyiting, who was a scholar-official during the Yongzheng era. He was staying at the home of Mr. Wang in the city. During the day, he woke up from a light nap and felt an itch on his left leg. When he looked at it, he saw a red, raised "正" character. After some time, four characters of "upright and dignified in court" appeared again, as large as a bowl, and could not be wiped away. The characters were neat and well-formed, with handwriting resembling Yu Shinan's *Temple Hall Stele*. Everyone who saw it found it strange, but they could not find the cause. One day before, Mr. Wu had moved his residence to Santai Mountain and passed by Zhang Tianguan's tomb, where the stone tablet bore the characters "upright and dignified in court." Some thought he had offended something, so he returned in a sedan chair to pray sincerely at the tomb. The place was not far from the Yu Zhongsu Temple, and he immediately prayed at the temple and asked for a divination sign. The deity showed the following sign: "A young man becomes successful and proud, and his achievements are so great that they overshadow his hat. This leads the villagers to bow down, and the Duke of Zhou is honored for a time." Someone nearby explained, "These are auspicious words, no need to say more." That autumn, the regular provincial examination was held, and it was determined to be a sign of protection and favor. The third line means that people from far and near all bow down and look closely. The fourth line subtly refers to the story of driving a needle through the thigh, and the title "Duke of Zhou" is also related to the surname Yu. When the provincial examination came, he did not pass. The four characters gradually faded away, and after that, there were no other strange occurrences. This happened on June 3rd, 1789.

I have examined in *Yong Zhuang Xiao Pin* that during the reign of Emperor Jiajing, a man from Hai Feng County in Shandong Province named Xu Er fell ill with typhoid fever, and suddenly, the characters "Wang Shandong" appeared on his arm. Changzhou's You Bao reported this to the authorities, and Xu Er was brought to the capital, examined, and released. A boy named Xianglin, nicknamed Doggie, lived in Weinan and was the son of a farmer. He was beautiful and charming in appearance, and sincere and affectionate in nature, but he was not favored by his parents. When there was famine in Guanzhong, he planned to seek food in other prefectures and entrusted Xianglin to his neighbor. The neighbor made him work the land, and if he was lazy, he would beat him. Unable to bear it, Xianglin took the opportunity to escape into Lantian Mountain, crossed the Qinling Mountains, and went west. During the day, he ate wild plants, and at night, he slept in caves, doing so for several months. It was the height of summer, and he went deeper into the mountains. One day, sitting on a high hill, he looked down into a cave, which was covered by forest vines. He entered the cave to take a nap. Soon, black smoke spewed in, and the fire burned his hair and fur with a sound. He quickly crawled out through the hole, and there was a giant python as big as a jar. Its head was not visible, its tail was outside the hole, and a toxic mist surrounded it, standing three zhang high. Xianglin was startled and fell to the ground, landing in a hole. After waking up, he looked at his body and head, which were as black as ink. He begged for food in the mountains, and the crowd shouted and pointed, calling him a ghost, and beat him with weapons and clubs. He thought he would surely die. Soon, he saw something in the overgrown vegetation that looked like a winnowing basket. Being very hungry, he cut it open and ate it, the liquid was as white as milk. A few days later, he felt itching and numbness in his body, so he bathed in a stream. Suddenly, his black skin shed like a cicada's shell, and his appearance became beautiful.

Xiang Lin was originally skilled in Qin music and, after leaving his mountain retreat, traveled from Hanzhong to Wuchang. There was another woman named Hu Da, whose art was highly refined. Xiang Lin asked her for guidance, hoping to use it to earn a living. She refused to teach him and began to mock him. Angered, he left and became a servant in the household of Jin Dan'er, a famous courtesan in Hanyang. Xiang Lin served her and observed her every frown and smile, every gesture, every meal, and every moment of waking and sleeping. Her clear and graceful appearance fully displayed all her charms. After living with her for a year, he happily said, "I have it now." He again asked to perform, and all the spectators were captivated, just like the story of Ma Ling performing *The Story of the Phoenix* as told in the *Zhuanghui Hall*. After several more months, one night while staying at an inn, suddenly a sharp blade flew through the window and struck his head. He quickly dodged out and saw it was Hu Da. Realizing he had provoked her jealousy, that place was no longer safe to stay in, and he returned to Weinan on the same day. When Xiang Lin first left, he was sixteen years old. After four more years, he returned, entering the house, not knowing where his parents were. Some said he had seen them in Shanxi, so he again left home and crossed the river, selling his skills from Pu Prefecture to Taiyuan to find them. One day, he performed at the residence of Shen Zhuping, an official observer. Among the audience and servants, there was an old man who resembled his father. When Xiang Lin was on stage, he caught a glimpse and could not help but cry out. Upon asking why, he asked someone to recognize the old man, and it turned out to be true. His mother was also at the residence, and upon hearing, she rushed out to embrace him. They looked at each other, overwhelmed with grief and unable to stand. Everyone in the room wept. The official observer was moved and gave him the best gift, ordering him to return with them.

**Returning to the Old Residence** He returned to his old residence and acquired fifty acres of land on the plain of the Jiu River, intending to serve his parents and spend the rest of his life doing so. **Phoenix Dung** In the spring of the year 1692 during the Kangxi era, people living near the coast of Qiongzhou suddenly saw black clouds covering the sky. The smell was extremely foul. An old man said, "This is the phoenix bird passing by. We should worry that its droppings might harm people, so we must quickly avoid it." The entire village fled. Soon after, the sky turned as dark as night, and it rained heavily. The next morning, when they returned, they found that the people's houses had all been crushed by the phoenix dung. When they dug out the dung, it all smelled like fish and shrimp. A single feather was left behind, which could cover several dozen houses, and one could ride a horse through its pores. The color of the feather was black, like that of a sea swallow. **Silver Companion** People know that tigers have companions, but they do not know that silver also has companions. Zhu Yuanfang lived in Fujian and found a treasure trove of gold and silver in a mountain valley. Suddenly, he smelled an unbearable stench, and his mouth and body began to twitch. An elder said, "This is a bandit who buried the gold and often kept a person prisoner, unable to die. He promised to let them go if they agreed to guard the treasure. The person agreed and was locked in the treasure trove. Anyone who finds the gold must first make an offering before they can take it." Zhu Yuanfang followed this advice and said, "You have been a bandit for too long. I will find this gold and release you." Afterward, the stench disappeared, and his illness was cured. Zhu Yuanfang became wealthy. A cousin named Zhou also found gold and silver but could not stay wealthy for long and returned the gold to the treasure trove. Tang wrote a poem about the silver companion, saying, "A dead enemy guards as a friend, how foolish you are, my companion! Try to speak to the person who dug the gold, how effective is this method?"

A Fly Treating a Disease A student named Yu had been ill for a long time. His family was extremely poor and could not afford medicine. On his table lay a book titled *Medical Convenience*. He checked and took the remedies suggested by the book, but none were effective. Suddenly, a fly flew in and landed on the book, making a loud noise. The student cried and prayed, saying, "Fly, you are a response, a spirit. If you have a spirit, I will open the book, choose a prescription, and trust in it. Perhaps it will treat my illness and make me recover?" He slowly turned several pages. The fly suddenly dropped down, landing on the page with the prescription for *Rhino Horn and Rehmannia Decoction*. He prepared the medicine according to the instructions and took several doses, eventually recovering. --- Mice Recommending a Scroll The magistrate of Fanchang, Mr. Huang, and I were together examining the provincial examination in Jiangnan during the year of Jiazi. Mr. Huang read a scroll with the surname Zhao, which did not meet his approval, so he placed it in the box of rejected papers. The next morning, when he reviewed the papers, the scroll was still on the table. Initially, he thought it had not been placed in the box and had forgotten it, so he put it back in the box. The following morning, the scroll was again on the table. Suspecting that someone in his household had cheated, he lit a candle at night, pretended to sleep, and watched. He saw three mice enter the box, carry a scroll together, and place it on the table. Mr. Huang suspected that this person had hidden virtue, so the examiner sent the mice to do it. He reluctantly recommended the scroll, and the candidate passed. When the list was published, the person was named Min. He came to see Mr. Huang and was told the reason. Mr. Huang asked, "What good deeds has your family done?" The person replied, "Our family is poor, we have no good deeds to do, but we have not allowed cats for three generations."

### Final Translation **Stone Figures Gambling** In front of the governor's office in Leizhou, twelve stone figures were erected, holding banners on both sides, which is where the current military office is located. One night, soldiers on night watch heard the sounds of people gambling and arguing. They rushed to investigate and found that it was the stone figures, with thousands of coins scattered on the ground. The next morning, they reported this to the county magistrate, who inspected the treasury. The locks and keys were as before, yet the amount of missing money matched the number of coins found. The county magistrate then moved the stone figures to the City God Temple and the Eastern Peak Temple, and the strange occurrences ceased. **Dog Chasing the Assistant Magistrate** In the year Jia Chen, there was a great famine, particularly severe in Pinghu. An assistant magistrate named Zhao descended to the county to collect taxes, enforcing strict and harsh punishments, causing great fear among the people. At the time, there were many beggars. Suddenly, a black dog stood upright and spoke in human language, saying to them: "Assistant Magistrate Zhao has taken 3,000 silver taels from the treasury to provide relief. Why don’t you go and ask him?" The beggars followed the dog to Zhao, and within moments, hundreds of people gathered. The ruffians then took advantage of the situation to cause a disturbance. Zhao was terrified and climbed over a wall to escape. ---

A Woman Born Through Her Mother's Side During the reign of Emperor Jiajing, a man named Shixiang, who was a lesser official in Xishan, had a son named Ping Zhen. Ping Zhen married a woman surnamed Wang, and she gave birth to a daughter who emerged from her left side. They named her Founü. Founü possessed an extraordinary level of intelligence and, by the age of five, behaved like an adult. By autumn, she gradually lost her appetite and her body began to shrink. One day, her mother's side opened again, and Founü jumped back in. Her mother died in pain, and she was cremated according to Buddhist rites. A small stupa was built on Red Stone Hill for her burial. Ping Zhen, grieving for his wife and daughter, died within two months. I have always heard that small animals, when startled, would return to their mother's womb, but I did not expect this to happen to humans as well. In the summer of June 3rd, Peng Zu died. On the day his coffin was carried, sixty people named She'er all froze to death. They were buried in the western hills, and their graves are still there today, known as "She'er Dun." Near the graves, there is a field of garlic chives that grows in spring without being planted and withers in autumn without being harvested. If someone dares to plow or dig near the graves, a sudden and intense storm will occur. A Drum Made from Human Skin At the Buddhist temple on Beigushan, there is a drum made from human skin. It was made from the skin of a pirate named Wang Gen, who was killed by General Tang during the Jiajing era. The sound of this drum is less resonant than other drums because human skin is thicker and more rigid than cowhide, making it less suitable for drumming.

### Translation #### Chapter One: The Dragon Dwelling on the Finger There was a man named Wang Xing from a village called Cui. On his left thumb, there was a red mark that was shaped in a winding curve, about an inch long and could be folded five or six times. Whenever it rained heavily, the mark would shake and not remain still. Wang Xing was disturbed by this and wanted to remove it. One night, he had a dream of a man with an extraordinary appearance who said to him, "I am a responsive dragon, exiled and residing in your body. Do not harm me. Three days from now, after midday, extend your finger outside the window frame, and I will leave then." On the appointed day, a heavy thunderstorm raged. Wang Xing did as instructed, and his finger split, and the responsive dragon arose. #### Chapter Five: The Method of Taking Over a Body Zhuang Yipu said that while traveling in Tibet, he rested beside a temple where a dead horse lay nearby, its stench unbearable. He wanted to move on but was too tired. Just as he was hesitating, an old monk and a young boy came and also rested at the temple. The young boy told the old monk, "Disciple, quickly send away the dead horse." The old monk immediately closed his eyes and remained silent. After a while, the dead horse suddenly moved, leaped up, and ran downwind for about two miles before falling back to the side of the road. The monk then opened his eyes and said to the young boy, "It has been sent away." This was the use of the method of taking over a body. However, the method has different applications for taking over living and dead bodies. Taking over a living body involves changing the soul while still carrying the spirit; taking over a dead body involves no spirit to possess, and after taking over, one still needs cultivation to nourish the spirit. Today, the red-robed lamas in Tibet all know this technique, which is mentioned in the *Lengyan Sutra* as the practice of ascetic heretics who throw ashes.

The phenomenon of a corpse following a person, known as *shi ben* (尸奔), is caused by the convergence of yin and yang energies. When a person dies, their yang energy is completely extinguished, leaving their body purely yin. If a living person with strong yang energy suddenly touches the corpse, the yin energy of the corpse suddenly opens and attracts the yang energy, causing the corpse to follow the person, as if it were bound and spinning. This is what the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) refers to as "when yin accumulates and meets yang, it inevitably leads to conflict." Therefore, those who accompany a corpse must avoid sleeping with their feet facing the deceased. When a person sleeps, their yang energy often flows out from the soles of their feet, particularly from the *yongquan* (涌泉) acupoint, which is powerful and unobstructed. If a living person sleeps with their feet facing the deceased, their yang energy will be completely directed into the corpse, causing it to stand up. This phenomenon is commonly referred to as "walking corpse" (*zou shi* 走尸), but it is actually due to the attraction of yang energy. The corpse cannot speak, but if it can speak, it is likely possessed by a spirit like "Huang Xiao'er" (黄小二) or an old demon. In the *Records of the Frontier States* (边州闻见录) by Chen Nieheng, it is recorded that a traveler was walking through the mountains when he heard someone calling his name and unconsciously responded. At dusk, he stayed at an inn and told the host about what had happened. The host said, "Do not worry, I can deal with it." That night, the host carried a sword and waited until the traveler fell asleep. At the third watch of the night, he indeed heard someone calling the traveler's name and saw an object resembling a person rushing into a tomb and disappearing.

The next day, they inquired about the neighbors and learned of a person who had recently died and been buried. Together, they reported to the authorities for an investigation. The corpse was found to be discolored and multicolored. The store owner said, "It is, but it has not yet become a spirit." They searched together in all directions and entered the deep mountains, where they saw a single skeleton, also multicolored and hairy. "This is Huang Xia'er," they said. When they burned it, it indeed made a chirping sound; but when they burned the newly buried corpse, there was no other difference. It is said that the soul of someone who died of starvation, after a long time, becomes a spirit. Especially, it borrows the body of someone who has recently died to harm people. Without borrowing, it becomes a calamity after a long time. If it encounters lightning and fire that disperse its energy, it can also spread and become an epidemic. These are all the evil qi of the mountains and rivers that accidentally affect after death. --- There are three types of skeletons in the earth: wandering corpses, buried corpses, and bones that do not decay. All of these lack outer coffins. Wandering corpses move with the lunar energy, shifting with the seasons and having no fixed location; buried corpses remain uncorrupted for a thousand years, always lying on the ground; bones that do not decay are the places where the spirit was concentrated during the person's lifetime. When these bones enter the ground, they turn black like jade, and after long exposure to the sun and moon's energy, they can also cause harm. Therefore, when someone who carries grain dies, their shoulder bones rot last; when a cart driver dies, their leg bones rot last. This is because they exerted themselves in life, causing the spirit to gather there, so they do not easily decay when buried. Buried corpses are the same; over time, they absorb spirit energy and become wandering corpses, and after a long time, they become flying night demons. The *Gou Lou Divine Book* states: "Old shellfish can ward off buried corpses."

Human qi separates dust in the world, and all things accumulate dust. Human qi can separate dust, so the eyes cannot see dust. Dust can cause decay, so palaces and rooms without people visiting decay easily. However, after a long time, buildings accumulate and receive human qi, interacting with the qi of the sun, moon, wind, and dew, and produce shadows in wood and stone, as recorded in the *Huan Wen Jia* and *Xia Ding Tu*. Doors, rooms, marketplaces, pools, marshes, and tools can all become spirits, with names that can be called. After a hundred years, there is a shadow; after a thousand years, the accumulated shadows form shapes. If the house is inhabited daily, the spirit qi cannot escape outward, constantly pressured by pure yang qi, and only remains in a latent form inside, forming an image of inner gold and water. Once sealed, after several decades, it receives human yang qi, and yin qi increasingly pressures, causing the inner yang qi to all reach the outside, then it produces sounds and forms and emerges, forming an image of outer fire and sun. It has no substance but forms shape through qi, so it can transform everything; this is an internal evil, not those that take advantage of emptiness from outside. Illuminate with burning alcohol, and the true form appears immediately; even the smell of sulfur causes them to retreat.

### Final Translation **The Power of Ghostly Qi** Sui Ji said: All spirits and ghosts use their qi to capture people and objects, and they can make small things appear large. When I was young, I was studying at the home of a Mr. Han in Xicheng, and I personally witnessed an old servant of his being tormented by a wronged ghost. One night, he suddenly disappeared, and all four corners of the doors and windows were locked. He was already dead in a mulberry garden two miles away, with blue hand marks on his neck. No one knows how he managed to leave the house. During the year Bao Yi, while I was staying in Changshan, I saw someone being captured by a demon and taken into a stone cave. The cave was not very large, just enough to fit the person, and the entrance was as small as a cup. When called, he responded, but could not get out. When the stone was broken open, the person was already dead. Also, in the house of my relative Tang, a fox spirit was causing trouble. One day, his wife couldn’t find her mirror. Later, when she took a flower from the vase, she felt the vase was twice as heavy as before. When she looked, the missing mirror was clearly inside the vase. The vase had a small opening but a large belly, and no one knows how the mirror got in. All of these are due to the power of qi. The *Book of Han*, in the "Biographies of Daoist Masters," describes a technique called "jin jia" (禁架), which is the same method.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. --- From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. --- As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. --- ### Final Translation **○ Mountain Demons Afraid of Mulberry Wood Knives** Chang Shan Quzi, a scholar from Ziting, had a study house in the mountains outside the eastern gate of the city. Sometimes, mountain demons would appear there. The locals were accustomed to seeing them and did not find them strange, calling them "one-legged ghosts." These demons walked with their heels facing forward, and their appearance was always accompanied by clouds and winds. The most feared by these demons was a knife made from old mulberry wood. If a knife made from old mulberry wood was used to strike them, they would die. Hanging such a knife on the door would also cause them to avoid it. Mountain demons loved to listen to songs. There was a man named Zhang who stayed in a mountain in Qizhou. Every night, the mountain demons would come and persistently bother him to sing. **○ Charm to Drive Away Malaria Demons** According to Daoist texts, malaria demons are divided according to the ten Heavenly Stems and twelve Earthly Branches, each with a name. If someone falls ill on a particular day, the demon's name can be found, and it can be driven away with a talisman. Those demons that do not follow the daily cycle are particularly fierce and destructive, known as Yue Zigui. They must be captured using the demon of the day, which is known as "using a thief to catch another thief." However, even when this method is used, it is sometimes ineffective. The charm "Chase Away Evil Malaria Demons" recorded in *The Taiping Guangji* is more effective. It says: "Buer Buer, the spirits of the four mountains, come and bind me. The Six Ding Messengers, the Five Way Generals, collect your essence and capture your spirit. Go quickly, go quickly, avoid meeting this person." Whenever a person is ill, if the charm is recited in the morning without interruption, the chills and fever will dissipate, and the person will sweat and recover. Mr. Zhang Yucun, who was engaged in salt trading in Taizhou, personally tested this charm and found it effective. It has been passed down, and no one has failed to see results.

**Translation:** The sunken wood, known as "孤阴沉木" (gū yīn chén mù), is a product of the mountains in Shinan Prefecture, Huguang. It is all dug up from the ground and is called "孤阴沉木," which can be interpreted as "lonely sunken wood." The wood is fragrant, light, and soft. If you pinch it with your fingernail, it will leave a dent, which will soon close again, much like *chī nán* (a type of agarwood). The locals say that if the wood is used as a coffin, it will become heavier over time and eventually sink into the ground. After a thousand years, the coffin will sink into the ground dozens of feet and become as hard and heavy as iron, hence it is highly valued. In Shinan, the wood can be bought for no more than 60-70 gold pieces, and one can get a good piece. Transporting it to Hankou is expensive, making it difficult to purchase unless one has a thousand gold pieces. Before the time of Pangu, there is no record of its origin, but it is said to be from the era of chaos, possibly referring to the mythical period of Longhan. Laozi, the founder of Daoism, is believed to have been born in the first year of the Longhan era and is associated with Daoist texts.

### Translation **○织登科记** Once, someone mistakenly entered a celestial pool and saw a woman weaving silk. The silk was inscribed with many ancient characters, which the person did not recognize. Upon asking, she replied, "This is the record of this year's successful candidates, to be presented to the Heavenly Emperor." The record of successful candidates must be woven, and the writings of those who pass must be cast. Heaven values the imperial examinations so highly that the *Thousand Buddhas' Names Sutra* is not an empty statement! If Yang Qiongfang gained the top rank due to a fire at the examination hall, how different is this from the story of Jiao Zong, the top-ranked scholar of the Ming dynasty? At the time, people said, "Without the fire at the southern examination hall, how could Jiao Zong become the top-ranked scholar!" **○朱鹿田** Mr. Zhu Lutian, while serving as a Criminal Department official, accompanied Grand Secretary Ma Gong on a mission to Henan to investigate certain matters. They stayed overnight at a public residence, where there were three bedrooms, with Zhu and Ma sharing adjacent rooms. It was July 16th, and the moon was exceptionally bright. Zhu was suffering from heat and could not sleep. At the third watch, a sudden wind blew, and the doors and windows opened by themselves. He saw a white vapor like a rainbow, winding its way inside, approaching his bed. Zhu struck it with a fan, and the vapor immediately retreated. Following closely behind, he saw the vapor enter Ma's room. Soon after, it emerged, and a red light chased and intertwined with the vapor. The white vapor could not withstand it, becoming increasingly faint, and eventually left the room. The red light also returned, ceasing its pursuit, and the doors and windows closed again. Listening to Ma, he was snoring loudly, as if he had not noticed anything. The next day, the attendants reported that two of their servants had died, both of whom were paralyzed and weak, with unknown causes of illness.

### 飞僵 All zombies, after a long time, can fly and no longer remain in their coffins. Their entire bodies are covered with hair over a foot long, long and hanging down loosely, and they emit light when moving. After even more time, they become flying night demons, which cannot be killed by anything other than a lightning strike, but can only be killed with a bird gun. The mountain people of Fujian often encounter this and call hunters to shoot it from the tops of trees. This creature is as strong as a bear and attacks people and damages crops at night. ### 程嘉荫 Zhao Yiji said: I was a classmate of Cheng Jiayin when I was young. Cheng Jiayin had a knack for clever ideas and was interested in Taoism. He got a copy of the *Qiqi Lu* (Book of Strange Devices) from a Taoist priest named Fan, which allowed him to create a mechanical ox. I personally saw its construction. The outer appearance is easy to understand, but the internal mechanisms are different. The throat and tongue are connected to horizontal and vertical wooden pieces, one attached to the root of the tongue and one attached to the heart, which is made of lead. The wood has holes around the edges, all connected by ropes that pass through the feet. When the heart moves, the lead weight causes the wood to tilt, and when the weight decreases, the tongue root tilts again, lifting the lead heart upward. In this way, the feet move up and down, allowing the mechanical ox to walk, but it is very slow and cannot run. If heavy objects are placed on its back, it moves even more slowly. Cheng Jiayin said that there is a more advanced version called the *Jiu Feng Lun Mu Jia*, which has five internal mechanisms corresponding to the five organs and four external mechanisms corresponding to the limbs. This version can move as fast as a flying object and can carry hundreds of pounds.
