When the tongue is turned, the lead mechanism rests on the waist, and a rope is pulled to make the feet curl up, similar to the mechanical ox described in the *Wu Hou* (Zhuge Liang) legend and various *Renshu* (Divination) books, but it is different from the Western mechanical ox.

"Also, they could make a speaking tube. The tube had a small gap (about an inch) separated by a gate, with a mechanism inside to control airflow. After someone spoke into the tube, the gate was closed. The gates had to be opened in a specific order; if they were opened in the wrong order, the message would not make sense. According to Cheng, this method could store information for up to 100 days, but after 100 days, the mechanism's effectiveness would diminish. Unfortunately, he died young. His parents believed that his excessive effort led to internal bleeding and death. Therefore, all the books he had obtained were burned to prevent them from causing harm to his younger brother. There is also the mythical creature known as 'water tiger.' According to the *Erya* (爾雅), a tiger with horns is called '' (a rare or mythical variant) and can move in water. However, it is not known that there are actually tigers that can swim. During the Kangxi era, Mr. Zhu Lutian saw a tiger being kept in a pool by the governor of Songjiang. The tiger was fed fish and shrimp and did not eat raw meat. When the tiger was dissected, three small tigers were found in its stomach. This was likely a shark that had transformed into a tiger, and it was caught in a net before it could reach land."

○ Green Spirit and Red Spirit *The Broad Language*: In Guangzhou, when girls reach the age of fifteen, many are afflicted by the Green Spirit and die; when boys have not yet married, many are afflicted by the Red Spirit and die. The saying goes, "Girls fear the Green Spirit, boys fear the Red Spirit." The Red Spirit is also called "Over Heaven," and the Green Spirit is also called "Prince Consort." Those who are afflicted must perform rituals to drive them away. If a boy is afflicted by the Green Spirit or a girl by the Red Spirit, the illness is incurable, as they are also evil spirits, similar to the cat spirits of Jinhua. ○ Scholar's Night Encounter with a Light *Mr. Yan, the Night Light*: I heard that an old scholar was walking at night and suddenly encountered his deceased friend. The scholar was always upright and did not fear the encounter. He asked, "Where are you going?" The friend replied, "I am a bureaucrat of the underworld, going to the South Village to perform a task." It happened to be the same path, so they walked together. They arrived at a dilapidated house, and the friend said, "This is the home of a scholar, and we cannot go in." The scholar asked, "How do you know?" The friend replied, "Ordinary people are busy with daily affairs during the day, and their spiritual essence is obscured. Only at night, when there is no thought, is the soul clear. The books read in the heart emit light from every pore. This light is ethereal and colorful, like brocade. Those who are as learned as Zeng, Confucius, and Wen, or as talented as Qu Yuan, Song Yu, Ban Gu, and Sima Qian, their light reaches the sky, competing with the stars and the moon. Those slightly less learned have a light several meters high, and those slightly less still have a light several feet high, gradually decreasing. Even the least learned have a light like a lamp, illuminating the windows. People cannot see this light, but only spirits and ghosts can. This room has a light seven or eight feet high, so I know it is the home of a scholar."

A scholar asked, "How much brilliance does my sleep hold after a lifetime of reading?" The ghost hesitated for a long time before saying, "Yesterday, I passed by your school. You were just napping during the day. I saw that in your chest, there was a complete set of high-quality lecture notes, several hundred exam papers, seventy to eighty essays, and thirty to forty strategic plans. Every word turned into black smoke, covering the roof. The sound of students reciting was as if in thick clouds and dense fog. In reality, no brightness was seen, and I dare not speak falsely." The scholar shouted angrily at it, and the ghost laughed loudly and left. ○ Fox Spirit's Righteous Advice Xian County Magistrate Ming Sheng, a person from Ying Mountain, once wanted to clear an unjust case but was concerned that his superiors would not approve, leaving him in doubt. There was a gatekeeper named Wang Banxian who had a friend, a fox spirit, who often predicted minor fortunes and misfortunes with accuracy. Ming Sheng sent Wang Banxian to ask the fox spirit. The fox spirit spoke seriously, "Honorable sir, as a parent to the people, you should only consider whether the case is unjust, not whether it will be approved. Do you not remember what Governor Li said?" The gatekeeper returned with the message, and Ming Sheng was deeply moved.

During the time when Governor Li Gong Wei was not yet prominent, he once accompanied a Taoist priest across the river. Just then, there was someone quarreling with the boatman, and the Taoist priest sighed and said, "Life is fleeting; still haggling over a few coins?" Soon after, that person was swept by the sail, fell into the river, and died. Li Gong was surprised by this. In midstream, the wind arose, and the boat was about to capsize. The Taoist priest performed ritual steps and recited spells, and the wind stopped, allowing them to be saved. Li Gong prostrated himself twice, thanked the Taoist priest for saving him, and the Taoist priest said, "That person who fell into the river, it was fate, and I could not save him; you are a noble person, faced with danger and saved, it is also fate, and I could not help but save you. Why thank me?" Li Gong bowed again and said, "I accept your teaching; I will accept my fate for the rest of my life." The Taoist priest said, "This is not entirely true. One's personal success or failure should be accepted; not accepting one's fate leads to rushing and competing, to any lengths. Even if Li Linfu and Qin Hui did not betray good people, they still became prime ministers, only adding to their own crimes. Concerning the welfare of the state and its people, one cannot say it is fate. The creation of talent by heaven and earth, the establishment of officials by the court, is to rectify the course of destiny. Holding power and authority, to resign oneself to fate, why would heaven and earth create such talent, why would the court set up such an official? The gatekeeper said, 'Knowing it is impossible but still doing it,' Zhuge Liang said, 'To serve with utmost dedication until death,' this is the way of the sages in establishing one's destiny, you should understand this." Li Gong respectfully accepted the teaching, bowed, and asked for the name of the Taoist priest. The Taoist priest said, "If I told you, you would be frightened." They got off the boat, walked several steps, and then disappeared.

Foreign curiosities and wonders are the most frequently reported. In Goryeo, there is a method of transportation using dogs to pull carts. The dead of a certain people retain consciousness and can be reborn after a century when buried in the ground. In the country of Tuhar, days are unusually long and nights short, with the sun setting and rising quickly. In the country of Shabi, the sound of the sun setting is like thunder, and the people must make loud noises with bells and drums to prevent children from being frightened, otherwise, the children will die of fright. In the country of Da'er, the inhabitants have exceptionally large ears, seven feet long and four feet wide, and they use one ear as a mattress and the other as a blanket when lying down. The people of Ningtai hibernate during winter like snakes and insects, not drinking, eating, speaking, or moving, and in spring, they begin to move slowly and resume normal activities. The people of a certain country hibernate once every hundred years. In Leizhou, the locals eat cooked meat and can transform it into raw meat through incantations, then transform it back into pigs and sheep, and finally return it to its original cooked state. The incantation used is "East Mountain Queen Mother's peach, West Mountain Queen Mother's peach," only ten characters long, but it is very mysterious. In the great country of Daqin, which is four thousand miles from Chang'an, sheep are born underground with their umbilical cords connected to the ground. If the umbilical cord is cut, the sheep will die, but if drums are struck to shake the ground, the umbilical cord will break, and the sheep will follow the water and grass. This account is recorded in the *New Book of Tang*, and recently, there have been sheep hides that support the ancient accounts, showing that the ancients were not lying.

Zhang Hao made a posture for crossing water while visiting the Bamboo Forest Temple in Zhenzhou. The temple is separated from a small river by about seven feet, and the monks use a wooden bridge to cross it. When Zhang Hao arrived, it was evening, and the bridge had already been removed. Zhang Hao leaped into the water and crossed it. When he reached the monk's hermitage, only half of his shoe was wet. The monk was greatly surprised and thought Zhang Hao was a deity. Zhang Hao laughed and said, "I am not a deity. In my youth, I had a teacher who taught me a method. I used thick bricks, each more than a foot high, placed horizontally on the ground, and laid them out about ten feet long. I leaped onto and ran across them, and the bricks did not tip over. I then switched to thinner bricks and tried again, moving back and forth without the bricks shaking. Next, I used old, rotten cloth, which could withstand my weight without tearing. I then switched to tofu, and finally, to fine paper and bamboo paper. If I could step on bamboo paper without breaking it, I could cross water. But the starting point must be at least twenty steps away, and I would take a deep breath and dive in, immediately assuming a tiger-like stance and leaping into the air like a flying tiger. The front of my shoe touches the water, but not more than five or six inches, and I would immediately get back on land. If I started to breathe deeply only when I reached the water's edge, I wouldn't be able to get into the right posture, but even at its maximum, it can only reach about seven feet."

During my examination of Wang Mang's military campaigns, he issued a call for those who could fly. Someone responded to the summons, tying bird feathers to their arms to create wings, and flew dozens of steps before falling. Wang Mang knew that such a feat was impossible. This is precisely the kind of impractical request he made. ○ During his tenure as a provincial governor, Tang Gong Zhiyu once investigated a murder case, and the evidence was conclusive. One night, while holding a candle and sitting alone, he suddenly heard faint weeping, which seemed to be approaching the window. He ordered a young servant girl to go and look, but she suddenly fainted. The governor opened the curtain and saw a ghost, covered in blood, kneeling on the steps. The ghost shouted at him angrily. The ghost bowed its head and said, "The one who killed me is someone else, but the county official has mistakenly convicted that person. My revenge has not been avenged, and I cannot rest in peace." The governor replied, "I understand." The ghost then disappeared. The next day, the governor personally questioned the witnesses, and they confirmed that the dead man's clothing and shoes matched what they had seen. The conviction became even more solid, and the case was ultimately resolved as the ghost had said, with the original suspect being acquitted. The interrogating official argued vigorously, but the governor remained steadfast, believing that "even if the mountains could be moved, this case would not be overturned." His advisor suspected that there might be other reasons, and after questioning the governor, he learned the full story, but there was nothing more that could be done. One evening, the advisor asked, "Where did the ghost come from?" The governor replied, "It appeared right at the steps." "Where did the ghost go?" "It suddenly leaped over the wall." The advisor said, "All ghosts have form but no substance; they should disappear suddenly, not leap over walls." He then examined the area where the ghost had leaped over the wall. Although the tiles and bricks were not broken, after the recent rain, there were faint traces of mud on the roofs of several buildings, leading down to the outer wall. He pointed this out to the governor and said, "This must have been done by the prisoner bribing a thief." The governor thought deeply and realized the truth, but he still upheld the original verdict, concealed the matter, and did not pursue it further.

Gu Liu, a woman from a farming family in Huai Town, did not know her husband's surname. During the years 1724 and 1725, a great famine struck, and her husband, believing he could not survive, left to beg for food in all directions. As he was about to depart, he knelt and touched his forehead to the ground before her, saying, "My parents are old and ill, and I am burdening you with my hair." Gu Liu was known for her beauty. Village youths noticed her lack of food and tried to seduce her with money, but she refused them all. She supported her in-laws with women's work. Eventually, she realized she could not sustain them and gathered the neighbors, bowing before them and saying, "My husband entrusted me with his parents, but now I am at my wit's end. If we do not come up with a plan, we will all die. If you can help me, help me; if not, I will sell flowers, so do not laugh at me." In the village, women who stood by the door were called "selling flowers." The neighbors hesitated and all left. She then wept bitterly and informed her in-laws, openly associating with various rakes. She secretly saved money for her sexual services and took in another woman, keeping them strictly guarded so that no outsiders could see their faces. Some said she was hoping to charge a high price, but she did not argue. After more than three years, her husband returned. After the formal greetings were over, she introduced him to her in-laws, saying, "My parents are still here, and now I am returning them to you." She then led the woman she had taken in to meet her husband, saying, "My body is tainted, and I cannot bear the shame of being your companion. Therefore, I have found you a wife, and I am giving her to you." Her husband was shocked and did not yet believe it. She then said, "I will prepare a meal for you." She went to the kitchen and committed suicide by cutting her throat.

The magistrate came to inspect, and she lay with her eyes wide open and not closing. The magistrate ordered her to be buried in the ancestral grave, without ing the grave, saying, "Not ing the grave, should be severed from her husband; burying in the ancestral grave, shows that she is not severed from her in-laws." Her eyes still did not close. Her in-laws wept and cried out, saying, "She was originally a chaste woman, because of us, she came to this state. My son, being a man, could not support us and left a young woman, the passersby know his heart! Whose fault is it to sever her? This is our family matter, officials need not interfere." After speaking, her eyes closed. There was also a girl from Mengcun. At the end of the Chongzhen era, a great bandit rampaged, seeing the girl was beautiful, he took her along with her parents. The girl did not submit to being defiled, so he bound her parents and subjected them to burning. The parents both cried out in deep distress, ordered the girl to follow the bandit. The girl asked to release her parents first before she would comply, the bandit knew that she would not comply, so he decided to defile her first and then release her. The girl then fiercely slapped the bandit's face and died with her parents, her body was left in the wild. Later, the bandit fought the soldiers, and the horse, upon reaching the body, hesitated and refused to proceed, eventually falling into the mud and being captured. These two events are opposite, and those who discuss them all have criticisms, one is criticized for losing her virtue, and the other for being too harsh. I say: both are right. Confucius said: "There were three righteous men in the state of Yin." Guo Liu changed her behavior, like Ji Zi becoming a slave; the girl from Mengcun upheld her virtue, like Bi Gan who died after remonstrating. Ancient people still pitied Xu Xiaoke's wife and Princess Yuechang, how much more so these two women?

### Translation #### The Foolish Ghost of Liu Yuchong Liu Yuchong was a man from Cangzhou. He was solitary and introverted, fond of discussing ancient systems, which were impractical and overly idealistic. Once, he commissioned Dong Tianshi to paint "Reading in Autumn Woods," and Ji Houzhai inscribed it: "Sitting alone at the base of an autumn tree, isolated and without companions. I do not know what book he reads, but his appearance is ancient. I only worry that the book he holds might be a treatise on the well-field system." This was meant to advise him. Once, he came across an ancient military treatise and studied it for years, believing he could command ten thousand soldiers. When bandits appeared, he trained local militia to fight them, but suffered a significant defeat. Later, he found an ancient book on water management and studied it for years, believing he could turn a thousand miles of land into fertile fields. He drew maps and presented his ideas to the local officials, who tested his plans in a village. The irrigation channels were just completed when a great flood came, flowing into the channels and nearly causing disaster. From then on, he became depressed and dissatisfied. He would often walk alone in the courtyard, shaking his head and muttering to himself, "Did the ancients deceive me?" He repeated these six words day after day, thousands of times. Soon after, he fell ill and died. Afterward, on clear, moonlit nights, his ghost was often seen walking alone and shaking his head at the base of the pine trees in front of his grave. If one listened closely, he would still be reciting these same six words. #### The Foolish Ghost's Love for His Wife In the capital, there was an elderly woman who saw ghosts. She often told others: "Last night, I saw a ghost at someone's house. He was incredibly foolish, but his situation was pitiful, and it moved my heart deeply."

A Ghost with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Final Translation A Ghost with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. A ghost named [Unknown], residing in a certain village, came from a moderately well-off family and died at the age of around 27 or 28. One hundred days after his death, his wife invited me to accompany her. I saw that he always sat under a lilac tree in the courtyard. Sometimes he would hear the sound of his wife crying, sometimes the sound of his child crying, and sometimes the quarreling between his wife and her siblings-in-law. Despite the intense heat, he could not get close, but he always listened at the window, and his sad expression was clearly visible. Later, he saw the matchmaker come to his wife's room and was startled, jumping up and looking around. After hearing that the marriage negotiations had failed, he showed a slight smile. Soon after, the matchmaker returned and went back and forth between the wife and her siblings-in-law. The ghost followed hurriedly, looking as if something had been lost. On the day of the dowry presentation, the ghost sat under the tree, staring directly at the wife's room. His tears fell like rain. From then on, whenever the wife went in or out, the ghost followed behind, showing even deeper attachment. On the day before the wedding, the wife prepared her dowry. The ghost wandered outside the eaves, sometimes leaning on a pillar and crying, sometimes bowing her head as if thinking. When she heard coughing from inside the room, she peeked through a crack, staying awake all night. The old woman sighed and said, "Why is the foolish ghost so attached?" The ghost did not hear her. The groom entered, holding a torch and leading the way. The ghost stood aside in a corner, still looking up at the wife. The narrator and the wife left and looked back, seeing the ghost following far behind to the groom's house. The ghost was stopped by the door god, bowed and begged, and was allowed to enter. The ghost hid in a corner of the wall, watching the wife perform the ceremony, standing fixed like someone in a trance. The wife entered the room, and the ghost approached the window to peek. Until the candles were extinguished and the wife went to bed, the ghost did not leave. The ghost was driven out by the central deity, leaving in a hurry.

Still at the wife's home, the wife left a child at home. Hearing the child cry for its mother, the old woman rushed out and circled around the child, rubbing her hands together in a helpless gesture. Soon, the sister-in-law came out and slapped the child, then stamped her feet and patted her chest, making a gesture of gnashing teeth from afar. The old woman could not bear to see it and went straight back home. Chapter: Fox Spirit Afraid of Wife. In the western city, there was a pawnshop at Jiyi Temple. A small building was occupied by a fox, and at night, one could always hear its voice, but it did not harm people. Over time, people became accustomed to it. One night, there was loud scolding and beating on the upper floor, and everyone went to listen to it. Suddenly, they heard someone crying out in pain, saying, "All the gentlemen downstairs should understand reason. Is there a world where a wife beats her husband?" Just now, one person was being beaten by his wife, and there were still scratches on his face that had not healed. Everyone laughed loudly, saying, "This is indeed a fact, it is not surprising." The group of foxes upstairs also laughed loudly, and their fight was resolved. Those who heard it all fell down laughing.

○ A military officer's wife, Ji Xiaolan, was in Wulumuqi. One day, it was reported that Military Officer Wang had been sent to transport military equipment to Ili, and his wife was left alone. By midday, the door did not open, and when called, she did not respond, suggesting something was amiss. The local magistrate, Mu Jintai, was ordered to investigate. Upon breaking down the door, they found two people, male and female, lying together on a pillow, naked and embracing, both with their bellies cut open and dead. The man was unknown, and no one recognized him. Neighbors were questioned, but no leads were found, and the case seemed to be a mystery. That night, the woman's body suddenly groaned, and those guarding her were startled to see her come back to life. The next day, she could speak and confessed: "I loved this man since childhood, and even after marriage, we continued to meet in secret. Later, when my husband was stationed in the western city, this man could not forget me and came searching for me. Just as he arrived at the door, he was led into the room, and none of the neighbors noticed. We feared that our brief reunion would end in separation, so we agreed to die together. When we were cut, the pain was so intense that I fainted; suddenly, I woke up as if from a dream. My soul had left my body. I quickly searched for this man, but he was nowhere to be found. I was alone in a sandy wasteland, with white grass and yellow clouds stretching endlessly in all directions. As I wandered, a ghost took me away to an official office, where I was severely interrogated and humiliated. I was told that my life was not yet over, so I was ordered to receive a hundred lashes and then sent back. The whip was made of iron, and the pain was unbearable; I fainted again. When I gradually regained consciousness, I had come back to life." Upon examining her thighs, the marks of the lashes were clearly visible. The higher-ranking official, Ba Gong, said, "She has already received punishment from the afterlife; therefore, her crime of adultery should not be severely punished."

In Mr. Flying Night Spirit's *Ürümqi Miscellaneous Poems*, it is said: "Mandarin ducks ultimately do not fly together, an old wish made in heaven or on earth has been broken. White grass rustles, burying the coffin of a traveler, a life filled with heartbreak in the Huashan area." Mr. Flying Night Spirit was in Ürümqi, and Captain Cai Liangdong recounted: "When this place was first pacified, I once went on a hunting expedition deep into the southern mountains. At dusk, I saw what appeared to be a shadow across a stream or ravine, and suspected it might be a thief. I hid in the bushes to investigate. I saw a person in military attire sitting on a large stone, with several soldiers standing nearby, all with fierce appearances. Their words were too distant to be understood, but I saw one soldier being ordered to bring out six women from a stone cave. The women were beautiful, fair-skinned, and dressed in colorful clothing, with their hands tied behind their backs. They trembled, bowed their heads, and knelt. One by one, they were brought to the person sitting on the stone, their lower garments were removed, and they were made to lie on the ground, where they were whipped until they bled. Their cries were pitiful and sorrowful, echoing through the forest and valleys. After the whipping, the six women trembled and knelt as they were sent away. They could no longer see the figure, and they cried softly as they returned to the cave."

In the area, one shot could reach, but the ravine was deep and the cliffs steep, with no path through. Thus, they had archers with strong bows shoot arrows together. On the opposite cliff, there was a tree with two arrows stuck in it, used as a marker. The next day, after taking a roundabout route for dozens of miles to find the location, they discovered the cave entrance covered with dust. They entered holding a torch, the cave twisting and turning, about four zhang deep, with no signs of human activity. They did not know what deity they had encountered the previous day or what was being beaten. Some said it was a flying demon transformed into a woman. In a new place called Xianan, Cheng Sheng named Dun had relatives living deep in the mountains, where their garden and pavilion were quite secluded and charming. Cheng Sheng went to visit them. By evening, the gate of the estate was locked, as there were tigers in the area. One night during the first watch, the moonlight was faint, and a sudden strong wind blew. A young servant wanted to ask for the key to leave, but his peers stopped him, and the master personally explained to him. The servant, having no choice, tried to climb over the wall to leave, but it was too high and steep. Suddenly, he heard the roar of a tiger outside the wall, and the master ordered the servants to hold the servant tightly. The servant went mad, hitting and calling out, losing consciousness. Cheng Sheng, knowing something was amiss, climbed the small tower to observe and saw a short-necked person outside the wall hitting it with bricks. Every time the bricks hit the wall, the servant would call out to leave, but he would stop when the bricks were not hitting. Cheng Sheng and the master both realized it must be a tiger's follower, so they held the servant even tighter. The servant called out for a long time, then suddenly changed into the sound of a pig, and his urine and feces both came down, and his excrement also became pig excrement. The people in the garden were greatly surprised. By the fifth watch, the servant fell asleep.

**Translation:** At dawn, the man and the host climbed the tower again to look out, and they saw a tiger leap away from the dense vegetation on the western side, while the *chāng* (a tiger spirit) was no longer visible. **○ Wolf Teeth** All ferocious beasts rely on their claws and teeth for their sharpness and fierceness, which allows them to grapple and devour. However, in ancient times, people only referred to wolf teeth, believing them to be sharp and harmful to things. A few years ago, a certain magistrate from Gānquán, returning to his office one day, saw a swift servant in the office room tied up a small animal like a dog, with light green eyes. Suspecting it to be a wolf, he inquired and found it to be true. He then led the wolf into the office. A guest from the magistrate's retinue prodded the wolf's mouth with a tobacco pipe, and the small wolf showed its jaw, making a biting motion. Upon careful observation, its teeth were bright white, varying in size and unevenly arranged, with its gums forming a single piece, unlike the separate and orderly arrangement of human and other animals' teeth. Thus, he suddenly realized why ancient people named weapons after wolf teeth, as they derived this from the wolf's unique anatomy. The wolf's ferocity and cruelty relied on these teeth, which were also uniquely bestowed by heaven, much like the human rib cage and the continuous arm structure of apes. **○ Strange Tower in Xi'an** In the four-fork street of Xi'an city, there was the residence of Governor Wang, who served in Zhejiang province. The residence had been locked for a long time, with a servant left to guard it. One day, a neighbor saw dozens of lamps hanging from the back tower when looking from afar, and hurriedly went to ask the servant. The servant opened the door and looked inside, but found nothing. There were also several children who went to play during the day and arrived at the back tower, where they saw an elderly man with a white beard leaning on the window of the tower, looking down. The children shouted at him, and the elderly man suddenly stuck out his tongue, which was over ten feet long and reached the ground. They were greatly frightened and scattered.

In a certain year during the Qianlong reign, the magistrate was involved in some matter, and this house became state property. His colleague from Gan Prefecture, High Official Cao, bought it. Subordinate to him, County Magistrate Huang Jingyue of Wugong went to the provincial capital to stay for the night. In the summer month, he was lying in the daytime in the front hall. In the evening, he suddenly woke up, and the northern window opened by itself. An object with a black face and red eyes came to peek. Huang shouted and got up, leading his servants to chase it, but they couldn't see it. High Official Cao went to the provincial capital and placed the file boxes from his previous post in Chang'an on the back floor. One day, when checking old cases, he ordered the servants to go upstairs and open the boxes, and they saw a huge snake coiled beside the boxes, which greatly frightened them, and they ran to report to High Official Cao. He personally went to see it, but there was nothing there. High Official Cao therefore dared not stay there. Suddenly one night, the back floor caught fire, and the officials tried to save it, but only the back floor was burned down. There was a pile of white bones in the courtyard. County Magistrate Zhou Xiaoting of Chang'an examined it and found several large teeth, each about five inches long. There was nothing else unusual. Governor Qin and Inspector Shu both took one or two of them away. People all said that this monster had burned itself to death. High Official Cao was promoted to Magistrate of Ningwu and finally moved in. Now this house has been sold to the former magistrate of , Yang Yiting, and there has been no other strange occurrence. ○ Two Strange Events in Wujin In the northern part of Wujin, a village known as Youcun, a certain family gave birth to a boy who grew rapidly. At just eleven months old, he was already a foot tall. Whenever he ate, he would finish three large bowls of rice, and if given glutinous rice cakes, he could eat all seven. However, he could not speak and still slept in a basket, requiring someone to carry him. This was during the fiftieth year of the Qianlong reign.

In the northern part of Pi Ling Prefecture, a woman surnamed Qin suddenly gave birth to a boy with a fierce and ugly appearance, a head with two horns, and each horn had two eyes. His entire body was green, covered in large, lumpy masses of flesh that were several inches long and thin like matchsticks. His cry was also very unusual. The family considered it an omen and buried him beside an abandoned garden. The next day, when people passed by, they still heard a bleating sound coming from underground. This was an event that occurred in the fifth year of the fifth month of the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang. ○ Zhou Jiaxuan, a historian from Xijiang, was delivering a lecture at the Temple of Confucius. He had recently been selected as a孝廉 and was on his way to the northern imperial examination. As he passed through the area around Zou and Lu, he had a dream where someone led him to a place with grand and imposing buildings. The sign above the building read "Temple of Confucius." He was puzzled, thinking that Confucius had long been honored alongside the sage, so why was there a separate temple here. Soon, he was called in, and an elderly person dressed in ancient attire, around fifty years old with gray, well-groomed hair and eyebrows, bowed and entered. He was asked to sit next to him, and the elderly person said, "You are a famous scholar from Xijiang. Do you know how to interpret the first chapter of *The Analects*: 'Filial piety and fraternal respect are the foundation of benevolence'?" Zhou replied, "Benevolence is the foremost of the five virtues, and filial piety and fraternal respect are the foremost virtues within benevolence." The elderly person said, "That is not correct. In ancient times, the characters for 'person' and 'benevolence' were the same. My first name is 'One who is filial and fraternal,' and the last sentence is 'Filial piety and fraternal respect are the foundation of benevolence.' The meaning is the same. The scholars of the Han and Song dynasties did not recognize that 'benevolence' and 'person' were the same character. They placed filial piety and fraternal respect outside of benevolence, adding unnecessary complications. You will go back to the world and explain this to the students." Zhou nodded and left. That year, he passed the imperial examination and became an imperial scholar, entering the Hanlin Academy.

I believe that in this chapter, "仁" (ren) is interpreted as "人" (ren), and there should be no doubt about this. In Wuhu, a man named Bao, skilled in painting, specialized in learning from Mi Yuanzhang and was able to capture the general essence of his style. Moreover, he could make the paper look old through the technique of burnishing and staining, and even experts could not tell the difference. Many collectors from the north and south bought his works, and he became wealthy as a result. One day, after he had grown tired from painting, he sat down and dozed off. Suddenly, he saw a person wearing Tang-style headgear and Song-style clothing, who entered his courtyard and shouted, "I am Mi Yuanzhang! You only learned the surface of my painting and deceived the world for profit. In a thousand or ten thousand years, people will only think of my paintings as being like this. Then my identity and name will all be ruined by you!" The apparition then took a stone from his sleeve and struck Bao's right arm. Bao felt a sharp pain and woke up in shock. From then on, when he held the brush, his wrist hurt and he could hardly bear it; but when he held chopsticks to count money, he was still fine. There was a man named Qiu from Wu. As a child, he studied classical essays and failed the exams many times. In anger, he said, "The Song scholars misled me!" So he burned all his *Jiangzhang* (lectures) and *Yulu* (sayings), and turned to the study of textual research. He revered Zheng Xuan and Kong Yingda as sages, while despising Cheng Hao, Cheng Yi, and Zhu Xi.

The narrator, poor, traveled to study in Chu and Shu. Passing through Mount Emei, he sat under an ancient pine tree, reviewing the commentary and annotations on the *Yili* (Rites of Zhou). Suddenly, a white-furred tiger carried him away. After walking several miles, the tiger dropped him into a deep valley and then left. The narrator felt regret, thinking this might be the retribution for rejecting the teachings of the Song Confucians. Just as he was feeling disappointed, he saw a stone gate open on the side of the valley. He entered, and there were grand and magnificent halls, with a sign reading "Civilization Hall." Millions of books were lined up on both sides, no one knew the number. The narrator flipped through the book catalog, expecting the *Six Classics* to be at the beginning, but unexpectedly, after flipping through, there were none. He felt doubtful. There was an anciently dressed person leaning against the door. The narrator bowed and asked, "Who is the god who resides here?" The person replied, "The ancient sage." The narrator asked, "The ancient sage originally created writing, should have countless volumes laid out, why are there no ancient *Six Classics*?" The anciently dressed person said, "There used to be these books, but they were called *Shi* (Poetry), *Shu* (Documents), *Zhouyi* (Changes), not *Jing* (Classics). Since the Han people did many things, they named them *Six Classics*, created annotations and commentaries, forced connections and interpretations, causing the anger of the Supreme Deity, blaming the ancient sage for creating writing, which led to this source of trouble. From then on, the annotations and commentaries were removed from the Civilization Hall, causing you to be unable to flip through them." The narrator asked, "Why did the annotations and commentaries cause the anger of the Supreme Deity?" The person replied, "The details of this matter are very long, you should listen quietly to my words. Do you know that there is only one sky over all the nations and regions? Since Pangu created the world, the legendary rulers of ancient China, none did not revere the Supreme Deity, the sky also enjoyed the sacrifices of oxen, for thousands of years."

Suddenly, during the late Eastern Han period, five demon deities appeared, each wearing a ceremonial crown with flowing ribbons and a dragon robe, invading heaven and claiming their titles. One of them, self-proclaiming himself as Cihou Nu, had a red face with a bristling beard, looking particularly fierce and evil. The other four brothers were dressed in green, yellow, white, and black, respectively, and were known as Lingwei Yang, Han Shuji, Bai Zhaoju, and Bian Guangji. They frowned and raised their heads, making noisy and quarrelsome demands, ultimately intending to seize the position of the supreme deity and divide the realm into five kingdoms. The supreme deity questioned the five about their origins and the source of their mandate, but they all stared blankly and could not answer. The emperor ordered divine soldiers to capture them, and the battle was still undecided. Just then, the Sage of the East ascended to heaven and reported, "These five deities' names are all prophecies and ominous words, passed down by Han scholars like Zheng Xuan and his disciples. If only Zheng Xuan is summoned, they will surrender without fighting." The emperor had no choice but to comply and immediately ordered the messenger from the underworld to summon Zheng Yuan and his disciples to the throne. Seeing their mature and dignified demeanor, and after drinking three hundred cups of wine without getting drunk, the emperor appointed them as officials in the Hall of Culture. The five demon deities finally submitted and remained still.

In all that Zheng proposed, the emperor also issued to the world. After a long time, it became evident that some of the teachings could not be implemented. The emperor’s crown used 288 pieces of jade, almost crushing his head. In the summer, when sacrificing to the earth, the emperor must wear a heavy fur coat, almost suffocating his body. He was only allowed one meal a day, but he must be persuaded to eat a second time, almost starving his stomach. In funeral rites, two sheng and four he of rice were used for the mouth and body, and the lord and high officials put four sheng of millet and wheat into their mouths. If the jaw could not be opened, a small hole was drilled in the cheek to insert the food. All descendants felt heartache. Misunderstandings spread, and over time, they became accepted, lasting nearly a thousand years. One day, the Heavenly Emperor sat in the Purple Palace and saw a creature fly down from the clouds. It had dragon scales and a horse mane, and it cried out in complaint: “I am a qilin, and I do not eat living insects or tread on evil grass. Everyone calls me a benevolent beast, and I wait for a sage to appear before descending to the world.

Vajuan (郑玄) interprets '觏' as '交媾,' meaning sexual intercourse, and explains that it involves the union of essence and the descent of the heart. He also interprets '五日为期，六日不詹' to mean that a woman should not go without sexual intercourse for more than five days, as it can lead to physical and emotional distress. The women, all of whom are noble and virtuous, should not be overly sexual. The qilin (麒麟) stood beside them and laughed loudly. The emperor asked, 'Why are you laughing?' The qilin replied, 'The ladies only know to blame Vajuan (郑玄), but they do not blame Daiseng (戴圣). Daiseng (戴圣) created the *Liji* (Book of Rites), and his sin is even greater. I once accompanied the noble young man (振振公子) in the mythical garden (灵囿) of King Wen of Zhou, and I saw that the palace women of King Wen had no fixed number, usually not more than twenty or thirty, and there were no titles such as 'nine consorts,' 'twenty-seven secondary consorts,' or 'eighty-one concubines.' I also did not see any 'golden ring to advance' or 'silver ring to retreat' rules.'

King Wen of Zhou was so busy that he did not have time to rest even at the end of the day, and he enjoyed life without excess; how could he possibly have the time to spend fifteen nights with over a hundred women? Dai Sheng was originally a corrupt official who created classical texts about the palace to flatter a foolish ruler; and Zheng Yuan, his disciple, further supported his work, leading to the later practice of the Sui palace using five stones of incense daily and the Kaiyuan palace having over 60,000 women, all initiated by him. Moreover, in his commentary on the *Book of Songs*, he explained that "the young woman does not provide enough," saying that "the character ''" refers to a woman's vulva, which is an event mentioned in the biography of the 13th king of the Jing dynasty, and such cruel punishment did not exist in the Three Dynasties. The Heavenly Emperor heard this and greatly regretted, saying, "I have made a mistake in choosing people." He called the divine sage and said, "You have made a contribution to the world through your creation of characters. Great sages like Duke of Zhou and Confucius are all your disciples. Unfortunately, later vulgar scholars have led to such a state of affairs. How can we remedy this?" The divine sage replied, "My three brothers and I created characters together. The characters I created all go downward. My younger brothers, Ju Song and Qu Lu, created characters that go either to the right or to the left. Characters that go to the right or left are used in the eastern and western regions; characters that go downward are used in the Central Plains. Now the eastern and western regions have only one teaching, but the teaching in the Central Plains is so chaotic. Therefore, we should invite those who have not yet completed their Buddhist studies from the West. To show great supernatural powers and eliminate these people completely."

The emperor said, "Summoning Buddha is correct, but why must one summon those who have not yet completed their study of Buddhism?" The ancient sage replied, "Buddha has no husband, wife, or father, hence it is called heretical. Fearing that if it comes to China, many people will not accept it. Only by briefly borrowing Buddhist texts to study them, and in middle age returning to Confucius and Zhou, acting like a Mohist but named as a Confucian, will people then be willing to accept it. The best ones from the Song dynasty are the most suitable." The Qilin, standing by, argued, "Chu certainly lost, but Qi was not right either. According to Han scholars' saying 'Qilin beats drums in the suburbs of heaven,' it's just that the Qilin brings bad luck, but the Heavenly Emperor still gets a good meal. If the Song scholars lead the moral and ritual teachings, explaining 'Heaven's will is what we call nature,' saying 'Heaven is the principle itself,' the ancient emperors only sacrificed to Heaven, not to principle. Will the Heavenly Emperor's sacrifices not be cut off from this? Moreover, the sharp-nosed thunder god might still cause trouble." The emperor asked, "Why is that?" The Qilin replied, "Zhu Xi's commentary has three sentences about 'abundant food,' saying 'Respecting the host's courtesy, not because of the food.' In the following commentary on 'When there is a sudden thunder, one must change,' it says 'Respecting the anger of Heaven.'"

Is it not that the following text secretly implies something different about the thunder? From this point on, no one fears the Thunder God, and how could the Thunder God be content with this? The Heavenly Emperor smiled and said, "What you say is true, but fates and fortunes have their ups and downs, and I cannot control it either. Let us summon those who are enlightened and see their true nature, and test their skills to see how they fare." Soon, the sage led the Song scholars onto the hall: there were those with wide sleeves and broad hats holding the Tai Chi circle; those who closed their eyes, pointed to their hearts, and claimed to be always alert; and those who picked flowers, played with the moon, and called themselves lively and vibrant. Finally, four people carried a large bucket, placing a thousand stalks of rice straw on top, saying, "This is a rice bucket. Since the deaths of Confucius and Mencius, no one can carry this bucket. The Tang scholar Han Yu foolishly tried to carry the bucket, and I took his letters with the Great Venerable Monk and found the real evidence, overturning many of the buckets he carried. How much more so would Zheng and Kong dare to cause trouble for us?" Not finished speaking, indeed, the five evil spirits, Red Furious and White Resisting, climbed walls and dug holes, and fled with lowered flags and silenced drums. The Heavenly Emperor was greatly pleased and immediately appointed these four as temporary officials of the Hall of Civilization. This is why Han learning has not flourished, and why the Hall of Civilization has no duties.

Qiu asked, "Given that, why are the annotations and commentaries of Song scholars not collected on the shelves?" The response was, "One mistake cannot be allowed to be repeated; the position of Song scholars is also likely to not last long. At present, the Lu and Wang families, as well as the current scholars Yan Xizhai, Li Gangzhu, and Mao Xihé, all challenge him." While they were discussing, suddenly the sound of bells and drums was heard, and inside, the ancient sage conveyed an imperial decree, saying, "I have ordered the white tiger to carry Qiu to come. Originally, I disliked his boasting about Han studies, looking down on all schools of thought, and using the emperor to command the feudal lords, so I had the intention to cast him into the jaws of tigers. Now I have heard that he has repented of his errors; I can grant him a cup of misty tea from the mountains, lead him out of the mountains, and make him recount what he has heard, so as to enlighten the world."

An ancient scholar led the way through a winding ravine, and Qiu asked, "According to the teachings of the ancient sages, the study of Han texts is not to be followed; according to the sayings of the Qilin, the scholars of the Song dynasty are also not to be valued. Then where shall I go?" The divine response was, "Adapting to the times is of great importance! A gentleman scholar acts according to the circumstances, hence the saying 'those who follow the will of heaven prosper.' For instance, in the practice of divine guidance, the worship of Emperor Zhang has declined, while the veneration of Guan Yu has risen—this is a clear example before our eyes. During the heyday of Han studies, Wang Bi of the Jin dynasty criticized Zheng Xuan as a 'old servant.' Zheng Xuan appeared in broad daylight, demanded his life, and left. Yuan Xingcong remarked, 'Today people would rather say that Confucius was mistaken, than to speak ill of Zheng Xuan.' This was also out of fear of Zheng Xuan's malevolent influence. Now that the prevailing trends have waned, his spirit is no longer potent, and people rarely discuss Confucius and Zheng Xuan anymore. During the peak of Song studies, the Yuan dynasty held rituals to honor Zhu Xi, even calling out the imperial name Chengjisihan during the ceremonies, elevating him to a status equal to heaven. When the Ming dynasty ascended, they invited four scholars from Jinhua to teach, all of whom were disciples of Zhu Xi, passing down his teachings in a direct lineage. They promulgated the *Four Books Comprehensive Commentary*, which became widely circulated throughout the land, binding the minds of intelligent and talented individuals, compelling them to adhere strictly to its teachings and neglect other books. Yang Sheng'an said, 'There are echo-chasing insects. Today’s scholars of Confucianism are all echo-chasing insects of the Song scholars.' If you do not become an echo-chasing insect, how can you achieve success in the imperial examinations and repay your sovereign and father?

Qiu asked, "Then does God also like the style of eight-legged essays?" The ancient person dressed in traditional attire laughed loudly and said, "God is not a scholar, so why would He need eight-legged essays? Not only does God not have such essays, but even the Dragon Palace and the Two You Mountains have never had such rotten things. Even ancient books with small print and narrow boards do not have such ugly forms." Qiu asked, "Then how is it that there are so many heroes among those who passed the imperial examinations through the study of eight-legged essays?" The god replied, "Scholars are like fish; they can be caught, shot, or netted. Large ones are like dragons and turtles, small ones like carp and minnows, all born from the same source, and the method of discovery does not change their inherent qualities. Throughout history, there have been famous officials who rose to prominence through the study of Confucian classics, poetry, essays, and policy discussions, as well as through the study of eight-legged essays. Heroes are not bound by the rules and regulations of the imperial examinations. Consider Lu Meng, who rose from a life of crime, and Guo Ziyi, who was once imprisoned but later became a hero. Even those who were once criminals can become heroes, and how much more so for those who passed the imperial examinations!" Qiu asked, "What does God like?" The god replied, "God likes poetry and literature." Qiu asked, "How do you know?" The god said, "Imagine God's palace being completed. Why would He not summon experienced scholars like Ma Jichang and Jing Dachun to write a record, but instead summon the young and lively Li He? Why would He not summon scholars like Zhou Dunyi, Cheng Hao, Zhang Zai, and Zhu Xi to teach and live in the abode of immortals, but instead summon Bai Juyi, who loved wine and women, and Shi Manqing, who was unrestrained and free-spirited?"

Qiu suddenly realized, and bowed twice, saying, "As the divine one has said, shall I abandon Han studies and Song studies, and instead focus on poetry and prose?" The spirit replied, "You are mistaken again! Human nature varies, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. Talent for writing is like water; if it has a source, it naturally becomes a river. Textual criticism and scholarship are like fire; if one's heart is empty, one must rely on external things to gain recognition, just as fire must cling to firewood. Your nature inherently lacks substance; how can you not waver between two opinions? Moreover, you have already mastered Han studies; try asking what the name of the rice that the emperor eats is?" Qiu could not answer. The spirit said, "Kong Yingda's commentary on 'sou sou' states: 'Pounding and scattering, making it tend toward the凿. One dan of millet becomes coarse, one dou becomes稗, and after removing eight sheng it becomes凿, and after removing nine sheng it becomes侍御.侍御 is what the king eats.' Try to think about what happens to the millet after it has been pounded eight or nine times; where will the coarse,秕, and chaff go? Heaven deliberately creates this class of people who eat chaff and are content with秕; some are meticulous in their考证, others are abstract and impractical in their讲学, each excelling in their own area, forming their own groups. I often see Confucius, Buddha, and Laozi meeting in the air, smiling at each other, bowing, and passing without speaking; this is why the heavens and earth are great."

Qiu heard this, and his complexion turned ashen, his thoughts lingering and he couldn't move. The god said, "Rest now! You were carried by a tiger into a mountain ravine, and the *Yili Zhushu* (Commentary on the Rites of Zhou) you carried in your sleeve has been half eaten by grubs! Why not hurry back home?" Qiu knelt twice and left the cave, and it still exists to this day. ○The Venerable Da Tong, a certain scholar-official from Wu Men, understood Zen principles and aspired to become a Buddha. Hearing that a monk named Da Tong at Tiantai Mountain was a hundred and twenty years old, he set out on foot to visit him. He knocked twice on the thatched hut, but the monk refused to see him, and the scholar-official knelt at the door.

One day, the monk called the scholar in and asked, "What brings you here?" The scholar replied, "I wish to learn Buddhism." The monk said, "Are you not the son of a certain Minister of Works?" The scholar answered, "Yes." "Is he still alive?" the monk asked. "He is," the scholar replied. "Do you have a wife and children?" the monk inquired. "I do," the scholar responded. The monk said, "You are mistaken! The Buddha's nature is compassionate. Your father is still alive, and your wife is still with you. Yet, you have the heart to leave your father and abandon your wife, greedily seeking to become a Buddha. Can this heart see the Buddha?" The scholar could not answer. The monk then asked, "To become a Buddha, one must accumulate merit. What merit have you accumulated?" The scholar said, "In times of famine, I always advocate for donations and distribute rice. When I encounter coffins, I bury them. Year after year, I buy living creatures and release them." The monk said, "Those who have the intention to accumulate virtue for the sake of gaining blessings are the same as those without virtue. It is like the law, where if someone kills by mistake, they are not held responsible for the death. You greedily seek to become a Buddha, yet force yourself to do good deeds. What merit is there? If you truly want to learn Buddhism, you should first learn from me, starting from this moment. I sit when I sit, I eat when I eat, I urinate and defecate when I urinate and defecate, I sleep when I sleep. Can you do the same?" The scholar replied, "I can." The monk made a long sigh, then closed his eyes and sat on the bed, remaining silent, not drinking, not eating, not sleeping, and not using the bathroom for a whole day. The scholar's bones and joints ached, his abdomen made rumbling sounds, and urine and feces flowed, but the monk was unaware. Unable to bear it, the scholar got up, knelt before the monk, and wished to return home. The monk did not answer, bowed his hands, smiled, and sent him out.

A Ghost of Surplus: A Tale from the Guangling Fayun Temple At the Guangling Fayun Temple, there was a monk named Ming Chu, who was close friends with a merchant from Zhongshan named Zhang. After Zhang's death, Ming Chu held several vegetarian feasts and recited scriptures for him. One day, Ming Chu unexpectedly met Zhang in the market. Ming Chu had not yet eaten, and Zhang invited him into a restaurant, where he ordered some flatbread. After eating, Ming Chu asked, "You are already dead, how can you be here?" Zhang replied, "I have a minor sin that has not been absolved, and I am now assigned to be a ghost of surplus in Yangzhou." Ming Chu asked, "What is a ghost of surplus?" Zhang explained, "All officials, merchants, and traders have a fixed amount of profit or interest. Any excess is considered surplus, which I can take. There are many like me in the human world." He then pointed to passersby and said that they were all ghosts of surplus. Soon after, a monk passed by, and Zhang pointed to him and said, "This monk is also one of us." He called the monk over to talk, and after a while, the monk disappeared. Ming Chu and Zhang walked south and encountered a woman selling flowers. Zhang said, "These two people are also ghosts, and the flowers she is selling are also used by ghosts, not by humans." Zhang paid for some flowers with a few coins and gave them to Ming Chu, saying, "Anyone who sees these flowers and laughs is a ghost." He then bid farewell and left. The flowers were bright red and very heavy, and Ming Chu returned in a dazed state. On the way back, many people laughed at the flowers. When he reached the north gate of the temple, Ming Chu thought to himself that he had traveled with a ghost and now held a flower from the ghost world, which felt particularly ominous. He threw the flowers into a ditch, and the sound of the water splashing echoed.

Upon returning, the people in the same courtyard noticed that his appearance was very unusual, thinking he was possessed by evil spirits. They all held up medicinal herbs and potions to save him. After a long time, he finally regained consciousness and explained the reason in detail. So they all went to look at the flower again, and it turned out to be a dead person's hand. Chapter Six: Duo Guan Duo Guan was a native of Putian, Fujian. He lost his father in infancy and depended on his aunt Zhang to nurse him. As he grew up, he became beautiful, and both his brother and aunt loved him. His brother went far away to do business and sometimes did not return for years. The aunt often stayed at her mother's house, taking the uncle with her and sending Duo Guan to an external tutor. In the town, Mr. Ye taught students at his home, and Duo Guan went to study there. Chen Zhongshao from Jiangxi was a noble young man. At eighteen, he passed the local examination, and his brother served in Fujian. Because his wife had died, he went to visit his brother. On the way to Putian, it rained, and he met Duo Guan on the road. Chen Zhongshao was so captivated that his spirit was taken, and he got off his carriage to follow him. Duo Guan looked back and saw Chen Zhongshao pulling his fresh clothes, wearing white boots and walking in muddy water, his appearance like that of a madman. He was quite suspicious of him. Chen Zhongshao followed him all the way to his home but could not enter. He asked the neighbors and learned that it was Duo Guan, who had returned from the school and then went to his aunt's house. Chen Zhongshao arrived at his brother's office and plotted with his favorite servant, Jing'er, to get Duo Guan. Jing'er said, "Why don't you ask your brother to let you travel and study? If he agrees, then the matter will be successful." His brother indeed agreed. Chen entrusted the magistrate of Putian to prepare a generous gift for Mr. Ye. Mr. Ye treated him with the courtesy due to a noble young man, not knowing that he was a distinguished scholar. Chen visited all his fellow students. When Duo Guan saw him, he was greatly surprised for a long time, realizing that the guest had come for him. From then on, he refused to visit and only locked himself in his room to read. After a month, he still could not find a way to communicate with Chen.

One night, Zhong Shao heard the moaning of Du Guan, who was ill in bed. Ye and a doctor came to diagnose his pulse and said, "He is weak and about to collapse; he cannot be cured without four ounces of ginseng." Upon hearing this, Ye wanted to send him home. Zhong Shao angrily said, "His family is poor; how could they afford it? Even if he goes home, he will die!" He immediately opened his box, took out gold, and gave it to the doctor. Then he told Ye, "If there is any problem, I will take full responsibility." He personally cared for Du Guan by administering medicine and soup, and did not remove his clothes for more than half a month. Du Guan soon recovered and was deeply grateful to Zhong Shao. They began to visit each other frequently, but there was never any playful demeanor. Zhong Shao found no opportunity to approach Du Guan, so he discussed the matter with Jing Er. Jing said, "I know he is grateful to you, but do you know if he loves you? You can pretend to be ill and test him." Following his advice, Du Guan came, and cared for Zhong Shao just as Zhong had cared for him. Jing Er bribed the doctor to pretend that the medicine required human arm blood to be effective. He ordered Jing to do it, and Jing pretended to refuse. Du Guan remained silent while standing by, and in the dark, he pierced his flesh to mix blood with the medicine and administered it. Zhong Shao, upon learning this, was greatly pleased and thought that he could now move forward. Just then, his brother Ying recommended him to the capital, and invited Zhong to go with him. Du Guan heard this and came to Zhong's room at night, saying, "In the past, you spent money to save me; did you not love me? Now your departure date is near, and out of righteousness, I cannot betray you. I will establish a three-day relationship and swear to keep this body until your return." He stayed in Zhong's room for three days, and then Zhong left.

Ye had a nephew named Chun, who was lustful and evil, but also very strong. He was attracted to the beauty of officials and wanted to have a close relationship with one, but was refused. One day, when Zhongshao's messenger arrived, Duoguan placed the letter on the table and went out to inquire about Zhong's well-being. Chun sneaked in and saw many intimate words in Zhong's letter. He said happily, "This can be exploited." When Duoguan returned, Chun showed him the letter and said, "You followed Chen Gongzi, but can't you also follow me?" Duoguan initially wanted to refuse, but then thought there was a letter and was concerned that the evidence could not be destroyed. He then pretended to laugh and said, "If you return my letter, tonight I will follow you." Chun was happy and returned the letter. Duoguan burned the letter and wrote two letters, one to bid farewell to Zhong and one to inform his sister-in-law. He placed the letters in a box and immediately took out his knife and committed suicide. His sister-in-law heard the news and opened the box to find the letters, and she brought the matter to court. Chun died in prison. When Zhongshao returned home and saw the left-behind letters, he was deeply moved and nearly fainted. He felt moved by Duoguan's righteousness and swore not to marry again. One night, he had a dream where Duoguan appeared and said, "Do not stop your ancestral worship because of me. If you marry, I will be your successor." Zhongshao followed his advice and had a son who looked very much like Duoguan, so he named him Xiduo. Before the events took place, Jing'er had predicted, "Duoguan is truly beautiful, but his eyes and brows have too much heroic spirit. At best, he can be a loyal minister or a heroic figure, but I worry about an unfortunate end." Indeed, the outcome matched what Jing'er had predicted.

### Translation #### Two Dream Prayers A scholar from Yixing, in his youth, went to the temple of Yu Zhongsu to pray for a dream. That night, he dreamed that a low-ranking official by the deity's side came and touched his arm in a familiar manner. The scholar became angry and woke up shouting, thinking that Yu Zhongsu could not control his subordinates and was not worthy of respect. He told all his friends and relatives about this. Later, the scholar became a Jinshi (imperial scholar) and was appointed as the magistrate of Longyang County in Huguang Province. He served there for more than ten years and died in office. Zhao Liteng prayed for a dream at his grave and dreamed that Shao Bao was sitting on a table, with two candles burning on it. The candles had green letters that read, "The crown passes through the South Pole, and the articles are listed on the upper platform," which he took as a great omen. However, he eventually died of illness. When he was being buried, his disciples gathered to pay their respects and set up an altar to offer sacrifices. The two candles in front of the altar had the same green letters, which were the same two sentences. #### Ghosts Being Scattered and Reassembled In Shaoxing, Fu Changchun, who was staying at Hu Fuyun's office, recounted an incident. One day, after Hu had finished handling affairs in the main hall, he told his fellow officials, "Just now, while sitting in the main hall, a low-ranking official arrived suddenly. As soon as he entered the door, a ghost suddenly emerged and met the official, who pushed the ghost down. The ghost's limbs scattered all over the ground, like ears, eyes, nose, mouth, hands, feet, waist, and abdomen, all peeled open and wriggling. After a while, they gradually reconnected, and after a long time, the ghost got up and left." Hu observed that the low-ranking official had very strong qi (vital energy), and the ghost was mistakenly caught by this energy and could not avoid being pushed down. At the moment of falling, the official did not even know it. There were many ghosts in the side corridor, laughing at the scene but not moving forward.

A Strange Phenomenon Involving a Stone Slab: According to Zhu Shulou from Tongcheng, his father once lived in Chaoyang County, not far from his home, where there was a dangerous mountain that was rarely visited by people. One day, a tenant reported, "There is the sound of a wooden fish on the mountain, but no monks have been seen coming and going. Please investigate." His father led several tenants through thorny and difficult terrain to the top of the mountain. They saw an old monk sitting on a meditation cushion in a stone cave, chanting and using a wooden fish. The father asked, "Where do you come from?" The monk did not answer. The father then asked, "Do you need food?" The monk replied, "I have been abstaining from food for many years. What need is there for fasting?" After saying this, the monk closed his eyes and continued to sit in meditation. The people were surprised and went back down the mountain. Zhu Shulou told his mother about the monk, and his mother said, "This is a divine monk. I have saved 500 taels of silver. You should build a Buddhist temple on the mountain to honor this monk." Zhu Shulou then led a group of people to start construction. The monk suddenly came out of the cave and pointed to a specific spot, saying, "If you see a stone slab here, be careful not to move it lightly, as doing so will cause a demon to emerge." The people did not believe the monk and thought there might be hidden gold under the stone. They dug up the stone slab when the monk was not around. Suddenly, a dark cloud rose into the sky, and flying sand blinded their eyes. The monk quickly came out of the cave and said, "The demon has escaped. Not believing my words, you have caused harm to yourselves. What can be done?"

The work was not yet completed when, indeed, a servant from the Fang family was almost killed by two female demons. The master came in a hurry, seeking advice from the monk, who then descended the mountain, built an altar, and set up a seven-star lamp. After the incantation lasted for a while, with both sleeves waving, he shouted into the void, "You have been in seclusion for a long time, but your wild nature still remains. Quickly follow me up the mountain to cultivate." That night, the Fang family found peace. Afterward, those who came up the mountain often saw two beautiful women standing beside the monk, holding scrolls and burning incense, their graceful postures charming, which everyone considered strange. Like this for six years. One day, the monk called Zhu and said, "I am called Daliang, and I once encountered a strange person who advised me to become a monk. Now my spiritual cultivation is complete, and I will ascend tomorrow. The two demons have already converted to Buddhism and will go elsewhere to cultivate true essence, but there is still a karmic debt with the Fang family. After my transformation, you must provide for them for seven days to resolve this matter." The next day, the monk set himself on fire. Thus, the two women returned to the Fang family, attaching themselves to the servant and demanding food and drink, saying, "I have not seen a play in a thousand years. Please perform seven plays for me, and I will only forgive you for the sake of the monk." The Fang family complied, and after the performance, everything became quiet. Only a red note was left on the table in the main hall, with the words "Yan Hong Huan Cui thanks for the play" written in large characters.

A Zombie's Greed for Wealth During the reign of the Ming dynasty, there was a man named Zhang Yugu from Jinling who befriended someone named Li. They traveled together to buy goods in Guangdong. Zhang had to return to the south due to some matters, and Li entrusted him with a family letter. After Zhang returned home, he sent a letter to Li's family and saw a servant in the hall, realizing that Li's father had passed away. Zhang performed a ritual offering and ceremony on behalf of Li's family. Li's family was grateful to him, and his wife came out to see him. She was only in her twenties and had a very beautiful and elegant appearance. She prepared a meal to entertain Zhang. It was already late, and she invited Zhang to stay overnight at her house. The place where he stayed was separated from the place where the coffin was by a courtyard. By the second drumbeat of the night, the moonlight was very bright. Zhang saw Li's wife come out from inside and peek through the crack in the window. He was surprised and thought it was a suspicious situation for a man and a woman to be in such a position. If he were to push the door open, he should sternly reject her. Soon, he saw this woman holding a stick of incense, whispering something to herself as if she were making a plea before her father-in-law's spirit. After finishing her plea, she returned to where Zhang was staying, untied her belt, and tied it tightly around the iron ring on the door. She then slowly walked away. Zhang was even more surprised and suspicious and dared not go to bed and sleep.

Suddenly, a sound was heard at the place where the coffin was stopped, and the coffin lid fell to the ground. A person sat up, with a dark face, sunken eyes, and green eyes that gleamed, appearing extremely fierce and evil. He walked out with large steps, heading straight for Zhang's place, let out a ghostly howl, and a cold wind arose. The ropes tied to the door were instantly cut. Zhang tried his best to block the door, but his strength was no match, and the corpse rushed in. Fortunately, there was a large wooden cabinet nearby; Zhang pushed it against the ghost, causing the cabinet to fall on the ghost, pinning it down, and Zhang also fell unconscious. Li's wife heard the commotion and rushed to the scene with her servants holding candles. She revived Zhang with ginger soup and told him, "This is my husband-in-law, who was always unscrupulous; after death, he turned into a zombie and often caused trouble. He loves money the most; last night, he sent a dream to me saying, 'There will be a messenger named Zhang who will come to my house, carrying two hundred gold pieces; I will harm him and take the money. I will place half in my coffin and give the other half to your household.' I thought it was a strange dream and did not believe what he said. I did not expect you to come here to stay; I burned incense and prayed, urging him not to harbor evil thoughts. Afraid he would push the door and harm you, I tied the door handle with ropes, but the ghost's power was so great." She then carried the corpse into the coffin with her servants. Zhang advised burning the body quickly to cut off his evil influence. She said, "I have had this idea for a long time, but because of my husband-in-law, I felt reluctant; now I have no choice but to follow custom." Zhang helped with the expenses for the ritual and invited famous monks to perform the rites and burn the body, thus bringing peace to the family.

A Fox Spirit Invites the Little General Li Banxian to a Banquet One day, a fox spirit invited the Little General Li Banxian, a man from Fengtian, whose teacher was Huang, a high-ranking official and mentor during the reign of Emperor Gaozong. Huang had served as the magistrate of Tongzhou, where he was overly merciful and was accused by his superiors of allowing bandits to harm the people. He was exiled to Fengtian, where he taught and instructed students. Upon meeting Li Banxian, Huang said, "You can spread the teachings, but you are not suited for the pursuit of official rank." Li Banxian bowed and accepted his command. Huang instructed him to worship the Big Dipper for 49 days, then gave him a scroll and a sword, after which he could drive away evil spirits and heal diseases. Huang visited Yunnan every year, traveling thousands of miles in a very short time. After his term ended, he was released but his ultimate fate remains unknown, as he was a man of esoteric arts. Li Banxian visited the capital city once a year, staying in the residence of a high-ranking official. He often witnessed him commanding spirits and demons, which proved to be quite effective. One day, a fox spirit invited him to a banquet. The dishes served included pork, sheep, chicken, and duck, but they were all eaten plain, without salt or sauce. The servants standing on either side, holding the dishes, were all large foxes dressed in paper clothing, known as the Yellow Little General. The host, however, was a fox in human form, dressed in silk and satin. Li Banxian found this strange and asked why. The fox spirit replied, "Your kind has little fortune and should only wear paper clothing. If you wear silk, you will fall ill; if you wear satin, you will die. The reason I have invited you today is that I have a request. Our descendants often behave improperly when they are away. I ask the master of ceremonies to send me written documents whenever such matters arise, so that I can handle them according to my own rules. Please do not harm their lives. If there is a document, it can be burned at the foot of the bend in the wall near the Forbidden City. Call out 'Yellow Little General' three times, and I will receive it." Li Banxian nodded and left.

A person suffering from a wasting disease due to karmic entanglements was treated by a semi-divine healer who performed rituals to dispel the misfortune. If the cause was a malevolent spirit that could not be driven away, the healer would perform a ritual to banish it. The ritual involved using one measure of rice, inserting a sword into it, burning talismans, and reciting incantations. The sword would then fly and strike the doorpost, where a ghostly hand appeared, and a piece about eight inches long was severed. The patient was cured, and the healer immediately left, accepting no gratitude or reward. --- In the case of two matters in the office of Xu Mingfu, the official Xu Zhen Jia, who was initially the magistrate of Gou Rong, had a relative named Zong who was involved in legal matters. The area around Gou Rong was mountainous, producing game such as pheasants, rabbits, deer, and roe deer. Each year, the official would summon hunters to capture these animals to supply the governor, as local tributes. One day, Xu Zhen Jia summoned the hunters to his office to test the firing of a gun. The loud explosion frightened Zong, who fled to a hidden place and remained motionless. By evening, he could not be found, so Xu sent people out of the city to search for him. They pursued him all the way to the provincial capital, where he hid in a small temple. Many people in the office believed that Zong was originally the offspring of a female fox, which explained his strange behavior.

After Xu was transferred to Qinghe and came to visit me on his way to the provincial capital, we stayed for a drink. He told me, "Among my friends in the office, many have external preferences. If the family members have any intention of displeasing his favorite servant, the official would want to leave immediately. Such a small matter would cause much trouble, leading to this custom becoming widespread. Even the dogs in the office followed suit, with two males pairing up, how can it not be ridiculous." A respected scholar named Sun, who was present, said, "What is there to be surprised about? My female duck and female chicken often act as if they are pairing up, which is even more laughable." Regarding the varied effects of sulfur, it is known that sulfur is toxic. However, those who take it often live long and healthy lives, while others die from boils on their backs or necks. Fortunes and misfortunes vary because each person's constitution is different. In our dynasty, the grand minister Tongzai Yong chewed snow and ice on the Winter Solstice, feeling no cold, and said it was because he had a yang constitution. The respected official Yin Wenrui did not wear a sable hat in the severe winter, but if he wore one, he would sweat profusely even in heavy snow. In the Song dynasty, the respected official Xia Ying took cinnabar and sulfur. If he missed these two ingredients, his hands and feet would turn cold, truly inexplicable. In Hangzhou, the respected teacher Wang Shishi Lin often took sulfur. After a long time, small blisters would often appear on his pores, with a column of blue smoke shooting out directly, all emitting the smell of sulfur. It is said that the poison came out through his pores, and there were no other problems. Until now, the person is still alive and healthy.

A nighttime boat from Hangzhou, traveling a hundred miles, was crowded with men and women mixed together, separated only by a partition. A young man from Renhe, known for his flirtatious nature and self-proclaimed charm, boarded the boat to travel to Fuyang. Peering through the partition, he saw a young woman who seemed to smile at him, and he assumed she was interested in him. By the third drumbeat, when the other passengers were asleep, the partition suddenly opened, and someone touched his lower body. The young man was overjoyed, raised himself to touch back, and found the person to be a woman. He climbed into her space, and they engaged in a sexual encounter without speaking a word.
