At dawn, the young man tried to leave his cabin, but the woman held him tightly, thinking he loved her and became even more affectionate. When the sun rose, he saw that the woman had white hair and was shocked. She said, "I am an old beggar woman, over sixty years old, with no husband, no children, and no relatives. I was worried about where to find a place to live, but unexpectedly, you showed me affection last night. The saying goes, 'A night of marriage brings a hundred nights of thought.' You are now my husband, and I am willing to rely on you, without asking for any dowry. I will follow you, eating when you eat and drinking when you drink. What do you think?" The young man panicked and called for help from the others. They all woke up and laughed, advising the young man to give the old woman some money, and she finally let him return to his cabin. As he looked back, the woman laughed at him again.

Mr. Chai Dongsheng: He boarded a night ferry bound for Wuxing, with fifteen people on board, both old and young. The boat was small and the number of passengers was large, so they had to squeeze together to sleep. In the middle of the night, he suddenly heard someone with a Shaanxi accent scolding harshly: "You are so rude!" He grabbed one person and beat him severely, shouting: "I am fifty-eight years old this year, and I have never done such a thing before. Now you took advantage of me being asleep and inserted your male organ into my anus. I woke up in pain, injuring my parents' legacy, and cannot face my ancestors in death. If you gentlemen do not believe me, please look at my two buttocks; there is still wet saliva on them." The person being beaten remained silent without a word. Chai and the other passengers got up and lit a fire, trying to mediate. They saw a young man with shame spreading across his face, his nose knocked out by the old man, blood flowing throughout the cabin. Chai asked, "What is your profession, sir?" The old man replied, "I am from Tongzhou in Shaanxi, and I teach children as a profession. I have always studied Confucianism and practiced the method of recording good and bad deeds as advocated by Yuan Mei. I have never had a single thought of lust, so how could I suffer such a karmic retribution?" Mr. Chai smiled and said, "Your practice of the method of recording good and bad deeds can help others in times of need, which is also a virtue. But if you were to beat this person to death, that would be excessive. We will make the disrespectful person bow and confess guilt for you, and each of us will spend two hundred coins to buy wine and meat to offer to the water god, for your repentance. How does that sound?" The old man nodded in agreement, and then released the young man.

At dawn, various guests gathered and laughed, encouraging each other to drink. The old man sat high and ate heartily, while the beaten person lowered their head and did not drink. Another young man laughed incessantly, dressed like a young female performer in a theater troupe. Everyone then realized that the person he had arranged to meet for a night of pleasure was this young man. In the 41st year of Qianlong, Sheng Linji, a 32-year-old man from Le'an County, suddenly used a kitchen knife to cut off the heads of his mother and sister. He placed them high on a table and bought incense, flowers, lanterns, and candles to worship them. His neighbors were shocked and asked him why he did this. He smiled and said, "I sent them to a good place to become Buddhas. I am just fulfilling my filial duty." The village head reported the incident to the authorities, who came to investigate. Sheng Linji welcomed them calmly and gave the same account as he had to his neighbors. The authorities requested the emperor's decree for his execution by slicing. As he faced death, he smiled and said nothing. According to his neighbors, he had always been quite filial to his mother and very harmonious with his sister.

### Sū Fuliàng's Castration Case Sū Chéng, a man from the city of Shānyáng in Shaanxi province, was naturally cunning and untrustworthy, and became even more wicked as he aged. He committed adultery with his son's wife, who refused him, threatening her with a knife. Out of necessity, she complied, but remained unwilling in her heart. Secretly, she plotted with her son Sū Fuliàng to move to a distant place to avoid him. Her relative, Niú Tínghuī, lived in a village thirty miles away from the city. They moved there, building a house facing the mountain, where they could easily call and respond to each other. After living there for over a month, Sū Chéng received information about their location and followed it, bringing food to pay respects to Niú Tínghuī. Niú prepared a meal to entertain the guests, and all the villagers gathered. During the meal, a guest named Yán Qī, who was very close to Niú, asked about the recent situation. Niú informed him, "The business is not going well; I sold two donkeys and got thirty taels of silver, using ten taels to buy rice and repair the house. Only twenty taels are left at home." Sū Chéng had lustful intentions toward his daughter-in-law but was annoyed by Sū Fuliàng's presence, finding it difficult to act. He knew that a neighbor, Sūn Sì, was extremely vicious and strong, feared by everyone in the village. So, he went to Sūn and plotted the murder of Niú Tínghuī, intending to divide the remaining money. Sūn Sì initially refused. Sū Chéng said, "My daughter-in-law is very beautiful. If you help me kill Niú Tínghuī and frame Sū Fuliàng, Sū Fuliàng will be punished. Then, I will give my daughter-in-law to you, not just dividing ten taels among us." Sūn Sì was moved and took it upon himself to kill Niú Tínghuī with a sense of determination.

On that night, with Zhao Cheng holding a knife, they rushed into the Niu family's home. Youliang, seeing the situation was dire, fled into a cave. Sun and Zhao, the two of them, ultimately killed the entire family of Niu, including the husband, wife, and children. They then went to report to the authorities, claiming that it was Youliang who had committed the crime. The county official, Lu Xuehong, hastily dispatched men to apprehend Youliang. Upon finding Youliang hiding in the cave, his actions were suspicious, so he was subjected to torture. Youliang, unable to bear to implicate his father, and also unable to withstand the torture, wept bitterly and falsely confessed. However, the knife used to kill the Niu family was originally owned by Sun's family and not by Zhao's. Despite repeatedly stating where the knife was hidden, repeated searches were unsuccessful. Lu Xuehong, believing that the weapon had not been found and that the verdict was ultimately not credible, prolonged the case through repeated examinations. As a result, over ten families of neighbors who had drank together at a banquet were implicated, leading to their property being seized.

One day, the police officer was leading Zhao Cheng back for another interrogation. Zhao Cheng was pleased that the case was almost over and walked slowly while singing loudly. His daughter-in-law saw him and scolded him, saying, "It is said that 'even a tiger does not eat its own cub.' You yourself killed someone, framed your son, and dragged your neighbors into trouble, yet you are still happy and singing? One should take responsibility for one's own actions. Will heaven and earth, and the ghosts and spirits, forgive you?" Zhao Cheng turned red and could not speak. The police officer reported his urgency to the official, who then thoroughly questioned Zhao Cheng. At first, Zhao Cheng did not confess, but after burning toxic fumes to his nose, he finally admitted the truth. According to the law, anyone who kills five people in one family must be punished by five people. The governor-general and the provincial governor were moved by the son's filial piety, and when they reported the case to the emperor, they added a document describing the circumstances. The emperor issued an imperial decree: Zhao Youliang's situation seems pitiable, but Zhao Cheng's wickedness is extreme. Such a person cannot be allowed to have descendants. Zhao Cheng shall be executed by the thousand cuts, and his son Zhao Youliang shall be castrated. After one hundred days, he shall be exiled to Heilongjiang. Outside the Shuncheng Gate in Beijing, there was a fight between two people, A and B. A punched B's throat, and B died and fell to the ground. It was already late, and the people on the road tied the murderer to the barracks and handed the body over to two soldiers to guard, waiting to report to the authorities in the morning. It happened to be raining snow, and an old and sick soldier, fearing the cold, told the younger soldier, "I will go home to put on warm clothes and drink some wine. I will be back in a short while." The younger soldier agreed. The old soldier did not return for a long time, and the younger soldier also bought some wine to keep warm and fell asleep drunk in the tent.

Early in the morning, they set out to search for the corpse, but it had vanished and could not be seen. Just as they were startled, an older person arrived and said, "I have already reported this to the district magistrate, who will come to investigate immediately." The younger person replied, "The corpse has been lost, and when the official arrives, there will be nothing to examine. Our crimes are serious, what shall we do?" The old soldier pondered for a long time and said, "I have a plan. At a certain deserted place, someone carried a coffin, which seems to contain a recently deceased person whose body has not yet decayed. I will break open the coffin with you, carry the corpse here, and substitute it to avoid punishment." The younger person agreed and followed the plan. Shortly after, the official arrived to examine the corpse, which had a long nail in its forehead, with blood flowing over its face. When questioned, the killer said, "I accidentally killed this person, and I did not nail the forehead. Moreover, this corpse's appearance is not that of the person I beat." The official could not make a decision. Just as they were shouting, a man shouted and entered, saying, "This matter has nothing to do with person A. I am the person who was beaten and fell to the ground. Initially, I lost consciousness and fell to the ground, but then I woke up and went home. I was not actually dead." The official then released the killer and investigated the person who carried the coffin from the deserted place. Upon closer examination, they discovered that the corpse with the nail in its forehead was named Liu Kuang, a dyer by profession. His wife had an affair with someone who, while Liu was drunk, nailed him to death. They released person A and punished the adulterer. A bystander commented, "A corpse is not something that can be replaced, yet the two soldiers had such a clever plan. This is not due to the soldiers' stupidity, but rather the skill of hidden gods and spirits."

All physical bodies of immortals and Buddhas are not true forms. Whenever I visit temples, I see statues of Bodhisattvas with physical bodies, and they are generally coated with lacquer and cloth. When you knock on them, they make a hollow sound. Although their tendons are twisted and barely visible, their heads and necks are always crooked. In Mount Wuyi, I saw a man named Cheng Gen, known as the "Grass Shoe Immortal," sitting in a stone cave. In Mount Jiuhua, I saw a monk named Wuxia, whose eyes hung down without pupils. When you shake his head, it still moves, and when you check his teeth, they are all rotten and fallen out. Only the Amitabha Buddha in Yongzhou, Guangxi, has a physical body with a head that is uniquely upright, and I often doubt this. Later, someone said: "During the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, there was a scholar named Xing who was studying at a village temple. One evening, he stepped out for a walk and heard someone crying out, 'I do not wish to become a Buddha.' Xing climbed a tree to secretly watch and saw a group of monks surrounding a monk, bowing with their hands together, and praying for him to be reborn in the Western Paradise. Beside them, there was a long iron bar, about three to four feet long. Xing did not understand the reason for this. He heard that in the county, there was a widespread rumor: 'On a certain day, a living Buddha will ascend to heaven. Please come to burn incense and pay respects.' More than ten thousand people came. Xing went to see, and the one ascending to heaven was the monk who had cried out, 'I do not wish to become a Buddha.' He had already been carried onto the incense platform to be burned. Xing immediately reported to the authorities, and upon examination, it was found that the monk was already dead. Blood was dripping continuously from the lotus seat, and there was a long iron nail driven through his anus and straight through the top of his head. The authorities arrested the evil monks and questioned them, and they said, 'We burned this monk to gain incense and money. Without using the iron nail, his head would be crooked at death and could not be upright.' So, all were punished according to the law. And those who had come to burn incense and make vows, now greatly regretted and scattered."

Outside the main gate of the temple in Quanzhou, there is a tomb. It is said that a certain censor entered the temple to worship Buddha, wanting to test whether it was a living body. He took a needle to prick the Buddha's ear, and fresh blood flowed out. The censor was greatly alarmed, fell out of the temple, and died. His family immediately buried him outside the temple gate as a warning. I observed the tombstone on the grave, which only recorded the name of someone from a previous dynasty, and there was no mention of this event. Although I did not prick the Buddha, I still stripped off the thirteen layers of clothing he wore, knocked on his chest and tapped it, and I also felt that my actions were disrespectful. In the southern part of Yandang Mountain, there are three moving and stationary stones, as large as a house supported by seven beams. One moves and one is stationary, pressing against each other. Tourists lie on the stones and push them with their feet. Even children aged seven or eight can make them move a few inches, making a loud sound. If pushed with hands, even more than ten porters cannot budge them a hair's breadth. All are physical phenomena of the world, some of which are inexplicable. In Yandang Mountain, there is a stone that looks like a standing woman, over five zhang long, with a bun-shaped hairstyle. When the azaleas bloom, they cover her head with red, but no flower touches her face. Her robe is slightly red, and her skirt is a somber green, as if naturally dyed, with clear boundaries. The folds of her clothes are distinctly woven-like.

### The Yu Biao of Mount Lu Beside the Zongsheng Hermitage on Mount Lu, there is a spring called Gu Lian Spring. The spring has a dangerous and deep cave. Someone descended into the cave and found a stone inscription with six characters in ancient script. The inscription reads: "Honghuang Yang Yu Nai Kai." The magistrate of Xingzi County, Ding Zhengxin, told me about this while we were seated by the Lotus Pond. ### Flying Bell, Dumb Bell, and Evil Bell On the summit of Fuhu Mountain in Wuyi Mountain, there is a bell hanging. It is said to have flown there during the Tang Dynasty, hanging thirty or more feet above the ground. No one could tie it, hence it is also called the "dumb bell." Outside Zhangjiakou, in the Temple of the Chief Magistrate, there is an "evil bell" that rings on its own after three o'clock at night. ### Rats Crossing the River In the fiftieth year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign, tens of thousands of rats crossed the river in a tail-to-tail formation, varying in size. They made a rustling sound as they moved through the water. In a short while, the river surface for a mile or so was covered by them. An old steersman said, "There must be a flood upstream." By July, Laian and Quanjiao counties experienced a sudden rise in water, causing all the embankments to collapse. ### The Passing of the Peng Bird In the sixtieth year of the Kangxi Emperor's reign, I was seven years old and had just started school. On July 3rd, after having lunch, the sky suddenly became as dark as night. Within a few moments, it gradually brightened, and the red sun shone brightly, with not a cloud in the sky. Someone said, "This is the great Peng bird passing by." The words of Zhuangzi, "wings like clouds hanging from the sky," are indeed not empty talk.

### Translation #### Jade Vessel in Stone In the 55th year of the Qianlong era, in Jingzhou, a mountain known as Zhou Wang Mountain collapsed, and a piece of uncut jade was carried downstream by the river. A farmer struck it with a hoe and found a jade bowl inside. The bowl was smooth, moist, white, and pure, without any carvings but with a blood-like coloration. The bowl was about six inches in circumference, but unfortunately, the stone was broken, and the bowl was damaged. The people could not understand how the bowl came to be inside the stone. Some said, "This must have been a jade bowl from a wealthy family a thousand years ago that fell into the mud. Over time, the mud dried out and turned into stone, thus enclosing the bowl within." #### Melon Vine Miracle Tao Fangbo, a high-ranking official in Jiangning, was friendly with Pu and Liu. On the Mid-Autumn Festival, he invited the two to drink wine together, and they each held a handful of melon seeds while walking and talking on the steps below. As they walked and talked, the wind blew several seeds onto the ground. In the summer, the spot where the seeds fell suddenly sprouted melon vines, which grew larger and larger until they produced three melons, each as big as a winnowing basket. People came to congratulate them, seeing this as an auspicious sign. The three men felt pleased upon hearing this. However, within a year, Tao was punished for his desk; Pu died of consumption; and Liu suffered from a severe case of leprosy, leading to his death due to the condition. #### Zither Transformation Wu Guanxing, a skilled zither player in Jinling, often told me: "The zither is the refined music of ancient kings, but it is just words, and I did not believe it." At the age of fifty, he was forced by General Zhao to play the tune "Ji Sheng Cao" (Parasitic Grass). While he played, court musicians sang licentious songs to accompany him. Suddenly, there was a loud sound of wind and thunder, and all seven strings of the zither broke. Looking up at the clear sky, there were no clouds. The entire household of General Zhao was shocked. From then on, whenever he played the zither for officials, he would burn incense and wash his hands, and he would only play ancient tunes.

### Translation **○古北口城楼火箭匣乾隆六年，嘉兴知府杨景震为卢案谪戍军台。登古北口城楼，上有一铜匣，封锁甚固，相传明代总兵戚继光所留，过客不许开看。杨抚玩良久，见匣上金镌一震卦，笑曰：“匣上卦名震，与我名景震相应，我当开之。”启其盖，飞出火箭一枝，着于对面景德庙正殿柱上，登时火起，将殿宇僧房焚烧殆尽。** In the sixth year of the Qianlong reign, Yang Jingzhen, the magistrate of Jiaxing, was exiled to a military post due to the Lu case. Ascending the city gate tower of Gubeikou, he noticed a copper box tightly sealed, with a golden engraving of the "Zhen" hexagram. It was said that the box was left by General Qi Jiguang of the Ming dynasty, and visitors were forbidden to open it. After examining it for a long time, Yang noticed the hexagram's name matched his own, and he laughed, saying, "The hexagram on the box is 'Zhen,' which corresponds to my name, Jingzhen. I shall open it." When he opened the lid, a rocket flew out and landed on the main hall pillar of the adjacent Jingde Temple, immediately causing a fire that burned down the temple buildings and monks' quarters almost completely. **○官受妓嗔杨镜村作苏州太守，娼禁甚宽；某太守治苏州，笞妓甚酷。后两人俱解组矣，偶过江都，有巨公某延之饮酒。座有三妓，皆苏人也，二人戏问：“苏州官长贤否？”三人但认识杨公，不认识某公，齐声对曰：“杨太老爷待奴辈仁慈，并禁地方衙役光棍吓诈，此等官府，自然公侯万代。后来某大老爷拿奴辈去，非笞即拶，并教供出嫖客姓名，以便他吓诈取钱，不供便打。如此等官，世世子孙要做奴辈这行生意的。”举座大笑某公不终席登车而去。** Governor Yang Jingcun of Suzhou was known for his lenient treatment of courtesans, while another governor was notorious for his severe beatings of courtesans. After both were relieved of their offices, they chanced to pass through Jiangdu, where a prominent official invited them to drink. Among the guests were three courtesans from Suzhou. Two of them jokingly asked, "How were the governors of Suzhou?" The three courtesans knew only of Yang, not the other governor, and they replied in unison, "Governor Yang treated us kindly and forbade local officials and ruffians from intimidating and extorting. Such officials will surely have descendants who are nobles for generations. Later, when the other governor took us in, he either beat or tortured us, and forced us to reveal the names of our patrons so he could extort money. If we refused, he would beat us. Such officials, their descendants will forever be in this line of work." Everyone at the table laughed, and the prominent official left without finishing his meal.

In Beijing, the wedding customs differ from those in the south. When Shao Youfang married, friends from the south came to congratulate him, intending to tease the newlyweds and pay their respects. Unexpectedly, when the bridal sedan chair arrived, it went directly into the inner chamber. The groom emerged, drawing his bow, and fired three loud arrows at the sedan chair's curtain. He then carried the bride out of the chair, who had disheveled hair and was wrapped in red silk. The groom removed the red scarf using a scale, without performing the traditional bowing ceremony, and sat facing each other on the bed. Two attendants covered all the windows and the entire room with red mats. They presented a large dumpling, which was cut open to reveal many smaller dumplings inside. The newlyweds drank and ate the dumplings, then removed their clothes and slept together. The next day, at dawn, the father-in-law lit a candle and performed a ritual to worship heaven, earth, the kitchen god, and the ancestral temple. After five days, a banquet was held for guests. On the day of the wedding, the guests received no tea or alcohol and left feeling hungry and thirsty. Someone mocked the situation, saying, "In Beijing, the wedding is very different. The bridal sedan chair is carried directly into the bridal chamber. A hard bow is drawn, and three arrows are fired at the face first. A large steamed dumpling is served, followed by a cup of wine. The red scarf is removed with a scale, and no bowing is done. The room is covered with red mats, blocking the air. The next morning, the father-in-law bows to heaven, earth, ancestors, and the kitchen god, causing his knees to ache."

During the Kangxi era, the river commissioner Zhao Shixian and the local river official Zhang Hao engaged in a competition of wealth. Zhang invited Zhao to a drinking party, where six thousand lanterns were hung in the trees, arranged high and low, resembling the scattered Milky Way. Three hundred soldiers were tasked with lighting candles and trimming wicks, creating a noisy and chaotic scene, which people considered impressive. After half a month, Zhao returned the invitation and added ten thousand more lanterns to the display, yet only about ten to twenty people were needed to light and maintain them. The atmosphere was solemn, and people doubted that such a display could be practically managed. When Zhao ordered the lanterns to be lit, there was a sudden sound, and all ten thousand lanterns lit up instantly, without the need for trimming the wicks, and they burned brightly throughout the night. Zhang was deeply ashamed but did not understand the reason behind it. He bribed Zhao's servant heavily and learned that Zhao used firecracker strings threaded through the wicks of the candles, arranged continuously, with each string passing through a hundred lanterns. When one string was lit, a hundred lanterns would light up instantly. Zhao used light silk as the wicks, each candle being half an inch long, with extremely small firecrackers hidden inside. When the firecrackers exploded, the candle wax flew away, and the candles lit up without the need for trimming. Meanwhile, the salt merchant An Luchun invited Zhao to a drinking party, with lanterns and decorations like clouds visible ten miles away. Upon arriving at his home, the east and west rooms were filled with countless precious and rare antiques, but Zhao looked at them as if they were nothing. It was only when the wine was flowing freely and the banquet was over that Zhao entered a small room for a private conversation. Two beautiful women presented a double brocade box, calling it "little trinkets." When Zhao opened the box, two live black minks from the northeast region jumped out, bowing and asking Zhao. Zhao finally laughed and said, "You've gone to a lot of trouble today."

During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, Zhu Ermei deceived people with evil arts and was known as a god or immortal, earning fame in the capital city. Kings and dukes all lowered their status to treat him humbly, except at the door of Xiong Wen-zhen Gong, where he finally failed to gain entry. One day, Zhu Ermei went to the gatekeeper and said, "What clothes is the magistrate wearing today, what dishes is he eating, and where is he sitting? I know all of these." The gatekeeper found his words to be accurate and reported to the magistrate. The magistrate smiled and said, "If Zhu Ermei's measurements of me are indeed accurate in every detail, he can be called a god. But I have the five words 'I do not like to see evil people' in my heart, which he is completely unaware of; can he be called a god?" The gatekeeper told Zhu Ermei, who felt ashamed and left. It is said that Zhu Ermei fought magic with Zhang the Immortal. He threw his cup of tea into the air, and it seemed to be held by someone, so it did not fall. Zhang smiled without saying anything. Zhu Ermei had a look of self-congratulation and sneered at Zhang for not being able to perform this trick. Zhang said, "I am not unable to do it, but I am concerned about breaking your method, so I did not do it." Zhu Ermei insisted, and Zhang, reluctantly, also threw a cup. Then Zhang's cup stayed in the air, while Zhu Ermei's cup fell. Someone asked the Immortal, who replied, "He relies on a fox spirit; I command the Five Thunder Gods. When the gods rise into the air, the fox spirit flees." Not long after, Zhu Ermei was defeated.

梁制府梁构亭总督直隶，自言五岁时，有外祖母杨氏无所依倚，就养女家，得奇疾，卧床能将缎被寸寸裂之，亦不知其指力之勇从何来也。一日召梁太夫人曰：“外孙二官以后切不许其立床边，他浑身上火，近之将人炙痛。现在我跟前某姑某舅，人虽物故，而于我有情，时来与我淡笑，一见二官到，无不爬升屋而逃者，使我心大不安。”梁太夫人即手麾公出。公不敢再入，时于窗缝中窥探，杨已知觉，蹙额曰：“二官这小儿又来作闹了，速赶他去。”如其言，杨始安寝。亡何，杨病重气绝矣，良久复苏，张目谓梁太夫人曰：“我魂灵要出去，汝家灶神、门神一齐拦柱大门，说我在是梁氏之人，不许我出去，奈何？”梁夫人曰：“当速请高僧来诵经，为母亲忏悔求请，何如？”杨曰：“不知仍教二官来向二神一说，神必首肯也。”太夫人即率公往门灶前代为通说，顷刻间，杨瞑目逝矣。 Translation: Governor-General Liang Guoting of Zhili, also known as Liang Gouting, recounted that when he was five years old, his maternal grandmother, Lady Yang, who had no one to rely on, lived with her daughter's family. She suffered from a strange illness, lying in bed and tearing the silk quilt into small pieces, and it was unknown where her extraordinary finger strength came from. One day, she summoned Lady Liang, saying, "From now on, do not allow my grandson Er Guan to stand by my bedside. His entire body is burning hot, and being near him will scorch people. Now, certain aunts and uncles who are deceased but still have feelings for me, sometimes come to visit and smile gently at me. But as soon as they see Er Guan, they all climb up onto the roof and run away, making me very uneasy." Lady Liang then waved him away with her hand. Er Guan dared not enter again and peeked through the window cracks. Lady Yang had already noticed and frowned, saying, "This little boy Er Guan is causing trouble again; quickly send him away." Following her words, Lady Yang began to rest peacefully. Soon after, she fell gravely ill and nearly died, but after a long time, she revived and opened her eyes, telling Lady Liang, "My soul wants to leave. Your family's stove god and door god are blocking the main door, saying I am a member of the Liang family and must not leave. What should I do?" Lady Liang replied, "We should quickly invite a high-ranking monk to recite scriptures for your mother's repentance and prayers. What do you think?" Lady Yang said, "Perhaps you should still have Er Guan come and speak to the two gods, and they will surely agree." Lady Liang then took Er Guan to the stove and door gods to relay the message. In a moment, Lady Yang closed her eyes and passed away. --- This translation aims to be faithful to the original text while ensuring it reads naturally in English, maintaining the cultural and historical context.

During his time as the magistrate of Liangxiang, the official suffered from a severe malaria. One night, he had a dream where the city god of his own district invited him to meet. The god said, "I was also a county official in this place before. The Supreme Deity, because I was an upright official, appointed me as the city god. The illness you are suffering from is the same as the one I had before. Later, after taking a certain medicine, I recovered. I will now give you the prescription." He told the names of several herbs in the medicine, bowed, and then left. The next day, the official reported the prescription, and indeed, it cured him after two doses. Checking the records of Liangxiang, it was found that there was such a person. Later, when he served as the magistrate of Xianghe, an old man brought his daughter to lodge a complaint. The daughter was quite beautiful. The official asked, "What is your complaint?" The old man replied, "My daughter is possessed by the city god. Every night, the god comes in a carriage to take her, and she becomes dazed and unconscious. He only lets her return home at the third watch of the next day. My daughter is already betrothed to a certain family, but that family dares not come to marry her." The official said, "I can govern the people, but not the gods." The old man said, "My daughter says that you came to the city god's temple to offer incense. The god, seeing you, would certainly come out to greet you. When you bow to the god, the god will avoid his seat to return your courtesy. The god respects you so much, if you speak a word, perhaps the god will listen." The official secretly felt pleased and confident, and immediately wrote a document and gave it to the old man to burn and throw it. The next day, the old man indeed came with his daughter to thank him, saying, "The god did not come to take my daughter last night."

Two Stories of Official Fortune Huayong served as the magistrate of Huaining. One day, a high-ranking official from Guangdong was on his way to pass through Huayong's jurisdiction. Huayong sent his capable servant, Zhang Rong, to prepare the official residence. Zhang Rong, a diligent and experienced servant, managed everything thoroughly, costing about a hundred gold coins. However, the high-ranking official was then ordered by the emperor to go to another place to preside over a case, so he did not come as planned. Zhang Rong was in a state of uncertainty when, coincidentally, Governor A Gong Sihua of Jiangxi Province was on his way to the capital. The route passed through this place, and Zhang Rong, acting on behalf of his master, presented an official document to greet him and informed him that the official residence was ready. Governor A Gong Sihua was greatly surprised, thinking that he had never met Huayong before and that Huayong was not one of his subordinates, so why such elaborate preparations? Upon entering the residence, he saw it decorated with colorful decorations and lanterns, with livestock and servants all present, which delighted him immensely. He then called Zhang Rong and said to him, "I am a disgraced person, and along the way, people have been cold and distant. Even the officials I once promoted treat me with icy indifference. How could your master show such ancient and noble sentiments? I should return your master's official document, but now, out of deep gratitude, I am afraid I might forget your master's name, so I will temporarily keep the document to have a chance to repay him in the future." After giving his instructions, Governor A Gong Sihua personally wrote a letter to Huayong, expressing his gratitude three times, and then rode away. Zhang Rong returned and informed his master about the events. The master criticized him for being overly involved, and a close advisor smiled and said, "This servant handles official matters with great care. Without such a display, how could he settle his accounts?" The master smiled and nodded in agreement.

Within two years, the governor (阿公) was reappointed to the position of governor of Shanxi. Huá Sì (华四), upon completing his term of service, was sent to the Ministry for an audience with the emperor and was then appointed to Shanxi by imperial decree. On his first visit to the governor's office to pay respects, the governor was overjoyed, as if he had found a treasure. He sent a servant to convey a message to the provincial officials: "Please ask the governor to delay the audience; my master's benefactor has arrived." He immediately opened the central gate and personally welcomed Huá Sì to the hall, calling him "old brother" and leading him inside. The governor arranged wine and food and treated him as a guest of honor. Huá Sì knelt and declined, fearful and unable to accept. The governor said, "To not repay a favor, what kind of person am I? Today I will do my best; tomorrow you will follow your own customs." They drank heartily, and the governor saw him off in a sedan chair. The provincial officials, upon hearing this, all looked at him with new respect.

Within half a year, he was promoted to the position of Tongpan (Deputy Governor); another half year later, he was promoted to Tongzhi (Associate Governor); he was then promoted again to the position of Zhi Fu (Governor) of Nan'an Prefecture. Uncle was transferred to Henan, and Hua also requested to return home to care for his parents, returning with great success. Zhang Rong was rewarded with 2,000 taels of gold, and Zhang also achieved a comfortable life. Fu Siye, a capable official in the Ministry of Personnel, was highly valued by Guoyi Gong Neqin when he was in charge of the Ministry of Personnel. Whenever other officials in the Ministry of Personnel could not resolve certain matters, they would call Fu Siye, who would resolve them in a few words, and Neqin highly valued him. According to tradition, when recommending candidates for the position of Langzhong (a high-ranking official), there were two types of recommendations: one for the main position and one for the deputy position. When a vacancy for a Langzhong in the Ministry of Revenue occurred, Neqin recommended him, and he was introduced to the Bright Hall. Upon entering the hall, Fu Siye immediately knelt, and the emperor found him to be foolish, so the deputy recommendation was used instead. After more than a year, a vacancy for a Langzhong in the Ministry of Personnel occurred, and Neqin recommended him again. Upon entering the hall, Fu Siye again knelt immediately, and the emperor was displeased, saying to Neqin, "How can you recommend such a foolish person?" Neqin replied, "Fu Siye is good at his work, which is why I have recommended him repeatedly. I did not expect him to be unfamiliar with court etiquette, which must be due to bad luck." The emperor's displeasure was somewhat alleviated.

Not long after, there was another recommendation for an audience. As he was about to enter the court, Nè admonished him, saying, "You have lost etiquette twice before. This time, be very careful and do not repeat the same mistake, which would bring shame to me." Fù nodded in agreement. When they reached the audience, all officials recited their resumes, but there was no mention of him, and Nè could not understand the reason. It was not until after the audience, when they reached the area outside the main gate, that Nè saw Fù with a swollen face, staggering and weeping. Nè asked him why, and Fù replied, "The officials stopped me twice at the entrance to the hall, where I saw a red-robed official taller than a man, who blocked my way. I had no choice but to kneel. This is the third time. I remembered your instructions and thought that if I saw another red-robed official, I would charge in directly and not be stopped. Unexpectedly, this person blocked me again in the hall; as I charged forward, he slapped me across the face, picked me up, and threw me, so I fell down the steps outside the hall, injuring my face. I cannot look upon the emperor's face, nor do I know what kind of karmic debt I incurred in a past life! Knowing I have thin fortune, I beg you not to recommend me again in the future." Nè had no choice but to accept this. All the officials heard this and were greatly surprised. They sent someone to carry him to the carriage and sent him back to his home. Soon after, he fell ill and died four days later.

The Acting Magistrate of Suining County, Qian, was once called upon to investigate a case. According to local custom, when a person was found dead on the road, the landowner had to pay 8,000 coins to the authorities to close the case. One day, a village reported that someone had drowned in a river. The official informed Qian about this precedent. Qian went to examine the body and found no injuries, so he ordered it to be buried immediately. After returning to his office, the official presented the customary payment of 8,000 coins from the landowner. Qian was about to accept it when he noticed the coins were tied with a red string, which looked very auspicious. He asked the official why the coins were tied with a red string. The official explained that the family was poor and unable to afford the payment, so they had been forced to sell one of their daughters to a villager as a concubine for 24,000 coins. The red string was used because it was considered a lucky sign. Qian realized that the money had been obtained under duress and felt it would be wrong to accept it. He summoned the villagers and questioned them, and they admitted the truth. He then called the man who had bought the girl and told him, "I received money from you, but you pressured the family to sell their daughter, which is unkind; and you took advantage of their desperation to buy her, which is unjust. I will not accept this money, and you must return the girl to her family immediately." The man agreed. Qian then asked the seller if there was any remaining money. The seller replied that it had all been spent by the officials in gambling. Qian ordered the officials to retrieve the money, but it had already been gambled away and distributed. Qian then turned to the man who had bought the girl and said, "I will compensate you with the original amount." He ordered the payment to be made and instructed the villagers to take the girl back home. The case was thus resolved.

Shortly after, Qian contracted a back abscess and fell unconscious in bed. In a dream, a man in green clothes summoned him to a place where the halls were grand and imposing. The supreme ruler seated on the throne said to Qian, "Your allotted time has come to an end, but fortunately, you have done a good deed that is sufficient to offset your fate. Do you know this?" Qian was confused and did not understand. The ruler ordered the judge to check the records, and it showed an incident from a certain year when he had saved a girl from being sold. The judge reported, "This good deed is very meritorious. According to the rules, it can extend your life by a decade and elevate your rank to the fifth grade." The ruler nodded in agreement and then sent the man in green clothes to revive Qian, and the abscess suddenly healed. From then on, Qian dedicated himself to doing good deeds. He donated funds for all charitable activities, such as providing food for the hungry and burying the dead. He was indeed promoted to the position of deputy magistrate, but after a decade, the abscess reappeared. His family prepared for his funeral arrangements, but they were still hesitant. They comforted Qian by saying, "You once did a good deed, so your life can still be extended. In recent years, you have done many good deeds. How can we be sure that the underworld will not grant you additional years?" Qian smiled and replied, "Not so. The good deeds of the past were done without any ulterior motive, so the underworld values me. The good deeds of today are done with a specific purpose, and I fear the underworld does not value me as much. This time, my fate has come to an end, and it is impossible to escape. Perhaps doing good with a heart of goodness is different from doing evil with a heart of malice. Perhaps there will be a reward in a future life." A few days later, the abscess burst, and he died.

### Translation of the Source Text In the seventh volume, concerning the divination of immortals, during the spring of the year Qianlong's third lunar cycle (1736), Yang Kouchu from Qiuchuan and Xu Cangxin from Jinling performed a divination ritual. A female immortal descended to the altar, reciting poetry: "Where can I find the old jade hairpin again? The sound of waves is like a dream, and resentment is like smoke. Once gone to the Springs of the Dead for more than a thousand years, it is only worth a half-day's sleep of longing. I am Wang Xiaojun, and I have met someone with whom I have a karmic connection. I wish to speak with him, so please do not be afraid." A friend named Meng, present at the altar, saw the words as being too sensuous and feared the invocation of evil spirits, intending to burn a retreat talisman. The divining instrument then wrote: "Since we have already summoned you to come, how can we dismiss you so easily! In the past, I had beautiful features, and you had talent and charm. We met under the great dike and wandered together in the shade of fine willows. Our pair was just forming when we were suddenly separated; like pearls sinking and jade falling, like orchids breaking and lilies withering. You have repeatedly taken on human form, while I remain a lingering soul in the waters. Now I am preparing to serve as an attendant to the wave god, and I was just summoned by a talisman. Across lifetimes, we have met an intimate friendship, not forgetting a single pure heart; though there is the obstacle of mountains and rivers, who knows the thousands of rows of red tears! Resentment is the difference between being visible and hidden, but it is fortunate that sincerity and faith are easy to come together. The window is bright and the wind and dew are cold; I will seek you after the Big Dipper has turned and the stars are aligned. In the quiet room, when the rain and clouds come, I will find you during beautiful dreams and deep sleep. Not calling out names, I fear that suspicion and fear might arise; only continuing the emotional bond, how dare one cause harm?" That night, Xu Cangxin indeed dreamed of a woman holding a round fan, extremely beautiful, and they engaged in intimate conversation, experiencing the utmost pleasure. The next day, he returned to the altar, and they lingered until dawn.

The next day, during another mediumistic session, the following poems were delivered: "Red clouds with wind and rain like dust, the wind carries a spirit through the air. Please try to smell the plum blossoms, the fragrant trees will remain young forever." Another poem read: "Dew-covered peach blossoms and wind-blown willows, last night we held hands. Eternal longing, did you know that Green Qīng has returned?" The session ended with the message "Take care" and no further sessions occurred thereafter. Regarding a certain descendant of the Mingjing, the great-grandson of the High-ranking Official Gāo Niàndōng from Zīchuān, he recounted that after his wedding, he suffered from a dizzy spell that caused him to fall unconscious. Several days later, he began to hear sounds as if someone was saying "Lèlè." After another few days, he saw a small figure, resembling a child about a foot tall. From then on, he became increasingly thin and weak, unable to get out of bed. His family believed it was an evil spirit and invited a magician to exorcise it, but it was ineffective. They then secretly placed a sword at the foot of his bed. When he was awake, he would often see the small figure darting from the foot of the bed to the table and disappearing. They then placed a copper basin of water under the table. One day, while he was just waking up from a nap, he saw a child approach. He swung the sword at it, and it fell into the water with a sharp sound. His family found a wooden puppet child in the basin, dressed in a red robe, with a red thread around its neck, and its hands were pulled into a strangling position. They destroyed the puppet, and the evil spirit was finally expelled. It was later said that a local craftsman died on the same day. It is believed that the craftsman had been dissatisfied with the repairs made to the house when the descendant of the Mingjing entered his wife's family home. The craftsman had used a magical technique to cause the illness, but when the technique was broken, the craftsman died. However, from then on, the descendant of the Mingjing remained sickly and weak, unable to walk.

The Scholar's Garden Once, when the moon was high, the scholar, accompanied by a young servant, went to his garden pavilion. Upon arriving, he immediately sent the servant to fetch the tea set from the inner chamber. Nearby lived a girl, now of marriageable age and beautiful, whom the scholar had known before. When the servant left, the girl climbed over the wall and peered in, holding a tea bowl. She descended gracefully, as if floating down from the wall. She entered the pavilion, placed the tea bowl on a table, and said to the scholar, "Knowing you are thirsty, I wish to offer you this." The scholar was suspicious of her unusual appearance and, still recovering from a previous illness, urged her to leave. The girl replied, "If you accept this, I will depart." Shortly after, the young servant returned, and the girl vanished. The scholar turned back to see that the tea bowl on the table now contained only a mulberry leaf holding a pinch of soil. From then on, whenever the curtains swayed gently in the stillness of the day or the moon shone brightly at night, the girl would appear. She spoke with great wisdom, and the scholar, though still recovering from his illness, managed to keep his composure. The girl did not attempt to be overly familiar or intimate, and her appearance and demeanor could change at will—she could be tall or short, plump or slender, all within a single day. At first, the scholar was suspicious of her, but over time, he grew fond of her, considering her a friend for conversation. He no longer questioned her origins. Her presence was invisible to others, but when she appeared, everyone in the room felt an eerie, chilling atmosphere.

**Translation:** One day, the scholar Mingjing dreamed of being intimate with a woman. Upon waking, he realized it was a woman. Mingjing knew it was a trick of her art. However, he wanted to keep her, but the woman quickly put on her clothes, got off the bed, and laughed loudly before leaving. He pulled her clothes, and they rustled like paper. Later, Mingjing learned the method of guidance, and the woman then disappeared completely. **○雷击两妇活一儿安东县村中一妇产子，唤稳婆接生，留宿一夜而去。其夫某自外归，抱子甚喜，欲祀神偿愿。忽探摸其枕惊曰：“我暗藏银四锭在内，无一人知道，如何失去？”妻怪而问之，因谓昨日收生婆睡此枕，可疑也。某即往问索银，许以一半为谢，一半偿还作酬神之用。稳婆勃然大怒，且骂且咒曰：“我为汝家接生，乃冤我为贼，是儿必死。若盗汝银，天雷打死！”骂之不已。某反疑其妇有别情，亦不敢索银。** In the village of Andong County, a woman gave birth to a child and called a midwife to assist. The midwife stayed for the night and then left. The husband, returning from outside, held the child and was very happy, wanting to offer sacrifices to the gods to fulfill a vow. Suddenly, he touched the pillow and exclaimed in surprise: "I had secretly hidden four ingots of silver in it, and no one knew. How could it have been lost?" His wife, puzzled, asked him about it, and he said that the midwife had slept on the pillow the previous day, which seemed suspicious. The husband then went to the midwife to demand the silver, offering half as thanks and half as repayment for the sacrifice to the gods. The midwife became extremely angry and cursed, saying: "I came to your house to deliver your child, and you accuse me of being a thief. This child will surely die. If I stole your silver, may I be struck down by lightning!" She continued to curse him. The husband then began to suspect that his wife had another lover and dared not demand the silver.

Three days after the child was born, the couple asked a midwife to wash the child. On that day, the midwife did not come and instead sent her daughter. At night, the child indeed died suddenly. The couple wept together, placed the child in a wooden box, and buried it in an empty plot of land. Everyone said, "The midwife's prediction was proven true." Suddenly, there was a great thunderstorm, and people far and near heard a loud clap of thunder. The whole village smelled sulfur, and everyone followed the scent. They saw two women kneeling on the empty plot, both burned by lightning, each holding two silver ingots in their hands. And the child they had buried was already crawling out of the ground, crying loudly. The neighbors ran to inform the family who had buried the child to come and recognize it. They saw that the child's navel had a needle tip sticking out. They pulled out the needle, and blood flowed, but the child remained unharmed. The two women killed by lightning were the midwife who had stolen the silver and her daughter. They had secretly stabbed the child's navel with a needle during the washing ritual, causing its death. They did this to fulfill their curse. Those who saw it were all shocked and frightened. This happened in June of the 57th year of the Qianlong era. ○Firespirit Strikes Wu Yang, courtesy name Nan Gu, was from the Ma Ji Mountain in Piling. When he was humble, he was a guest teacher at a certain residence. The family was building a new house, and the carpenters stored wood shavings in a basin to hide fire for cooking. One night, in the middle of the night, Nan Gu heard a sound at the corner of the house. He got up and looked, and saw a red-faced person blowing towards the fire. Nan Gu scolded him, and the person replied, "I am sent by the Fire God, and this house will be burned today." Nan Gu said, "I am here, how can it be?" The person nodded and left. A few days later, Nan Gu was about to leave the residence and warned the owner to be on alert. On that day, when Nan Gu returned, the house was indeed burned. Later, Nan Gu passed the imperial examination in the Dingwei year of the Wanli era and served up to the position of Lübo.

A Man Who Killed His Aunt and Four Others Paid with Their Lives The magistrate of Jianping, Zhou Jun, had a cousin who related: Two brothers each married and had a son. After their parents' death, they left behind a weak sister whom they could not care for, and the two wives particularly mistreated her. The sister was already betrothed to the son of a scholar, but he was too poor to marry her, so she moved in with them. The two wives often talked to each other, saying, "One aunt is already a burden, now there's another mouth to feed, what shall we do? We will eventually find a way to get rid of her." Coincidentally, the brothers were studying at a temple outside the city, and the sister's fiancé also went to visit his relatives. The two wives both used an excuse to return home, and locked up all the firewood, rice, and food before leaving. The next day, the aunt entered the kitchen, unable to cook. She endured hunger for two days, too ashamed to ask for help, and after much deliberation, she had no choice but to hang herself. The two wives then returned, called their husbands, and lied that she died of illness. They hastily buried her and sent a letter to her husband's family to take the coffin away, thinking they had gotten rid of her. However, in the room, they often heard ghostly cries. After several months, the older wife and her son suddenly fell ill and died. Not long after, the younger wife's son also fell ill, terrified, she asked her husband to guard him around the clock. At two drums at night, suddenly a cold wind blew, the door curtain suddenly opened, and they saw a soldier with red hair and blue face, long teeth, and a steel fork, who grabbed the child and took him away. They chased after him, seeing the child still moving naked, but suddenly he disappeared. They looked back at the bed, and found the child dead, while the wife was still moaning. At dawn, the wife also died.

The gentleman, witnessing the death of his wife and children, was filled with deep regret and often told others to warn them. The crime of killing one aunt leads to four people compensating for it; how severe! The crime of secretly causing death is very serious! ○ Mistakenly Killing the Golden Child When the Viceroy Yan was sent on a mission to Wulin, during his leisure time, he wished to paint a small portrait. The magistrate of Yin County, Mr. Qian, invited Mr. Miao from Jiyang to visit together, to paint the gentleman's portrait, which was very lifelike. The gentleman was pleased and entrusted Mr. Qian to complete the painting. Mr. Qian, knowing that the gentleman often talked about Buddhism, painted him wearing a red robe sitting cross-legged in a mountain cave. The gentleman saw it and was greatly pleased, saying, "This is my past life!" Mr. Qian asked why, and the gentleman said, "Formerly, I was the commander in Yunnan. Just then, Prince Consort Sertebetzenburen was gravely ill, died and then revived. He urged his attendants to invite me to his bedside and said, 'Just now, I arrived at a mountain, with tall pines reaching the sky, and greenery all around. There is a stone cave inside, with several ancient arhats lined up. Beside it, there are mats, leaving one seat empty. One arhat pointed out and said, "This is the former residence of A-someone. Because of mistakenly killing a golden child, he was exiled to the human world. If one can resolve not to kill wantonly and has the means to save lives, then one can return to their position. I will convey this message to you." Upon lifting the mat to look, it was suddenly a child's skeleton. You must take good care of yourself.' The prince consort finished speaking and then passed away. Now, your painting perfectly matches the previous signs; is this not fate!" The gentleman took this painting back to the capital. It was nearly sung by all the prominent officials, and Mr. Miao thus gained fame for his skill in capturing the spirit.

Mr. Qian Meigu, from Piling (modern-day Changzhou, Jiangsu), whose given name was Qian Chun, served as the Minister of Revenue in the capital during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen of the Ming Dynasty. As a young child, he contracted smallpox, which was extremely severe, and he was on the brink of death. His father, Mr. Qixin, who cherished his only son, held him in his arms, unwilling to abandon him, and was walking around the steps. Suddenly, he heard a loud voice from the sky, sternly commanding, "Who caused the smallpox for the Minister of Revenue Qian? He should be lashed twenty times! Quickly send a mild form of smallpox instead." Then, he heard the sound of the tiles, like beans being scattered. Looking at the child in his arms, he had already recovered. As a young adult, he often slept on the upper floor. During the summer, he happened to stay elsewhere, and a servant secretly lay on his bed. He vaguely heard a loud voice saying, "Despicable! Despicable! Who are you to dare lie on this bed!" The servant felt uneasy and quickly got up, only to find the bed moved to a dark corner, no longer the place where he was lying. After this, the servant was very afraid of Mr. Qian Meigu and always knelt and reported matters respectfully.

### Translation ○ Dreaming of Ink, Wenmin Gong Qian from Wujin, in the year Wuwu, was preparing to take the Shuntian examination. Before the exam, he dreamed of arriving at the area outside Zhengyang Gate, where he saw a man with a dignified appearance, setting up a tent and displaying several pieces of ink underneath. First, there was a bearded man buying ink, and Gong also bought some. The ink seller looked at Gong closely and gave him two pieces of ink, then gave one piece to the bearded man. He then woke up. Later, when he met his examiner, Sun Wendeng Gong, he realized that the ink seller was indeed Sun. Next, a colleague came to pay respects, and the bearded man appeared, who turned out to be Li Shimeng from Wuxi. It seems that the two pieces of ink represented two successful exams, and Li ended his career as the governor of Changping Prefecture. ○ After the Yichou imperial examination, there was someone in the capital who dreamed of reading the heavenly list and saw the name of the forty-first candidate written in gold-embossed script as "Ji Gui," with a small yellow umbrella placed above it. When he woke up, he only remembered the surname Ji but forgot the name, thinking that the person must be of Manchu descent and that there must be something unusual about him. When the list was released, the forty-first candidate was indeed Wenmin Gong Qian, who was soon appointed as a member of the Hanlin Academy. The person found this puzzling. One day, during a banquet, they discussed this matter, and Tang Taishi Dashen was present. He laughed and said, "Qian Zeng, the top-ranked candidate, has the nickname Ji Gui. What is there to doubt?" Everyone then suddenly understood.

A Married Woman's Encounter with a Strange Phenomenon in Qingjian County, Shaanxi In a certain village in Qingjian County, Shaanxi, a woman was returning to her parents' home for a visit. Her father accompanied her back. Midway, they traversed a mountain path, and suddenly a fierce wind arose, causing her clothes to be completely blown away, leaving her naked. With no other choice, her father removed his own clothes to wrap her and supported her as they continued. By evening, they arrived at her husband's home. The husband, noticing the unusual state of his wife, questioned her, and the father explained the incident. The husband, angry and suspicious, exclaimed, "What kind of evil spirit is this? I will surely hunt it down with a spear tomorrow!" They then went to bed. At dawn, the woman suddenly cried out in shock, and her husband was found to have no head. Her family then reported the matter to the authorities. The county magistrate, Mr. Dai, questioned the woman, suspecting that she might have had an affair and killed her husband. He interrogated her, but she steadfastly denied any wrongdoing. The father, on his knees, wept and recounted the entire story. The magistrate, personally leading the officials, ordered them to take her to the spot where she had lost her clothes and conducted a thorough search. They discovered a deep cave on the side of the mountain. The magistrate offered a reward for anyone who could explore the cave, and a strong soldier volunteered. Equipped with a torch, he entered the cave, traveling for dozens of paces before suddenly seeing a bright light. Inside, he encountered a fierce-looking monk lying motionless on a dirt bed. Frightened, the soldier returned and reported to the magistrate. The magistrate then sent several more strong officials, armed with ropes and weapons, to enter the cave. By the time they arrived, the monk had awakened. The officials rushed forward and quickly bound him, dragging him out to see the magistrate. The magistrate questioned him repeatedly, but the monk remained silent. Even when slapped across the face, he did not speak. Not knowing what else to do, the magistrate ordered him to be chained with several loops of chain and supervised the officials to escort him into the city, intending to imprison him in the jail. After traveling about a mile, a sudden fierce wind arose, blinding everyone. Shortly after, the monk and several of the escorting officials vanished without a trace. The matter was then dropped. Mr. Dai, whose name was Shusheng, was from Jingxi. His close associates and friends later recounted these strange events to him.

**Dragon Duel** In the month of August in the year 1761 during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor, at the side of Zhaobao Mountain in Zhoushan, the sky suddenly turned dark during the day. Two dragons were fighting, and one was thrown onto the shore. It was as large as several dozen encirclements, resembling the dragons depicted in human art, but with short horns and very long whiskers. Upon falling to the ground, it began to move slowly, but soon died. The stench could be smelled for a mile, and the villagers competed to take pieces of it. One of its vertebrae was just right for a mortar. Someone who got its jaw sold it for twenty strings of cash. **Silkworm Incident** In May of the year 1762 during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor, in Yixing, Jiangsu Province, near the Dongcang Bridge, several miles from the city, a woman from a certain village whose son had smallpox. The doctor prescribed medicine that required silkworms. Her husband was working outside, and her mother-in-law ordered her to find silkworms. The woman went to the fields to look for them and saw an old mulberry tree with very large silkworms wriggling. She was happy and caught them. After walking a few steps, she suddenly lost the silkworms. The woman told her mother-in-law, "These are live silkworms, they don't have wings to fly, if they fell they must be in the grass, why don't you go look for them." The woman returned to the place to search and found a hole in the gap between the trees. As she was carefully looking, suddenly a huge turtle raised its head, having a human-like head, one arm, and angry eyes, pointing at the woman and speaking human words, "If you disturb me again, I will eat you." The woman was shocked and fell down. Her mother-in-law was surprised that she had not returned and went to see her. She saw her lying on the ground, spitting foam, and pale. She helped her back home, and she gradually recovered, then she described what she had seen. The child eventually died, and the woman soon fell ill, no one knew what strange thing it was.

Feng Xinfa, a clerk in the Shan Yin County library, returned home at night during the winter of the year Xinhai, as his mother was ill. Upon lighting the lamps, he saw Han Shenghua come, and completely forgot that he was dead, conversing with him as if nothing had changed. Han said, "There is an official matter at your brother's house that needs to be handled. The document has been approved and is ready to be executed. It can be dispatched in three days. I will take care of it for you." Feng, who was involved in many affairs, feared being reported and negotiated a bribe with him, promising six thousand coins. Han agreed and left after thanking him. Feng was surprised that Han had died, thinking his mother's illness must be serious, and also wondered if the bribe of six thousand coins might save her. When the three days passed and Han came, he suddenly entered inside, and Feng's mother died. Is it that even in the afterlife, matters are handled like lawsuits in the human world, regardless of winning or losing, always requiring expenses? Or is it that officials who disregard their own relatives in life also do not care about them in death?

The Old Man of Gao, a Scholar from Shan Yin, and the Water Spirit Before he achieved success, the father of Gao, a scholar from Shan Yin, made a living as a laborer. One evening, as he was returning home, he encountered a tall ghost standing by the roadside, leaning against someone's house with its waist resting on the eaves. The old man stood and waited for it. The ghost, holding a child in its hand, said to it, "I want to eat you. You should become a ninth-rank official, have three thousand acres of land, a house with nine rafters, and two sons. If I really want to eat you, my heart cannot bear to do so." Then the ghost placed the child on the tiles, turned around to leave, and saw the old man. The old man was drunk and had been standing for a long time, completely without fear. He thought that the ghost would not even eat a child from a moderately well-off family. As long as he did not starve to death, the ghost would not eat him. Why should he fear the ghost? He then said to it, "I have heard that the longest-lived spirits are called Wangliang, who can bestow wealth and nobility. I will beg you to make me rich." The ghost waved its sleeve and ordered the old man to leave. The old man insisted on asking. The ghost pulled out a rope from its sleeve, tied a bamboo pole, and made it look like a weighing device. The old man then asked for a hammer, and the ghost waved its robe and left. The old man returned home and told his wife, who took a ladder and carried the child down. The next day, a man from a village called Feng, who had lost his son, searched everywhere but could not find him. The old man of Gao showed him the child and recounted the ghost's words. The father of Feng then bowed to the old man and called him "father-in-law." Later, Feng indeed became a patrol officer in Shanxi, with land and houses as promised by the water spirit. Gao also became wealthy from this, and his son passed the imperial examinations.

### The Strange Otters of Shanyin A scholar from Shanyin named Shi Han recounted: The waters of Yue are home to many otter monsters. The smaller ones merely splash water to annoy people and hide when driven away, while the older ones can bewitch people like ghosts. My family had such otter monsters in the past; they would harass wealthy individuals who had passed the imperial examinations. Within a hundred years, we encountered them three times, and they could not be driven away, nor did they cause any harm. In the year Ding Hai, I returned to my hometown. At night, as I was about to sleep, I heard a sound like dropping snail shells, with countless small objects scattered on my table and bed. When I lit a candle, there was nothing there. Suspecting that the north window was left unlocked, I locked it, and the strange occurrences gradually calmed down. Twenty years later, in the year Bing Wu, during a period of mourning, I lay on my side. Suddenly, I felt something pressing on my chest. A small hand frequently stroked my head, and its body was very slippery. It murmured obscene words in my ear. In my dream, I saw a young woman with a fair face, about twenty-four or twenty-five years old, wearing a purple silk shirt, a black silk half-sleeve, and a dark blue skirt. She tried to hug me. When I rejected her, she hugged me from behind and continuously tugged my ear and made noise. In my dream, I said to her, "There really are such lustful old women in the world! Twenty years ago, I was still not to be tempted, but can you tempt me today?" I woke up in surprise, hearing the murmuring sound in my ear and the stroking sensation on my head, which still had not completely gone. The creature quickly escaped from the pillow, light and small like a cat. The next day, it came again, and I felt something on my right thigh. In my dream, I saw the same woman, wearing the same clothes, standing a bit farther away and calling out through the railing. I secretly thought, "Yesterday, even when close, she didn't disturb me. Today, standing far away, she dares to tempt me?" I woke up, and the creature jumped off my thigh. The strange occurrences also ceased.

Early winter of the Ding Wei year, I stayed overnight at a new building of the Chen family by the Cangcha Lake mouth. As soon as I extinguished the candle, an object jumped onto the bed. I knew it was neither a ghost nor a thief. If I were to shout loudly, it would frighten the neighbors and just make me the subject of ridicule. I thought of a way to chase it away and remembered the story of Mr. Hang Dazong's "Unclean Record Vajra Mantra." I tried reciting it, and the creature immediately lay down and remained still. At the fifth watch, it jumped off the bed with a sound and left. In the morning, I saw the place where it had lain with the clothes folded up and added on. Because I was a guest, I did not inform the host. A month later, I stayed here again. When I took off my clothes, I remembered the previous incident. I wanted to avoid it but could not. I hugged the quilt and sat, got tired and closed my eyes, and then the creature was already inside the bed. Holding the "Vajra Mantra" slightly relaxed, it would immediately move and want to climb up; when the recitation relaxed, it gradually approached the chest, making a sharp, fine sound like a mouse. Then it spoke in human language, saying, "If you wear the talisman of the True One of the Correct One, I am not afraid, but if your mouth moves, I am very afraid." At the fifth watch, it went out by circling around the feet. That night, I recited the mantra more than a hundred times. The next day, my family was surprised that I had been talking in my sleep for a long time. Since then, the Chen family also had no other strange occurrences.

On the second day of the second month this year, the village schoolmaster Shen Zhaoyuan came to report an otter's mischief. The otter's fur could be counted on his clothes, and he urgently sought help, wanting to resign from his position. I advised him to recite the "Filth-Trace Mantra," but he couldn't recite it suddenly. However, I vaguely remembered a saying from the *Bencao* that bears die when they eat salt and otters die when they drink alcohol. I recalled that Xu Jingfang, a Jinshi of the Dingwei year, had once used this to dispel an otter demon in his schoolhouse. I instructed Mr. Shen to try it. That evening, he placed two carp and a jar of wine on the table. At the second watch, the otter arrived. Mr. Shen was already confused and couldn't make a sound. All he could see was the otter jumping onto the table to drink. The wine jar tilted, and the otter licked the spilled wine with a sound. It ate all the fish. After jumping down, it wanted to climb onto Mr. Shen's bed. Its front feet lifted, but its hind feet did not follow, and it fell to the ground three times. The otter was drunk. It fled away, and now it has completely disappeared. Thus, the knowledge gained from various sources is not to be shunned, as it can also save people. The otter's fondness for alcohol, those who live near water should know about it; and the bear's love for salt, those who live in the mountains must also know about it. ○ A cypress incense hairpin should not be used in a funeral. Chen Sheng, a villager from Kuaiji, married a woman from the Jin family of the county. They had a very close and harmonious marriage. After the woman died, Chen set up an image and offered prayers, facing it morning and evening, as if she were still alive.

After Jin's second aunt fell ill and died, they were preparing for her burial when she suddenly revived. The family was greatly surprised, for it was Jin's eldest aunt who spoke, saying, "I was mistakenly taken by a deity to the underworld. After being questioned and clarified, my soul was released to return. However, during the burial, a cypress-scented pin was used, preventing my soul from re-entering. Now my younger sister's life has ended, so I asked the underworld to borrow her body to return my soul. I will return to Chen." The family was greatly astonished. Jin pointed out the boxes and clothes she had kept during her life, and every single one was accurate. She also recounted intimate and private conversations with Chen in the bedroom, confirming she was indeed Chen's wife. Jin's brother returned from afar, and the girl spoke of the time she had passed through his home, leaving food, dishes, wine, and cups, and the unpleasant smell of meat on the boat where her brother had bought it. All these details matched what her brother had experienced in the past, without any discrepancies. However, his younger sister was already betrothed to a man of a certain surname. The clan suspected that his younger sister might be using ghostly speech to cover up her ambiguous situation, so they did not hastily return her to Chen. Chen also believed that his elder sister's soul was in his younger sister's body and could not bear to bring her back. The man's family insisted on marrying her, and the parents then sent the girl there. As soon as she got off the carriage, she loudly said, "I am Jin's eldest aunt, not the second aunt. I will return to Chen's family, not yours. If you insist on keeping me, it will bring great misfortune, and you will have no regrets." That night, her father-in-law and mother-in-law locked her in the same room with the man. Three days later, the man died suddenly without any illness. Chen became even more afraid to bring her back, and she ended up staying loyal to the man's family. She knew all the good and bad events in the village beforehand, speaking like a witch, and the villagers found her strange. Some people said, "This is a demon possessing her. It is not the true soul of the eldest aunt. Chen did not bring her back, not without reason."

A Hunter's Account of Tigers Rumors have it that tigers harm people because of ghosts who strip the body and feed it to the tiger. It is also said that tigers can perform the ritualistic walk known as the "Yubu," causing the body to rise and strip itself. Both of these claims are unfounded. People do not see tigers, so they make such speculative statements. There was a hunter named Zheng who said: "When a tiger seizes a person, it bites the neck, causing the person to suffer intense pain. The hands and feet naturally stretch out, and the body tilts downward, causing the clothes to slip off. When a person is not in distress and maintains proper decorum, they stand tall and dignified. But when they are in dire straits and have nowhere to turn, they shiver and shrink, and their clothes loosen." When Zheng was young, he once encountered two tigers with a companion. One tiger took his companion away, while the other was hit by Zheng's spear but did not die and escaped. Fearing that the tiger might return, Zheng climbed a tall tree to observe from a distance. He saw that the tiger had first removed his companion's shoes, then his socks, and finally his pants. With clear eyes, Zheng beckoned to his companion to search for him. They found his clothes scattered along the way, and his body was about five miles away, with only the left arm remaining. They found old wounds on the body, confirming it was indeed his companion, and the internal organs were not fully eaten. Another two or three miles away, the tiger that had been hit by the spear lay stiff and dead. It is rumored that tigers bite people on the head during the first half of the month, on the shoulders and back during the middle half, and on the waist and legs during the last half. This is greatly incorrect. Zheng observed that the tiger always bit the shoulders and neck. The tiger strikes with force and throws down from above to bite, and it cannot lift the body without biting the shoulders and neck. There is no difference between the upper and lower halves of the month. Even though the initial part of the body that the tiger eats may not be visible, the remaining parts, such as the hands and feet, show no difference between the upper and lower halves of the month.

Large tigers can weigh up to a thousand pounds, while smaller ones weigh two or three hundred pounds. Combined with their sharp claws and agile leaps, human strength is certainly insufficient to overcome them. What we rely on is human ingenuity, which can counteract the tiger's greed and stupidity. When a tiger's spirit is strong, it often does not die immediately even when shot. Once, Zheng entered a deep mountain, and at a bend in the path, he saw a tiger crouching by the side of the road like a large ox. Zheng was very frightened and did not have time to use his gun. Instead, he shouted loudly to intimidate it with his presence. The tiger indeed jumped away. Zheng judged that it would return and there was no village to hide in, so he first observed where the tiger went and hid in a depression on a slope. The tiger indeed leaped back, hit Zheng with his gun, and jumped away again. Zheng thought that if the tiger came again, it would be difficult to defend against it, so he quickly climbed a tall tree to avoid it. Soon after, the tiger arrived and could not find Zheng. Zheng was in a desperate situation, and his foot accidentally touched a branch, causing it to move. The tiger looked up and saw Zheng, leaped up to attack him, and struck the large branches twice before falling. The tree shook violently, and the leaves rustled. The tiger was severely injured and could no longer jump. It then bit and broke the stones by the roadside until it died. The spirit of a deceased person (伥) must attach itself to an object to act. It can attach to cats, rabbits, chickens, ducks, frogs, or pheasants, and all can make barking sounds. It leads the tiger away by making barking sounds from two or three li ahead, and the tiger follows the sound. The method to control it is as follows: as soon as you hear the spirit, use a nail to pin it to the first tree you encounter. Then strike the object the spirit is attached to, and both the object and the spirit will be destroyed. Some say: nails are made of metal, and trees are made of wood. The soul belongs to wood, and the spirit belongs to metal. This method aligns the spirit with the soul. The spirit is associated with violence and killing, and the伥 is the spirit. By appeasing the spirit and aligning it with the soul, the spirit finds peace and no longer serves the tiger.

The sound of the *cháng* is mournful and long, without a change in tone, but at night, when it is deep and quiet, it can also speak in human language. Zheng once went hunting with his companions, and their boat was moored at the foot of the stream. One night, they heard a knocking sound on the shore, and after a while, someone inside the house responded and wanted to get up. His wife strongly opposed, saying, "It is late at night; it is better to avoid it, do not go and open the door." The knocker became more urgent. The wife, lying down, asked, "Who is the guest?" and replied, "From the next room." "Who is the guest?" she asked again, and it replied, "From the next room." The couple did not get up, but told it to come the next day. The knocking still persisted, and Zheng found it strange. He looked through a crack and saw something like several stone grain bags blocking the door. In the slanted moonlight, he carefully examined it and realized it was a tiger, which was hitting its head against the door. The two words it responded to were *cháng*. Zheng quietly woke up his fellow travelers and told them about it. They were all afraid and hid under the boat. Zheng then shot at the tiger from behind. The tiger was startled and in pain, gnawing through the door and damaging the eaves before leaving. The next day, they saw that where the *cháng* had been kneeling and nodding, there were two depressions. After walking more than two miles, they found a dead tiger in the stream, weighing six hundred pounds. Some say: the tiger was injured and fell into the water, unable to get up.
