Others say: the tiger was hit by a gun and was very hot, so it went to the water to cool off, and died from the wound. When a tiger eats a rabbit, it goes down in one gulp. When a tiger eats a chicken or a pheasant, it bites it up and down a few times, then spits out the remaining feathers like scattered flower rain, covering an area of more than ten feet. The pheasant has five-colored plumage, and when it scatters in flight, it is the most beautiful to see.

Legend has it that tigers are afraid of humans, hence they do not harm drunkards or eat children, but this is not true. Drunkards must be very drunk, stumbling and swaying, which makes it difficult for the tiger to catch them, as they are not steady. As for children, there was a story in Chu Li about two brothers who went out at night to use the toilet. The older brother, aged thirteen or fourteen, was squatting on the toilet, while the younger brother, aged nine, was standing under the eaves. He saw something that looked like a ball of pine needles thrown towards them. The younger brother shrank back and said to his older brother, "What is that?" The older brother replied, "It's just a pine ball." The tiger approached and abandoned the younger brother, seizing the older brother instead. The next day, they followed the blood trail and found the older brother's clothes and shoes scattered everywhere, with dozens of small pine roots pulled up, indicating the marks left by the older brother while being attacked. The bloodstains were wide, suggesting that the tiger had eaten him, and the blood on the grass had been licked up. Tigers also eat vegetables when hungry. In Chu Li, a woman and her sister-in-law were on the second floor, roasting potatoes. They threw the potato skins out the window. The aunt happened to lean out the window and saw the tiger licking up the potato skins. The sister-in-law was alarmed and prepared more potatoes, giving some to the tiger to prevent it from jumping up. The aunt wanted to close the window but hesitated, fearing the tiger might grab her hand. Sitting and waiting, she saw that the sister-in-law's supply of potatoes would soon run out. So, she tried throwing a whole potato to the tiger. The tiger looked up for a long time, saw the thrown object, swallowed it, and then jumped away. Two days later, the tiger was killed, and its claws were found embedded in its chest.

A Traditional Belief About Tigers The traditional belief that tigers do not mate again is incorrect. Tigers live alone, but if there are two, they must be male and female; if there are three, four, or five, they must be a mother tiger and her cubs. When the cubs grow up, the males and females, as well as the mother and her cubs, all fight, and they still live alone. Generally, during a night when the moon is surrounded by a halo, tigers mate, after midnight. The next day, there will definitely be a strong wind. When I was young, I once heard two tigers calling to each other, but I didn't know why. One night, I stayed in a temple tower on a mountain and heard two tigers calling from a great distance; the sound could be heard outside the forest. Peering out, I saw the moon surrounded by a halo, and there was a pile of something, white on top and black on the bottom, like a mound of earth shaking. After a while, the one below roared fiercely, shaking the valley; it seems that when their openings first met, the male was in pain and jumped in surprise. In the morning, the two tigers were on the mound, jumping and fighting with each other, and after a while, they finally separated. That day, the monks in the temple dared not open the door. A month later, in the early morning, I saw the two white and black tigers on the other side of the mountain, jumping and rising together; once they landed, they released each other. The following year, there were four cubs walking together. Some say, "When tigers mate and leap, they get one cub." All four cubs were born from one mating.

When Zheng was in his later years, after reaching his seventies, he would always carry an umbrella when walking, with an iron pole for self-defense. He often said, "If I encounter a tiger once, I wait for it to pounce and then dodge to the left or right, using the pole to strike its waist, which can make it not rise to pounce again. If I encounter two or three tigers, I open the umbrella and spin it, which can make the tiger doubt and dare not pounce on me." Several years later, Zheng went to a neighboring village to watch a village play and shouldered the umbrella on his way back. In the middle of the journey, it became dark, and a tiger suddenly appeared on the left side of the path. Zheng could not avoid the pounce and fell into the ravine. He quickly sat up, opened the umbrella, and waited for the tiger. Unaware that the tiger also fell down, it pressed down on Zheng's body. The umbrella spun like a wheel, and the tiger crouched between Zheng's waist and legs, staring at the spinning umbrella. Zheng quickly took the iron knife he was wearing, with his right hand striking its tail and his left hand pulling its genitalia. The tiger was still confused by the umbrella and was shocked by touching its genitalia. It leaped up with great force, cutting off its genitalia by more than an inch. Zheng held the ground, his hand not letting go of the umbrella. Fortunately, neighbors who were watching a play passed by, called out to help, and carried him home. But Zheng was exhausted. Two days later, he died.

A Ghost Requests Shen Liren, the Imperial Attendant, to Take Office Shen Liren, whose courtesy name was Sun Lian, fell ill at his lodging in the capital for more than ten days. He told his close friends, "There was a man in red clothes who descended from the sky into the courtyard and said that the position of the city god of Baoding in Zhili Province was vacant and that he should be ordered to take over. I replied, 'My old father is in the south, my wife and children have no support, I am alone and without companions, dying away from home is pitiable.' I asked the man in red to kindly decline on my behalf and choose someone else. The man in red left and then returned, saying, 'Your father, as a commoner, received titles and honors, already very honored. There is a brother who will take care of him, so he will not be left unattended; your son has already entered the school, what else do you have to worry about? If everyone were to be called but all refused, there would be no one to be called!' The man in red spoke thus, and I no longer hope for life. If you could arrange my after-death affairs." Many of his close friends tried to comfort him, saying it was just delirium due to illness. However, after this, Shen did not speak again, and medicines and drinks were all stopped.

After three days, at the first watch, the driver stayed at the door. He heard a loud knocking sound and asked who it was. The knockers replied, "Please sir, it's time for you to take office." The driver thought they had knocked on the wrong door and told them to find another house. The knockers insisted, "This is definitely your house." The driver said, "Our master is a government official in the capital, and he hasn't left the city for ten years. He is currently ill and cannot take office." The knockers replied, "We are not officials from outside. We are officials of the provincial city god's office in Zhili Province. A new official will be taking office tomorrow, and we are waiting here for you. There is no one to manage things, and you have not prepared any travel gear or gifts. So we are informing you." The driver was very frightened and pulled his neck into the blanket, unable to sleep. After the fourth watch, he heard Shen's voice calling from inside. Then, the sound of a sedan chair's pole touching the door was heard, and the sound of coughing was like Shen's. The sound gradually faded away, and then the sound of someone crying could be heard. The next day, the driver told Shen's close associates about the incident. They realized that what was said the day before was not delirious.

The Art of Communicating with the Dead According to Mr. Gu Meipo, the Deputy Magistrate of Nantang, the Celestial Master Zhang had a method known as the "Art of Communicating with the Dead." This ritual could summon the spirits of the deceased to inquire about matters that were unclear. The spirits would speak through a living person, who would be unaware of the communication and had to be a simple-minded individual to serve as a medium. Gu Meipo himself had witnessed the 56th generation of Celestial Masters performing this ritual. At one point, a certain judge had lost fifty taels of his salary, which he could not recover. Overwhelmed with shame and regret, he committed suicide by hanging. After his death, the missing silver still could not be found. The owner then decided to use the Art of Communicating with the Dead. They instructed a water carrier to stand on the threshold, sprayed him with water, and attached over a hundred talismans to his body, nearly covering him entirely. Talismans were placed on his eyes and ears, but not on his head or mouth. Initially, the water carrier moved, but soon he became motionless, as if cast in bronze. After a short while, he began to speak and found himself at the gate of the underworld. There, he saw the judge standing outside the gate, his shoulder carrying a beam with a rope attached, waiting to be judged. When the water carrier arrived, the judge said, "Go back and tell the Celestial Master that the silver was hidden under the floor by my private male lover." The Celestial Master sent someone to check, and indeed, the silver was found, not a single coin missing. The Celestial Master then asked, "Why do you carry a beam on your shoulder?" The judge replied, "All those who die by hanging carry a beam with a rope attached and cannot be freed. It is a heavy burden, and only through rituals performed in the world of the living can we be released. Otherwise, we cannot be reborn. Please, Celestial Master, show your compassion and perform the rituals for us." The Celestial Master agreed.

Suddenly, the King of the Underworld issued a command to the judges of the Celestial Master's office: "Know that you have often disturbed the underworld with trivial matters. Therefore, you are to be punished with twenty strokes. Afterwards, you must strictly prohibit such actions. Otherwise, you will face severe punishment." The waterman stood motionless, then suddenly made a gesture of bending, counted to twenty, and stood up again, still motionless. All the words from the underworld were spoken by the waterman. The Celestial Master asked for a confession or statement, and the waterman answered each question immediately. The waterman then said, "The door gods of the Celestial Master's office do not allow me to enter." This was because the waterman had forgotten to burn a talisman to command the door gods. After waking up, the waterman felt his feet were extremely weak. Matters of the underworld were very vague and unclear. As more talismans were being pasted on, the waterman felt increasingly constrained. The sides of his body were pressed tightly, and he felt his soul burst out from the top of his head, the pain was unbearable. When he returned, he still entered through the top of his head, and his whole body felt completely relaxed and comfortable, as if a heavy burden had been lifted, like finally getting to sleep after extreme fatigue. After waking up, he had marks on his buttocks, which were blue and took a long time to fade. From then on, the judges dared not lightly use the method to communicate with the underworld.

In the city of Yue, there is a district known as the Inactive People's Alley, where the area is one square li in size. The men are musicians, and the women are xipi. During weddings in the general populace, the men sing, and the women assist the bride in her preparations, including dressing and paying respects. They stand by the door like maids until the bride enters her room and then leaves, which is why they are called xipi. They are skilled at pleasing people, and both men and women find their interactions enjoyable. These entertainers have regular clients or employers, much like a landlord and tenant relationship, and they can substitute for one another. All transactions are formalized with contracts. Aside from weddings and funerals, they often engage in matchmaking and selling agricultural products. There was a young nobleman who enjoyed the company of such entertainers. One day, a xipi he was particularly close to told him, "On a certain day, you can come to my house, and I will prepare a feast for you." The nobleman went as agreed, and she said, "Please wait, there is something better in store." The nobleman did not understand and thought it was just playful talk. Shortly after, a sedan chair carrying a young woman arrived at the door. When he saw her, she was a beautiful young woman, dressed elegantly, and about twenty-three or twenty-four years old. The xipi communicated on her behalf, and they settled down to drink tea. The xipi left and locked the door. The nobleman understood her intentions and approached the young woman, who did not strongly resist. After their enjoyment, he asked her name and where she lived, but she did not answer. When he requested to meet again, she said, "It depends on whether our缘分 (fate) is fulfilled." When he opened the curtain to leave, the xipi had been waiting outside for a long time and helped her dress. They left together in the sedan chair. The nobleman insisted on knowing the young woman's name, but the xipi firmly refused to reveal it.

The following year, the young lord watched a water performance, and in the painted boat, there was a person adorned with pearls and jade, with maids and elderly women serving by her side, conveying her meaning with her eyes. Soon after, the oars rubbed against the sides of the boat, creating a sound, and they dispersed immediately upon seeing something, and could no longer be recognized. Water Dog Demons and Their Lust Water dogs are known for their lustful nature. In Wu and Yue, many women from small households wash their undergarments in the water, and after eating these garments for a long time, the water dogs can become strange and bewitch people. Females often mate with different species, and if they become strange, they can bewilder men, but they do not immediately turn into demons and kill. Males can smell the scent of young women's undergarments and immediately cling and do not leave, even if many people chase and hit them, until death, their strength does not wane. In the month of November 1911, a person from Cai Village married a woman. The guests dispersed, and the maids and servants went to bed. The young man was drunk and went to sleep first, and the new bride closed the door and loosened her belt. Then something wrapped around her feet, snorting and drooling. The new bride was startled and bewildered, but she was quite clever and did not make a sound. She secretly opened the door and told her mother-in-law, who knew it was a water dog demon. The new bride returned to her room, and the water dog was kneeling at the door waiting, continuing to wrap around her feet as before. After some time, the husband's parents gathered over ten strong men, each holding a candle and a stick, and they entered the room, immediately locking the door and standing guard. They saw the water dog and attacked together. If the water dog went onto the bed, they attacked from above; if it landed, they attacked from below; if it moved towards the tables, they gathered to attack. The house had no intact items, and the water dog was beaten to death on the ground. Its fur was black like a mirror, its body was one foot five inches long, and its sexual organ was seven inches long, no different from a human, but its meat lumps were very large. They skinned it, and the value of the skin was enough to compensate for the destroyed items. Its meat was too fishy to eat.

Someone said, "Otter liver marrow is included in medical texts. Its power is like this, it can be used as an aphrodisiac, but it is regrettable that the medical texts do not record it, and the villagers are all unaware of it." ○ A child named Weng Li Wang, carrying a basket of rice, crossed a wooden bridge by the stream at dusk when it was raining. The child held onto the wooden railing to cross the bridge. A tiger was lurking under the bridge and bit the child's head from the front, took the basket and left. The child fell to the ground, thinking it was someone who pushed him down, took the basket and left. The next day, people in the mountains saw the tiger running wildly through the mountains, the tiger had the basket in its mouth and couldn't get rid of it. When the tiger's mouth closed, the basket closed with it; when the tiger's mouth opened, the basket opened with it, the basket was full in the tiger's mouth. The vine is tough, the strands of the vine embedded themselves in the tiger's teeth. The tiger's nature is impulsive, it could not bear it, it ran for three days and finally died in the mountains. Its head was still raised, its mouth was open, its mouth still held the basket. ○ Gan Gong entered a dream. Gan Zongzai, my student in the year Yǐwèi, had a dream. His grandson achieved success, became a Hanlin scholar in a certain term, served as the chief examiner in Hubei, and died in the examination hall. Later, his younger brother became the governor of Hanxing, supervised the autumn examinations. Lying in bed at night, he dreamed of Gan Gong pulling the curtain and entering, surprised and said, "Is my second uncle here?" The governor also woke up in surprise. Toward the people around him, only then did he realize where he was, it was the place where the chief examiner's coffin was temporarily placed.

●卷八○尸变 In Yinxian, there was a man named Tang Adā who was in the capital. When his brother visited, he treated him rudely. Someone asked him why, and he replied that twenty years ago, he and his brother had been guarding the corpse of a neighbor's daughter. His brother went downstairs to fetch tea, and Adā, admiring the corpse's beauty, harbored impure thoughts. He stared at it for a long time, when suddenly the corpse stood up and chased him around the room. Adā reached the door and tried to escape, but the door was already knocked from the outside. It turned out that his brother had gone upstairs and seen the corpse chasing him, so he was afraid and knocked on the door. Adā jumped out of the window and ran away, but the corpse could not jump. Adā fainted and died on the tiles, while the corpse stood stiffly and motionless. The next morning, the family went upstairs and found the corpse still standing stiffly. They took a rice sieve to lower the corpse and bury it. Three days later, Adā returned from the market and saw the woman scolding him in broad daylight. Adā went back to the city, then to the capital, and has not dared to return home ever since. ○鬼买行头 In Hangzhou, there was a man named Shī Sānpìn who ran a thread shop. After his death, he had no children, and his wife, having stolen from his home, remarried someone else. Sānpìn went to the underworld to lodge a complaint, but the king of the underworld refused to hear it. Sānpìn consulted the judges and clerks of the underworld, who said: "If a woman remarries without taking her husband's property, then we have no grounds to intervene. But if your wife took your property and remarried, then you have money to give us. We can bring your wife to you, and even if the lord knows, it will not be a great crime. However, you must bring silver to buy attire for the underworld, so that you can scare your new husband and take your wife's soul." Sānpìn followed their advice, crossed the river to his family home to borrow four hundred units of underworld funds. The new husband's family heard that when firecrackers were set off, the ghost would call out, and they saw those who had drowned or hanged themselves, all of whom were wearing the attire of the underworld. ---

After the tenth month, a newly deceased carpenter ghost appeared. The胥役 said, "This ghost has the power to take your wife's soul." The carpenter indeed chopped her bed and cut off her foot, and the wife cried for three days before dying. Later, the husband gathered the necessary funds for medical treatment, coffin, and prayers, and the expenses exactly matched the amount the ghost had brought. ○ Han Liu, a Messenger of Death from Shanyin, appeared to Ye, a nineteen-year-old from a money shop, who had been ill for over twenty days. He suddenly stood up and knelt for several days, saying, "I am Han Liu, a living Messenger of Death from Shanyin, now serving in the afterlife. I was friendly with your uncle in life. Your life is not yet over, but you are being punished in the afterlife for cursing your stepmother behind her back when you were young. However, it can still be reversed. Your uncle must perform a ritual at the first judge's shrine to the east of the Yue Temple the day after my master goes out to visit guests. He should burn paper money and pray devoutly, and I will intercede on your behalf. But when entering the temple, do not mention anything; just burn more paper money." The next day, Han Liu returned and said, "Your uncle can gather witnesses and write a guarantee, burn it immediately, and I will take it to intercede for you. Your uncle should come tomorrow at noon, without waiting for my master to return."

To the appointed time, Uncle Leaf went to the temple to pray. Han had already arrived at home to communicate, instructing him to kneel and say, "The petition has been submitted, much effort has been made, the internal matter has been decided, but only requires eight hundred units of currency. Your uncle has his own knowledge and experience. Try asking, 'How did the sparrow come here?'" Leaf's illness was then cured. The son of the woman who sells grilled cake at Qingliang Bridge is a county official. In the summer of the Geng Xu year, he carried his uniform home. Another official named Xu lost his uniform, saw him, and asked if it was his uniform. Angry that he was accused of theft, he cursed him. The next day, together with his mother, the woman who sells grilled cake, they went to the city's city god temple, placed an incense burner, and cursed him, also cursing the god for being unresponsive. At that time, there were three other officials named Ye, Li, and Sun, who saw and tried to stop them, and the matter was settled. In September, another official named Cheng died.

In the year of the Metal Pig (辛亥年), on the evening of the fourteenth day of the first lunar month, a man went to watch the lanterns and suddenly collapsed upon returning home. By morning, his face was pale, and he claimed to have been struck and rebuked by officials of the underworld. He recounted the process of being arrested and brought to the underworld, saying, "The King of the Underworld judged a man surnamed Cheng to be a thief of clothing, having already lost his allotted years of life. He is now being punished with a wooden collar. Mr. Xu happened to ask about it, but originally had no fault. Mr. Ye, Mr. Li, and Mr. Sun, though not involved, were willing to step forward enthusiastically to resolve disputes and remove obstacles, warning people not to disrespect deities. Each was granted three more years of good fortune. I, for a slight offense of disrespecting deities, was struck and then ordered to receive forty lashes from officials in the world." He also said that Han Liu handled the matter and released him. After the official seal was opened, he was indeed punished with forty lashes by public officials. Mr. Ye is old, Mr. Li and Mr. Sun are middle-aged, and they are all fine today. Dai Qi, also a servant from Shanyin, loved gambling and prostitution, often staying away for over a month. His wife, whose surname is not specified, entrusted her neighbor Wang San to deliver a message, saying she needed money and rice to live. Wang San found Dai Qi in a brothel and jokingly said, "You are here enjoying yourself, your wife sent a message: you have no money to send back, even if you return, you will still need to support a man!" Dai Qi believed it to be true and said, "His wife would say such things to Wang San, she must have a reason."

That night at the second watch, Dai returned home and knocked on the door urgently. The wife got up, put on her clothes, and opened the door, angry at his long absence, making a face and remaining silent before entering the room and lying down. Dai thought she had a secret lover in the room, so he lit a lamp and searched everywhere but found nothing, sitting there doubting her. Just then, there was someone named Wu, also a fellow officer, who passed through his alley and happened to tap the ash from his pipe on the wall three times. Dai, suspecting an appointment, thought that he must have come by arrangement and opened the door to chase him. Wu, surprised, ran away quickly. Dai chased him for more than a mile and caught up with Wu, and they each looked at each other and dispersed. Dai returned home, accusing the wife of having an affair with Wu and beat her. She was pregnant for more than a month and died. That winter, Wang San died of illness. In the first ten days of the first month of the year Xin Hai, after dinner, Wu's mouth became stiff, and he then became mute and fell into a deep sleep. The next morning when he woke up, he said, "I should go to thank Han Six, I should go to inform Dai Seven." When his mouth was stiff, he saw two officers from the underworld, one of whom was Han Six. They took him to the underworld, where he saw the chief wearing a warm hat like a high-ranking official. The chief decided the case: "Wang caused a death by mocking someone with words, his life is over, he should be beaten forty times, put in stocks for three years, and another case will be decided. Wu used illegal food ash and should not have tapped it on someone's door at night; Dai, after opening the door, should not have run away; Dai, after chasing for more than a mile and meeting him, should have explained the reason to dispel doubts. Wu should lose half a decade of his life and be beaten one hundred and twenty times. Dai, who roamed and did not return home, killed his wife out of suspicion, should be cut off from his descendants and suffer poverty and hunger. Check the underworld records, Dai already has a seven-year-old son, order five ghosts to take his soul." Han Six also said, "I read the verdict to you and charged eight hundred units." Then he went to Han's house to burn paper money as thanks.

Dai was greatly alarmed by what he heard, and took his son to pray to the gods. On the third day, the son died suddenly without any illness. The bruises on Wu's face faded after a month. ○ A Ghost Buys a Position in Shanyin County Mr. Xu, a clerk in the county office, was ill and saw his deceased elder brother come to him and say, "I have already bought a position for you in the underworld. After you die, you can still serve as a clerk in the underworld, and there will be no suffering." Soon after, a deceased county official named ZHU came to him and said, "You can avoid death if you pay me a large sum of money. I can help you resolve the issue." Mr. Xu agreed. After ZHU left, his elder brother came back and said, "The deceased ZHU was actually trying to buy your position, and he wanted to cheat you out of your money. Your lifespan is predetermined; seeking to avoid death is futile, and you are only giving up this position for nothing." Mr. Xu said, "I have already agreed to ZHU, what should I do?" His elder brother replied, "The affairs of the underworld are like those of the human world. This position is still a long way off in time, so this is just a preliminary arrangement. ZHU can still change his mind, it's not too late." Mr. Xu asked, "Then where can I find ZHU to change the agreement?" His elder brother said, "I can go." The next day, his elder brother and ZHU came together, and they gathered to discuss it. ZHU indeed wanted to buy the position, and he argued with Mr. Xu's elder brother. Another deceased ghost, Mr. XX, also came and mediated the dispute, deciding that five years later, this position would be given to Mr. Xu first, and after Mr. Xu had served for five years, ZHU would then take over. ZHU agreed, but soon after, he came back and said, "I cannot wait, I will look for another position." Mr. Xu's illness gradually improved.

This matter occurred during the Qianlong Xinhai year, and now Mr. Xu is safe. The clerks of Shanyin can all speak of it, and it is very certain. ○ The folk deities of Wen Chang, known as the Heavenly Peng in Taoism and the Yaksas in Buddhism, are quite effective in their divine power. In the early autumn of the Bing Xu year, Lou Xiangfu from Anchang Lane in Shanyin, who had taken leave from his post as a circuit inspector in Shanxi, went out to visit a friend. He happened to meet an old friend, Jingfu, on the road. Jingfu pulled him to the side of the road and whispered confidentially, "Our family's matter of repairing the ancestral temple has been exposed! The ghost of the ancestor who sold the land, surnamed Zhou, is very powerful. Initially, he accused the local land and city gods, and I have already cleared the matter for him. Now, Wen Chang has been sent by the Heavenly Emperor to handle a maritime case in Zha浦, and he has come personally to the sea. Zhou has petitioned him directly, and the general has already agreed. He has sent a deputy deity to the ancestral temple to investigate the matter with the city and land gods. The repair of the ancestral temple was originally overseen by my brothers, but the matter of relocating the tomb was actually handled by you. You should be prepared for questioning. You should quickly return home, bathe, change your clothes, choose a room to rest in, and wait for the summons. Instruct your family not to make noise, and especially avoid crying, as crying will scatter your soul and prevent it from returning. Do not be afraid of this matter; I believe that the city and land gods will also protect you and will not overturn the case. I will provide spiritual assistance for you; you can burn more paper money and also burn copies of the land sale contract of the Zhou family." Lou Xiangfu was speaking in low tones at the side of the road, with no one to respond to him, and his friend found this strange.

Xiangfu finished speaking and directly returned home to bathe and change clothes, then entered the study room and locked himself in. His family gathered outside the window, quietly watching and listening. After a while, he made sounds, all statements of truth and confession. He ordered his family to prepare many tea sets for guests, even up to one hundred and more cups were still not enough, and the guests left at the fifth watch of the night. In the morning, Xiangfu woke up and unlocked the door to come out, explaining the matters he had been questioned about. When buying land to build a shrine, he had moved over ten coffins. Xiangfu provided funds to the workers, but the workers were negligent and left behind one bone of the Zhou ancestor. After the move was completed, only then did they find the bone, fearing the owner's blame, they secretly discarded the bone into the river. The Zhou family continued to complain in the underworld, and summoned the ghosts of those whose coffins were moved to appeal to the Warm God. The god ordered the city god to investigate the whereabouts of the bone, and it was found in the water, clearly. The god said, "The Zhou descendants received money, willing to sell the land and move the coffins, and Lousi repeatedly paid for the labor to build the ancestral hall, and there are records, so they are originally without fault." The Zhou descendants are few and scattered, their descendants selling the ancestral tomb is originally inappropriate, but they are already poor, so there is no need to discuss further. The worker, Wang, received payment for moving the bones and secretly threw them into the water, truly a fault, his fate is exhausted, and he is handed over to the Department of Fates for punishment. The Zhou family cried and left.

Zhou Ben, a fellow villager, had military achievements before his death, but Liu refused to mention his name. In that year, there was a tidal disaster in Zhapu, drowning thousands of people. General Wen's mission, his words were cautious. Liu is a simple and honest person, now eighty-three years old, still walks healthily without a cane. --- ### Ghost Smoking Talk - Bamboo Green, Name Shaking, Deqing Person In the summer of the Qianlong era, he stayed in Suzhou looking for a teaching position, and once contracted a severe case of typhoid fever, which lasted several days and left him extremely weak. In a delirious state, he dreamed of a man in a blue robe holding a scroll in front of him and said, "Call you to go." Tan said, "Who called me?" The man said, "The King of Hell called you." Upon hearing this, Tan was frightened and did not want to go. The man in the blue robe then opened the scroll, which contained black paper with white characters, resembling a modern-day memorial. Tan unconsciously followed him.

At one place, there was an official sitting at a desk, with a clerk standing beside him, seemingly arguing about official matters. Tan approached the desk, and the clerk asked, "Are you Tan the scholar-official?" Tan replied, "Yes." The clerk said, "What you are saying pertains to your own affairs." Frightened, Tan turned around and fled. He then arrived at another place, where he saw a round archway. Looking into the spacious hall inside, he saw over ten tables arranged, with officials in formal attire sitting above, as if they were reviewing cases. In the center, an official with a golden face, who looked terrifying, was seated. Tan dared not enter. A person in plain clothes pushed him from behind. He was already at the desk. The official with the golden face asked, "There is someone surnamed Yan who has accused you in my office." Tan said, "What am I being accused of?" The official replied, "You are accused of being an adulterous man and woman." Tan said, "There is no such thing." The official with the golden face immediately ordered the ghost soldiers to bring the accused and witnesses. Then, there were over ten prison carts pushed to the bottom of the steps. First, a male accused was called, who saw Tan and said, "It is not this person." Later, a female accused recognized from afar and said, "Although it is not this person, the appearance is somewhat similar." The official with the golden face then asked Tan, "Do you know the Buddhist nun from the Rice Lane?" Tan replied, "I do not recognize her." The official with the golden face immediately ordered the person in plain clothes to send him back to the mortal world. The female accused in the cart still waved to Tan and said, "Why not come to my place for tea?" Tan did not respond and left.

On the way, a man dressed in blue clothes took out a pipe from a sock bucket, which was only five inches long, and asked me to smoke. I realized it was a ghost and refused to take a puff. After waking up from the dream, sweat soaked through the heavy quilt, and his illness was cured. ○ Encounter with a Fox Spirit In She County, there was a young man named Li Shengxiu, who had a beautiful appearance and was fourteen years old. He studied in a separate house twenty li away from Yan Town. One night, when the water clock struck two, the student fell asleep. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful woman sitting on the bed, looking at each other with a smile. She was about fifteen or sixteen years old. The student's heart was moved, and he touched her hand. She did not reject him, and they engaged in intimate relations. Every night, she would mysteriously appear, often teaching the student to write poetry and fill in lyrics, and would revise them. Sometimes they discussed current affairs, but she looked sad and unhappy, saying, "This has nothing to do with learning, and since you have no chance of success in the imperial examinations, why bother with such hard work?" Thus, they exchanged verses, and he did not feel lonely or bored. For several years, no one knew about it. There was another person named Yang Sheng, who was a cousin or relative of the student. He also studied in the same courtyard, and his room was only separated by a wall. He often wondered why the student closed his door immediately after dark. One night under the moonlight, Yang Sheng secretly peered through a crack in the wall and saw the student embracing a beautiful woman sitting. He quickly knocked on the door and entered, searching everywhere but found nothing. When asked, he first denied it. The next night, he peered again and saw the same scene, and heard the sounds of laughter and conversation. He knew it was a fox spirit and rushed to tell the student's father. The father urged the student to return home, and the fox spirit followed him home. No one else saw it, only the student saw it, and the whole family worried that the student might be harmed.

One day, the protagonist's wife visited his room and loudly scolded him, saying, "How can an evil fox have no shame! It wants to forcibly take away someone else's husband. Moreover, our younger brother is already engaged to a family, and in the future when he marries, who will be the legitimate heir?" That night, the fox wept and told the protagonist, "Your wife reprimanded me, and her words were very just. I cannot stay, and now we part forever." The protagonist shed tears, but could not persuade the fox to stay. They both sobbed beside the pillow. When they heard the rooster crow, the fox got up and disappeared. The protagonist, Li, was skilled in poetry and music, and also proficient in martial arts, all of which he learned from the fox. It is said that the poems and verses the fox gave him were very beautiful and elegant, but unfortunately, they were not recorded by those who transmitted the story. This account was told by Hong Jieting from Xin'an, and Li himself spoke openly about it without reservation. **The Fairy Child Summoning Rain** In Guangdong, there was a severe drought, and Governor Sun prayed for rain but saw no result. At the time, he was inspecting Chaozhou prefecture. On the way, he saw thousands of people gathered on a hill in front. He sent someone to ask what was happening, and they replied, "We are here to see the fairy child." Previously, there was a twelve-year-old boy named Sun from a village in Chaozhou. He was herding calves with the other village boys and playing on the hill. One boy playfully hit him with a fist. As he hit away, suddenly the boy Sun's feet were several feet off the ground. Another boy hit him with a stone, and the higher he hit, the higher the boy rose, but the stone could not touch his body. The children then ran to tell others, causing a commotion among the villagers. Those from several miles away all came to see. His parents cried out in anguish, but the boy only laughed silently.

One day, the protagonist's wife visited his room and loudly scolded him, saying, "How can an evil fox have no shame! It wants to forcibly take away someone else's husband. Moreover, our younger brother is already engaged to a family, and in the future when he marries, who will be the legitimate heir?" That night, the fox wept and told the protagonist, "Your wife reprimanded me, and her words were very just. I cannot stay, and now we part forever." The protagonist shed tears, but could not persuade the fox to stay. They both sobbed beside the pillow. When they heard the rooster crow, the fox got up and disappeared. The protagonist, Li, was skilled in poetry and music, and also proficient in martial arts, all of which he learned from the fox. It is said that the poems and verses the fox gave him were very beautiful and elegant, but unfortunately, they were not recorded by those who transmitted the story. This account was told by Hong Jieting from Xin'an, and Li himself spoke openly about it without reservation. **The Fairy Child Summoning Rain** In Guangdong, there was a severe drought, and Governor Sun prayed for rain but saw no result. At the time, he was inspecting Chaozhou prefecture. On the way, he saw thousands of people gathered on a hill in front. He sent someone to ask what was happening, and they replied, "We are here to see the fairy child." Previously, there was a twelve-year-old boy named Sun from a village in Chaozhou. He was herding calves with the other village boys and playing on the hill. One boy playfully hit him with a fist. As he hit away, suddenly the boy Sun's feet were several feet off the ground. Another boy hit him with a stone, and the higher he hit, the higher the boy rose, but the stone could not touch his body. The children then ran to tell others, causing a commotion among the villagers. Those from several miles away all came to see. His parents cried out in anguish, but the boy only laughed silently.

Governor-General heard of this and was astonished. He went on foot with his subordinates and officials to observe the scene. Looking up, he saw a child with a green hat hanging on his back and a cowwhip stuck in his waist, standing in mid-air. The governor-general was worried about the drought and prayed, saying, "Are you truly a divine being? If you can bring rain for three days to save the crops, I will build a shrine for you." The child smiled and nodded. Soon after, a single cloud appeared, and the child disappeared without a trace. The governor-general also mounted a carriage and continued his journey. Shortly thereafter, heavy rain poured down, and for several days the wind blew. Reports from various parts of Guangdong stated that the rain had filled all the ditches and ponds. The governor-general then ordered a statue of the child to be carved and sent a painter to his home to draw a portrait of him. The child's parents were simple farmers and found it difficult to describe his appearance. Despite drawing several versions, none resembled him. Just when they were at a loss, the child suddenly descended from the air, smiling and saying, "I came especially to draw my face." The painter then completed the portrait. The parents tried to keep him, but he suddenly vanished. The statue was then placed in the Three Xuan Palace in Wuyang City and was inscribed as "Feathered Immortal Master Sun," and the incense burned brightly. This event took place in May of the 52nd year of the Qianlong reign. From She County, Hong Jieting traveled to the eastern part of Guangdong and personally witnessed the welcome of the image of the child Master Sun. He then inquired about the details and, fearing there might be omissions or doubts, intended to consult Master Bu Shan for verification in the future.

In the year of Jǐ Yǒu during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, the magistrate of Changshu County was Jìng. One evening, at the second watch, a civilian named someone returned home and suddenly saw a figure wearing red pants and black whips holding a torch standing in the street. Only the upper part of the figure was visible from the waist up. The person fled into a relative's home, and the entity immediately pursued him there. He then took a copper basin and hit it, causing the entity to transform into five parts. Terrified, he closed the door and entered. Later, a patrol boat was on the river, and the soldiers on board saw that the people they were guarding were all wearing red pants and black boots. They realized these were demons. They struck the demons with guns, and each one transformed into five parts. Soon after, the river was filled with these transformed entities. At night, they suddenly entered civilian homes, causing unrest throughout the city. The magistrate Jìng sent someone to invite Mr. Gu, whose courtesy name was Demao, to come. He asked Mr. Gu about the cause. Mr. Gu said, "Try hitting them with a drum." The strange phenomenon became even more severe. Then, he ordered them to hit the demons with gongs, and the demons retreated. He explained, "These are the appearance of an army of the dead. Soldiers advance with drums and retreat with metal." The order was given to the entire county to strike gongs, and it took three days to calm down. In Hangzhou, there was a wealthy young man named Jīn Tǐngzhī, a handsome youth who could not obtain the affection of a certain woman. Therefore, a demon impersonated this woman to bewitch him. At night, the demon would hug him very tightly, and Jīn would ejaculate profusely, almost developing a wasting disease. He fled to another house. When the demon arrived, it could not find him. It immediately tied straw mats together to form a person on an empty floor, used a clay bowl as a head, inserted mountain flowers, and wore a red brocade robe to scare his family. It also made a murmuring, chattering sound.

One day, a large steamed bun was brought, inscribed with the four characters "秀结宜男" (Beautifully Tied for a Son), written in graceful and charming calligraphy. The household invited Guo Anbo and Wan Jinpeng to inspect it. Wan said, "This is a snake demon, cultivated for over a thousand years. I have taken the Bodhisattva precepts and cannot bring myself to kill it, but can only drive it away." Guo then drew a Pre-Qin Eight Trigrams diagram to affix for protection, while Wan wrote the four characters "楞严咒心" (Heart Mantra from the Avatamsaka Sutra) to treat it. The demon began to weep and speak to a young maid, saying, "I originally come from Yangzhou, visiting my sister. Because the drum tower was destroyed, I cannot see my sister. I happened to see Jin Lang, who is handsome, and fell in love with him. Now I am being chased away, and I have set a deadline for myself to leave. But from now on, I cannot see Jin Lang. I ask the singing boy he likes to sing the 'Yangguan Tune' for me, and that will be enough." The household arrived at the appointed time and indeed sent him off with music and clear singing. They then rewarded the singing boy with an embroidered bottle bag and six taels of silver, and he left. This was an event in the second month of the Renzi year. ○ A Black Demon Feared Salt Ding Xianrong, from Zhucheng, said that there was a village named Yinjia outside the city, with many ancient tombs. It was said that there was a monster in the tombs, shaped like a human face but without substance, only a black mass of gas about ten feet tall, appearing at night and hiding during the day. When it appeared, it would encounter people on the road, stopping at a distance of one arrow's length, and would emit a roar like thunder, causing people's hearts to tremble and their courage to falter. Only those who saw it could hear it; others would not feel it. After roaring, it would block people with its black gas, which was extremely foul-smelling, causing anyone who smelled it to faint. The villagers warned each other about this, considering it a dangerous place, and no one ventured out at dusk or night.

A salt trader was selling salt elsewhere and indulged in drinking, becoming drunk and forgetting his caution. He accidentally stepped on something else. It was late at night, with the moon up and it being two o'clock in the morning, when a strange creature suddenly appeared, blocking the path and roaring loudly. The trader tried to fend it off with a wooden stick, but it seemed to be unaffected. Frightened and at a loss, he quickly took some salt and threw it at the creature. The creature gradually retreated and disappeared into the ground. The trader then emptied the entire salt from his basket at that spot and left. In the morning, he went back to check the site and found the discarded salt piled up on the ground, all turning red, with a foul smell and scattered blood spots. After this incident, the strange creature did not appear again. ○ A Zombie Grabbing a Person Can Be Treated with Date Pits During his time as an official, Yu Mingfu Peilian, before reaching his position, was a guest in Henan. He mentioned that in that region, there were many wild graves, and there was a problem of zombies grabbing people. The locals had a method to deal with it, and they did not consider it unusual. Anyone who was grabbed by a zombie held tightly, even if their hands were broken and their nails dug into the skin, could not escape. By using seven date pits and nailing them into the zombie's back, the hands would loosen and the person could be freed. This method had been tested and proven effective. If a recently deceased body moved, it was called "走影" (shadow-walking), which was caused by the influence of yang energy. If a person was grabbed by a zombie, this method could also be used to free them.

○ **Method of Assessing Boys' Maturity (量童子法)**: According to *The Chu Shi's Legacy*, boys reach sexual maturity at the age of 16 (two eights) and can have sexual relations with women. Their sexual vigor begins to decline at the age of 64 (eight eights). However, in recent times, the strength of a person's constitution varies, and there are cases of boys getting married and having children as young as 13 or 14, which seems to deviate from the fixed numbers. There is a common method for assessing boys' maturity, which can determine whether they are capable of having sexual relations. The method involves using a piece of coarse string to measure around the boy's neck, noting the length of the string. The string is then folded in half and used to measure from the tip of the nose to the ear. If the length of the neck exceeds the length from the nose to the ear, the boy is considered capable of sexual relations; otherwise, he is still considered a child and not capable of having sexual relations. ○ **Wan Jinpeng's Account**: According to Wan Jinpeng, when Hu Zhongcheng, a government official, was gravely ill, he suddenly told his family, "Tomorrow, carefully close my door, do not call me or enter." Following his instructions, the family members closed the door and did not call or enter. The next day, as evening approached, they did not call or open the door. The wife was suspicious and went to look through a crack in the door. She saw two tables set up facing each other inside the room. On the table facing south, there was a large, golden-eyed creature with a huge mouth, its eyes shining and its mouth moving. On the table facing north, Hu Zhongcheng was sitting, facing the creature. There were papers and a pen on the table, and he was holding the pen, as if he was about to write a letter, though it was unclear what they were discussing. She was greatly alarmed and burst into the room. Hu Zhongcheng threw down the pen and stood up, saying, "You have ruined my plan! Otherwise, I could have prolonged my life by a few more years. But this is also fate. Quickly prepare my funeral arrangements, I will die within three days." Indeed, he died within three days, but no one knows what the large-headed creature was.

At the time, Zhang Liudry was present, and he said, "This is called 'Spiritual Talisman,' and it is associated with the Star of Wen Chang. Those who have literary reputation and talent/morality often seek it out and gain from it. In the past, Zhu Xi commented on the *Four Books*, and whenever he encountered this talisman, his literary thoughts advanced daily. Later, he could summon it to come and dismiss it to go, and whenever he encountered doubtful points, he would analyze and explain them in detail. The vice minister likely wanted to summon it to come and pray for his fortune and destiny, but unexpectedly, it was defeated by women." I then asked from which book this information came, and he replied, "It is recorded in the preface of Zhu Xi's collected works." I noted it down and intended to investigate its beginning and end during my leisure time. ○ The Swallowing-Boat Fish All seafarers, when setting sail, would purchase ash from written paper and carry it with them, believing that the sea is full of strange winds and all kinds of water monsters, including the Swallowing-Boat Fish. Throwing ash at them would cause them to leave. There was a salt merchant engaged in maritime trade, who loaded his boat with salt and set out. One day, he suddenly encountered a large fish that came by sucking up waves. There was no ash with writing on the boat, so he threw salt packets at it, and the fish swallowed and sucked up dozens before leaving. Several days later, he heard that a large fish had died on the beach, and the remnants of the salt packets in its stomach were still undigested. He then realized that it had died from eating the salt.

○ **Chicken Feather Smoke Kills Snakes** (李金什言): Burning chicken feathers to produce smoke can kill all poisonous snakes upon smelling it. All creatures related to dragons and giant snails are the same; none can escape. However, no one knows why they are controlled in this way. Or perhaps it is easy to understand. All dragons, giant snails, and snakes are yin. Chickens are originally symbols of accumulated yang in the south, their nature belongs to fire, the most yang. When they touch the most yang qi, they all die immediately. This is the control of the small and the great, lying in qi, not in form. ○ **Snake Clamp** (浙江衢州常山县有山名石空山, 山麓有寺, 曰石空寺, 山下溪水汇注, 民田皆枕山开陌): In Zhejiang Province, Quzhou City, Changshan County, there is a mountain called Shíkōng Mountain. At the foot of the mountain, there is a temple called Shíkōng Temple. The mountain is surrounded by streams that flow into the fields, which are all located at the foot of the mountain. The soil in the area produces a substance that looks like a pine cone or a lychee, of similar size. Its outer skin is the color of pine bark. When broken, it resembles asphalt. When burned, it vaporizes. The local people call this substance "snake clamp." When asked about its meaning, they say: "This is the soil that snakes contain when they hibernate, which they spit out onto the ground when they emerge from hibernation, hence the name." According to this, it is likely a byproduct of lead and mercury, which vaporize when exposed to fire, rather than the soil that snakes carry. The local people may not be aware of this.

When the younger monk left one day, the older monk suddenly surrounded and blocked the door with bricks, locking the room. Soon, a fire broke out inside. People tried to put it out but could not enter. The smoke and flames filled the air, and a white crane emerged through the smoke. After the fire was extinguished, they examined the remains and buried them in a pagoda courtyard. The younger monk never returned, and no one knew where he went.

○Xie Zhen and the Study of Objects Xie Zhen, courtesy name Ziwei, was from Wujin and came to Hangzhou as a guest in the government. He was a man of extraordinary talent, known for his sociability and love of guests. In his spare time, he was highly skilled in various arts and delved deeply into the study of principles and causation (格致之学). One day, he told someone, "Ancient people made objects with great care, even everyday small items contain profound truths. For example, brooms and dusters, which are used to remove dirt, are often overlooked. But did you know that inserting flowers into the corners of a broom can summon the deity Zicu; sweeping a young chicken's back with a broom can reveal the direction of its hair; burning a broom during an epidemic can ward off evil spirits; and cucumbers will rot faster when exposed to the wind of a broom. All these phenomena follow the principles of mutual influence and response." I then pointed to the fire-making tools on the right side of his seat and asked, "Do these also have some underlying principle?" He replied, "Metal and stone objects are formed by the condensation of earth and fire energies and are inherently similar, both being hard in nature. When iron strikes stone, it produces fire in response, as if the stone is repaying the iron's action. Therefore, the fire-making tool (火刀) should not be used to make fire frequently, as doing so will make it less effective. If the fire stone (火石) produces little fire, soak it in water for a day or two, and it will produce more fire when used again. Why is this so? Because metal is the 'mother' of water, and using it to make fire depletes its energy, making it less effective. The fire from the fire stone is distributed among the four elements, and when used excessively, the fire is hidden deep within the stone, making it hard to produce. Soaking it in water revives the fire, causing it to emerge fully, thus producing more fire when struck." I tested this and found it to be true.

Zhang Ningren, the narrator, said: His neighbor, an old man, was skilled in consuming smoke, holding a bamboo tube about five feet long, which he had used for over thirty years. Suddenly, a Taoist passed by the door and looked at Zhang's smoke tube and said, "This object of yours has gained human essence and energy, and it has long become a smoke dragon, effective in treating timidity. In the future, if anyone asks for it, do not give it lightly." One day, indeed a merchant came, saying that his son was suffering from timidity, "Knowing you have an old bamboo smoke tube, I beg to buy it for treatment." He then paid seventy thousand and took away a half-foot section. The son took it and, after consuming it, the tuberculosis worms were completely transformed into purple water and expelled. Another day, he met the Taoist again at the door, showing him the remaining tube and said, "The dragon is already injured in the tail, but it can still live. It needs to eat for another ten years, then it can be made into an elixir." Seeking the method, the Taoist only smiled and said nothing, then left directly. The bamboo tube still exists today, and Zhang has seen it, indeed it has a luster, with hair and beard fully reflected. When hung on the wall at night, all poisonous insects dare not approach.

○ **Formal and Gaseous Intercourse**: Liu Shangshe, a scholar from the city of Cheng, said: "All birds have eight external orifices, and internally they have fewer large intestines, only small intestines, which share the same opening for excretion. Animals with nine orifices (including a separate opening for excretion) have both large and small intestines, so they also have separate openings for excretion." Zhao Yiji said: "How can we know that the single intestine in birds is the small intestine and not the large intestine?" Liu replied: "In humans, the large intestine connects to the anus, while the small intestine connects to the urethra, which is the opening for urine. Animals are the same. Birds, however, have the small intestine positioned behind the cloaca. Observing geese and ducks during mating, the urethra protrudes from the back, which is evidence that it is the small intestine. Generally, birds with flat beaks engage in physical intercourse, while those with pointed beaks engage in non-physical intercourse, without any physical instruments." This explanation supplements the lost text "《禽经》." ○ **Honey Tiger**: The honey tiger is a type of bee, resembling a silkworm moth in appearance. It has spots on its head, two short whiskers on its nose, and black threads like iron wire on its mouth. It enters flower clusters to collect nectar, extending its black threads into the center of the flower to extract it, much like an elephant uses its trunk. Unlike regular bees, which use their feet to collect nectar, the honey tiger uses its "nose," demonstrating a unique method of nectar collection.

A Servant from the Wang Family of Zhucheng named Wang San, who had managed a farm for many years, said: "This insect is most numerous in Shandong and is a great pest to agriculture. The locals call it 'Gulu Gazi.' It has a body with five colors, fine hairs, and resembles a silkworm moth, with a tail that spreads out like a goose's tail. Males are slender and can be used in medicine, while females are fat and not used medicinally. In autumn, females have eggs inside their bodies, which hatch into various types of larvae. When the eggs are laid on soybean pods, they become bean worms that resemble green caterpillars. If the bean worms are stacked on top of each other, their fine hairs fall off. They can be cooked with oil, salt, garlic, and chili, and their taste is better than that of silkworm pupae. When they prey on bees, they enter the hive and use their proboscis to inject venom, killing the bees, and then slowly eat them. While bees have stingers at the end of their bodies, this insect has its stinger at the front. Those with stingers at the end belong to yin, while those with stingers at the front belong to yang. By using yang energy to control yin energy, the insect can defeat bees." ○ A Magical Grass from Southern Yunnan After returning from Yunnan, Hu Lishu, an official from the region, said that the area is rich in magical grasses. Recently, there is a plant called "An Luotuo" that is highly sought after. It can transform copper into silver and is also used to treat diseases. Local women use love potions to attract men. When the potion is made, it must be tested before use. The test involves placing two large stones at opposite ends of a room, coated with the potion, and observing if they move closer together at night. The potion is made from a combination of various herbs. Thus, the number of plants produced in remote and uncultivated regions that are not recorded in the *Bencao* (Herbal) is limitless, and it is not only the recent discovery of chicken blood vine gel that is valued.

**Translation:** A Deer Nourished by Sheep's Milk is produced in the mountains near Lin'an during the Qingming Festival period. Its offspring can only walk after it rains; if there is no rain, it remains immobile. Local people capture it and raise it at home, feeding it with sheep's milk. As it grows, it follows the sheep, and its wild nature becomes slightly tamed, making it suitable as an ornamental animal in gardens, and it is called a "sheep-milk deer." A Multi-Horned Beast: Monk Zhi Ding, residing in Tianmu Mountain, described a deep part of the mountain that stretches for one or two dozen miles, densely covered with thick undergrowth and without paths. It produces sandalwood, suitable for making planks, and porcupines often build their nests in tree crevices, causing trouble for woodworkers. Suddenly, one year, the porcupines disappeared completely, and no one knew where they went. The mountain people were happy and began freely using axes to cut down trees. A craftsman entered a deserted valley and saw an animal entangled in a large vine on a tree. Upon closer inspection, the animal looked like a cow but was twice as large. Its body was covered with short horns, two or three inches long, grayish-black in color, resembling sheep's horns, numbering in the thousands; it had a single horn on its head, red like blood, two or three feet long. The large vine often climbs big trees, and this beast accidentally jumped into it, getting its horns wrapped by the vine, with its four legs suspended in the air. The vine's soft and flexible nature left the beast with no place to exert force, and it eventually starved to death. It was then discovered that the porcupines had all been eaten by this beast, but no one knew what this beast was called.

Zhang Shouzhuang from Jiangzhong says: A guest was traveling on the Yangtze River one day when he suddenly saw something floating on the river surface, resembling a yellow cloth bundle in shape. It was swimming along with the waves, and he could not carefully examine it in a hurry. He called out to the boatman to look at it. The helmsman inside the boat was greatly alarmed and said, "There will surely be a danger of capsizing the boat, what should we do?" Quickly, they removed all the sails and masts from the boat, leaving only the bottom of the boat. Now the guest could sit calmly and wait. Just as the arrangements were completed, indeed, a sudden gust of wind arose, and the boat entered and exited from dangerous waves. Finally, fortunately without harm, other boats that were not prepared all suffered capsizing and drowning. He asked the reason and learned that because his father had also seen this object and suffered a disaster in the past, therefore he knew about it. But he did not know what it was. Recalling Jia Wencong, who was experienced in merchant ships, he once said that when traveling on the river, there would be strong winds, and there must first be a wind flag appearing on the water surface. Or is this the same thing?

**Translation:** There was a man surnamed Cheng in Hanshan, Hezhou, who lost his sight at a young age. While traveling, he encountered a mysterious person who taught him the method of water divination. Cheng used this method to make decisions for others, often with remarkable accuracy. His technique was somewhat unique: he would place a bowl of water on a table, write symbols and incantations on it, and then observe as foam rose to the surface, coalescing into characters. Once one set of characters formed, another would appear. If someone did not recognize a character, it would reappear as before. This process could repeat dozens of times, sometimes forming poems or cryptic responses, all of which provided deep insight into the subtlest aspects of a person. Another tale involves a man named Mao Ba, who, in his youth, traveled to Jiangxi to sell paper. The region was known for its many paper factories nestled in the mountains. The workers at these factories would lock their doors as the sun set, warning each other not to venture out, saying, "There are many strange creatures in the mountains, not just tigers and wolves." One night, the moon was exceptionally bright, and Mao Ba could not sleep. He thought about opening the door to enjoy the moonlight, hesitating repeatedly but ultimately deciding that his courage and strength would be enough to protect him. He opened the door and stepped out. After walking only a few dozen steps, he suddenly saw a group of dozens of monkeys running and crying, all climbing up a large tree. Mao Ba climbed another tree nearby to observe from a distance. Soon, he saw a snake emerge from the forest. Its body was as thick as a pillar, with two glaring eyes. Its scales were hard like fish scales, and below its waist, it had nine tails, which it dragged along as it moved, making a sound like iron armor. When it reached the tree, it planted its tail upside down and began to rotate in a dancing manner. At the end of each tail was a small hole from which it shot saliva, which hit the tree like bullets. Any monkey that was hit would scream, fall to the ground, and die with its belly split open. The snake then slowly ate three monkeys and slithered away with its tails. Frightened, Mao Ba returned home and, from that day on, dared not go out at night.

Scorpions and Geckos: The Poisonous Essence According to Liu Yixuan, scorpions and geckos, known as *shè hǔ* (蝎虎), are identified as *shǒu gōng* (守宫). He states that their essence, or ejaculate, is extremely poisonous. If a person accidentally consumes it, they cannot come into contact with water. Even a single drop of water on the body, no matter where it is, can dissolve human flesh and bones into water. There was once a case in southern China where two boys returned from school. Their mother cooked dried winter vegetables with meat as a meal for them. It was the hot summer, and after eating, the boys went to bathe. They did not emerge from the bath for a long time, which prompted their family to investigate. Upon looking, they found only blood water in the tub, with the boys' flesh and bones dissolved. The community was shocked and confused about the cause of the incident. They then checked the stored dried vegetables and discovered two large geckos mating on the vegetables, with their essence spilling into the food. This revealed that the boys had been mistakenly fed the contaminated vegetables. However, when examining *The Book of Nurturing Life*, it is advised that during the summer, cold tea left overnight should not be consumed. Geckos are known to be sexually promiscuous and will mate when they encounter water. This behavior could lead to their essence contaminating the tea. However, ancient texts do not mention that the gecko's essence can dissolve human bones.

A Strange Thunderstorm in Wancheng On the first day of the eighth month in the fifty-sixth year of the Qianlong reign, at midday, a dark cloud approached from the southeast, blocking the river and hovering just a few feet above the ground. Shortly after, thunder and lightning erupted, followed by heavy rain and wind. From noon until late evening, there were dozens of thunderclaps, causing houses to shake. A flash of lightning caused a sharp sound in the window paper, and at that moment, everyone was filled with fear, unable to predict what would happen next. The next morning, it was discovered that lightning had struck several locations, including the flagpole in front of the governor's office, which was split in half, with jagged edges resembling the teeth of a comb, about three to four inches deep. A large snake, about ten feet long, was found dead in a pool in front of the gunpowder depot. Many other walls and roofs were damaged, with beams and rafters collapsing in numerous places. A fisherman named You had dreamed several days earlier that someone had asked to be hidden in his home. He declined, citing lack of space. The next morning, he saw a creature resembling a monkey with green claws and red fur about two feet long, sitting on the roof ridge and moving the tiles. There were no other unusual occurrences. On that day, when the thunderstorm began, neighbors saw multiple lightning bolts resembling golden ropes coiling around You's house. Next to the house was an old willow tree with a hollow trunk, whose bark was almost completely stripped off by the lightning. The tree trunk split open, as if it had been pounded flat on the ground, and inside were black coal-like substances, suggesting it had been burned. It is thought that the creature was hit by lightning while hiding in the willow tree, and the lightning struck the tree to take it away. However, the true nature of the creature remains unknown.

After several days, a messenger came from Huangpu, saying that the thunder on that day was unusually quiet; another messenger came from Tongcheng, but when asked, he did not know about it. Huangpu is thirty miles from Wan, while Tongcheng is one hundred miles away, and the difference is as such. **Chapter Nine, Section Zero: The Embroidery Maidens of the Heavenly Queen in Qinghe County** In Qinghe County, there were families with the surnames Wang, Liu, and Yan. Three women from these families were all intelligent and beautiful, with similar appearances. The Wang family’s daughter married into the Wang clan, the Liu family’s daughter married into the Yan clan (the sister of the Yan woman), and the Yan family’s daughter married into the Wangjiaying clan, whose family was quite wealthy. In the 51st year of the Qianlong era, the Yan woman fell seriously ill and told her husband, “I and the Wang woman from the same county, as well as my sister-in-law, were all embroidery maidens of the Heavenly Queen at the temple by the river. We were demoted for some reason, but our term has now ended, and we are returning. The other two women will also be going with us.” Her husband went to inquire with the Wang and Liu families, and indeed, both the Wang and Liu women were also gravely ill. Soon after, the Yan woman died, followed by the Wang woman. The Yan woman’s mother, upon hearing of her daughter’s death and seeing her daughter-in-law on the brink of death, was greatly afraid. She hurried to the Heavenly Queen’s temple and wept, praying, “My daughter has already passed away, and now I have only one daughter-in-law left. If she dies, how will I survive? I beg you to spare her so that I may live out my remaining days.” Soon after, the Liu woman’s illness indeed improved.

After a year, Liu suddenly became pregnant. On the night before giving birth, she dreamed of the Heavenly Empress, who said, "Because your grandmother is old, I have temporarily allowed you to remain in the mortal world. How could you allow a child to be born?" She then touched her abdomen. In the morning, her abdomen was flat like that of an ordinary person. Previously, Liu had been a woman who, from childhood until she married Yan, would lock her door for one or two days each month, all night long, and no one was allowed to see her. Those who secretly listened heard as if several people were talking and laughing, until dawn. The family persistently questioned her, but she never spoke about it, and they only understood it now. She still exists today. Fei Songtao from Dai Prefecture was living temporarily in Qinghe and witnessed this event. ○ The Goddess of Peach Blossom Spring A girl from Zheng's family in Taoyuan County was born with a dignified and neat appearance and rarely spoke or laughed. When she reached the age of coming-of-age, one day she told her mother, "I will die on a certain day, and after death, I will become the deity of a certain village. That place will worship me in a temple." Her mother thought she was crazy and did not believe her. When the time came, she fell slightly ill and died after several days. She died while sitting upright, and her face and appearance were as if she were alive. Strange fragrances were heard in the room, and the appearance of clouds and flags, and the sound of wind and horses, were seen vaguely by the family. A few days later, the men and women of a certain village dreamed of the girl the same day, and she told them, "I will be worshipped here with blood offerings and will bring blessings to you." The residents considered her a divine being, donated money to create a statue, and named it the "Nanny Temple," which became quite famous for its miraculous powers. This occurred in the 34th year of the Qianlong reign.

R once had a maid named Li, with whom she was very close. When R became a deity, she would summon Li several times a month, taking her in a sedan chair to the temple, where she would sleep all day and return awake. If the deity wanted to keep her, she would be forcibly taken back, and even ten people could not lift the sedan chair. After Li married, she still went to the summons as usual. In the winter of the fifty-first year, Li told her husband, "The Lady has ordered me to go on the twelfth month's certain day, and I will not return." Her husband, who never believed in deities, merely agreed. On the day, Li bathed and burned incense. She sent someone to summon her husband for a final farewell. Her husband did not come, and Li angrily said, "You have wasted my time! Change it to the first month of next year." When her husband returned, he heard she was not dead and thought it was a lie. In the following year, Li summoned her husband again to say goodbye. Her husband angrily said, "She is playing tricks again!" and returned to see if she was dead. When he returned, Li was talking and laughing as usual, giving him a few instructions about household matters, and then she leaned against the table and closed her eyes, passing away. ○ In the fifty-sixth year of the Qianlong era, several days before the autumn sacrifice at the Anqing prefectural school, the ritual vessels were cleaned and prepared, and the offerings were arranged in the Minglun Hall. At night, someone was sent to guard them. There was a strong and brave porter named Tian who took on the task alone. That night, under a faint moon, Tian woke up at the third watch and heard two people whispering. He opened his eyes and saw two people climbing the steps toward his bed. Tian leaped up and shouted, and the two people rushed forward to fight. Tian fought hard and caught one, throwing him down the steps, where he let out a loud cry and transformed into a fox before running away; the other continued to fight, and Tian caught him as well, throwing him down, where he also transformed into a fox and ran away. Tian thought they would not come again and went to bed.

Not yet ripe, a sudden loud crowd was heard approaching. He rushed up and saw an elderly man with completely white beard and eyebrows, walking bent over, leading a group of about ten young men, shouting orders to strike someone named 田. 田 was enraged and fiercely punched the crowd, causing everyone to fall immediately, with no one able to resist. The old man angrily said, "How despicable!" and leaped, striking 田's left side with his head. The impact was as painful as hitting a large rock, unbearable pain, causing 田 to fall to the ground and be unable to get up. The old man shouted for the group to quickly drag 田 to the left side of the back room. 田 thought that going there would surely mean death. He saw a large bell hanging on a frame to the right of the hall. With the help of others, he moved quickly and unexpectedly under the frame. Using one elbow to hold onto the frame and one hand to resist. The old man became very angry and forcefully pulled 田's elbow. 田 was afraid and firmly held on with both hands. The old man's strength was great, dragging 田 and the frame several feet. The bell rang loudly, and the old man trembled and stopped. He ordered the foxes to attack 田, causing him to lose all his skin from head to toe and vomit several liters of blood. Until dawn, they left, and 田 also collapsed and lost consciousness. When morning came, the person in charge entered, saw the scene, was greatly frightened, poured hot water over 田, and after a long time, 田 regained consciousness and told the beginning and end of the story. They then realized it was a fox spirit, and the next night, they gathered more than ten people to guard against it. No one dares to sleep, sitting until the fourth watch, seeing nothing. Just as they are about to sleep, they hear the sound of running, and they open their eyes and look up. They hear an old man ask if 田 is there, and the group checks and says no. The old man says, "Fortunately, he escaped, go away," and then everything becomes quiet again.

Tian lay ill for more than a month and soon recovered. After his recovery, he wanted to carry a knife and stay in the hall to take revenge, but his wife strongly opposed it, and he stopped. ○ Ghosts of Hunan Examination Hall, Qianlong Bǐnwǔ Exam. During the autumn examination in Hunan, Li Zhou official Feng Mingting was commissioned to patrol the examination hall. On the third day, the fourteenth night, Feng and his colleague Li sat together in the public hall, where Li was just leaning on the table to sleep. That night, the moonlight was dim. Feng saw something long more than two zhang downstairs, with a waist and abdomen like a drum, the whole body covered with hair, and two eyes flickering like torches. It came out from the west examination hall and slowly entered the east examination hall. Feng was usually brave and not afraid. When he first saw it, he whispered to Li. Li woke up, looked up, and was greatly surprised, then fell to the table. After the object left, he got up, and they went to the sleeping place together. Feng ordered the servants to sleep in the same room. Feng thought Li was timid. After lying down, he intentionally knocked on the wall and hit the bed to scare him for fun.
