Just as they were laughing, suddenly there was a loud howl that lasted for a long time before stopping. Everyone was trembling with fear and covered their heads with blankets. A little while later, they heard a loud human sound. Feng and Li both got up with their clothes on, and the chief examiner and the two main examiners all got up. They sent people to investigate inside and outside. Near and far, no one did not hear it, and all were greatly surprised. At that time, seventeen or eighteen recommendation scrolls had been determined for the first round. The two main examiners reviewed again and eliminated seven scrolls. Later, there were no other strange occurrences. Is it because these seven people did not qualify that caused such strange occurrences?

In a village in Chuzhou, there was an elderly woman from the Huang family who was sitting alone in her room. In the afternoon, a sudden storm of rain and wind arrived. Suddenly, a thunderclap sounded, and various objects under the left wall all moved to the room, standing four or five feet away from the wall. The white mud on the wall, which was no thicker than three inches, also stood upright, four or five feet away from the wall, like a wall itself, without any damage. The woman was shocked and unconscious for a long time before regaining consciousness. She did not know what had been hit, and there were no other strange occurrences in her family. In an inn in Dazhou, two guests were staying together. One morning, they got up early, and there was a strong wind with light rain. One guest was sitting on the earthen bed with a large earthenware basin covering him, while the other guest was sitting on the threshold and talking to him. The guest sitting on the threshold suddenly looked up and saw a two-inch flame in the deep part of the house, like a small snake jumping. He quickly called out to the guest on the earthen bed to look. Before the other could answer, a thunderclap sounded, and a piece of the roof was torn off. Everyone rushed inside and saw one person lying stiff on the ground, while the other was sitting firmly on the earthen bed, not moving. Upon closer inspection, the latter was already dead, with a hole as small as a bean on his head. Initially, they suspected it was struck by lightning, but looking up, they saw the roof tiles flying outward, not falling from above. They moved the body and saw that the bottom of the basin he was sitting on also had a hole as small as a bean. They lifted the basin and saw that the earthen bed also had a hole. It turned out that the lightning had started from the ground, passed through the earthen bed, the basin, and the body, and then broke through the roof. The person on the ground was revived by pouring hot water and survived.

A Man Transformed into a Fish Zhi Hua, serving as the Deputy Commissioner of Huainan, was traveling through Kuizhou on his way to deliver military supplies and pay to Sichuan. Along the road, people were shouting and making a commotion, as if the entire country was in a frenzy. When asked about it, they said: "A woman from a certain village named Xu, who was sleeping with her husband, very much loved each other. In the morning, her face and skin remained the same, but her lower body had already transformed into a fish shape. Below her breasts, there were scales and a slimy, fishy texture. Her mouth could still speak, and her appearance was also ordinary, but she only wept and wailed, saying: 'When I was sleeping, I had no other pain, only itching in my lower body. I scratched it, and it gradually became bumpy, as if it would turn into scabies. Unbeknownst to me, after the fifth watch, my two feet merged together, unable to stretch or bend. I rubbed it, and it had already turned into a fish tail. What shall I do now?' The couple hugged each other and cried." Zhi Hua sent his family members to see it, and it was indeed true. Due to the urgency of official duties, he could not stay, and he did not know if the authorities would release her into the river after reporting the incident, or if they would keep her at home. He did not have time to ask further.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Example 2 **Source:** 兩頭四臂女 靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。 **Translation:** A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Example 3 **Source:** 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。 **Translation:** In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. --- ### Example 4 **Source:** 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。 **Translation:** From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. --- ### Example 5 **Source:** 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。 **Translation:** As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. --- Source text: ○韩昌黎称老相公韩文公为贡院土地。庚子岁，有嘉兴秀才陈效曾者，先试前数日入庙，庙祝令拜。生曰：“昌黎者何拜之为？学不足师，文不足师！”祝强之，大诟而出。试毕，归家而死。 殓数日矣，其妻惧，与小姑合被而寝。夜半，小姑登厕，忽见兄排户搴嫂帷帐而入。嫂奔出，姑大呼，家人凑集，而嫂之声音状貌俨然兄矣，大声曰：“我效曾也，身何在？”家人曰：“殓矣。”狂奔至棺所，扣棺而哭曰：“我得罪老相公，相公之门人家仆锁我厅事，俟老相公科场事毕，当放我。昨老相公放榜出，责我二十板，我得归，何殓我之速也？”又大哭，家人曰：“老相公何人也？”曰：“土地。”何人也？“曰：”韩昌黎。“客曰：”昌黎，伯也。依今时称谓，当曰伯爷；依家人称之，当曰老爷；乃冥中仅称老相公。“ Provide only the final translation with no additional commentary.

In the Fragrant Hills of the capital, there was a woman who was the wife of a soldier. She lived with her sister-in-law and mother-in-law. The sister-in-law was promiscuous and would set up a chamber pot at the back door to entice passersby, exposing herself, and would invite them in for sexual intercourse. This went on for many years. One day, the sister-in-law and mother-in-law were waiting at the back door. A butcher pushing a small cart passed by the alley and stopped to relieve himself. His genitals were much larger than those of the men she had previously encountered, and the sister-in-law was overjoyed. She welcomed him into the house and led him to her bed, where she undressed him and began to have intercourse with him. The mother-in-law sat beside her and watched until the act was completed, then wanted to have her turn. The butcher had a long-lasting performance, from noon until the end of the afternoon, he finally finished, and was very hungry, so he asked for food. The sister-in-law quickly prepared food for him. The mother-in-law thought it was her turn next and also loosened her clothes, touching the butcher's genitals, and began to suck and lick him. The butcher's performance resumed. The sister-in-law said, "The butcher is very persistent, you might not be able to handle it, let me take over." The mother-in-law agreed, and they both got into bed. The sister-in-law continued to have intercourse with the butcher, while the mother-in-law was so aroused that she ended up with water running down her feet. She was angry that the sister-in-law had deceived her, so she went to another room and hanged herself. The mother-in-law's husband's family took the matter to court, claiming she died because the sister-in-law had tormented her, but they did not know how shameful the act was. The sister-in-law's own husband, who was a street watchman, returned home and saw that his wife looked uneasy and the bedding was dirty. He then privately interrogated her severely and finally learned the truth, and reported it to the authorities.

This case was presided over by the Fujian Division of the Ministry of Punishments during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor in the year 1776. Upon completion of the trial, the court found that the confession contained obscene and indecent content, which was deemed unfit for official records. The court compared the case to a less serious offense and sentenced the defendant to eighty strokes of the cane. ○ A farmer from the Xinfang Bridge area in the northwest of Yixing discovered an object while plowing his field. The object was circular, resembling a compass, with a circumference of over two feet. The outer edge was a deep purple color, somewhat like jade but not quite. Inside, there was a white stone that was transparent and clear, resembling crystal but not exactly. It stood upright like a lid. The farmer sold it to a pharmacy in the eastern part of the town for eight hundred coins. A traveler from the west of the pond later bought it for ten thousand coins and took it to Chongming Island, where he sold it for one thousand seven hundred taels of silver. A sea merchant remarked, "This is a mirror that reflects the sea. It can show strange fish and all kinds of reefs in the dark waters, allowing one to avoid dangers up to a hundred miles away."

### Translation ○ The Reverend Shen, a secretary from Huzhou, known as Nèi ān, possessed a grain-like Buddha statue, enclosed in a glass box. The box was half an inch long, with a base about two-tenths of an inch high, containing a large grain about one and a half inches long. The grain had an awn, also about an inch long. There was a hole beside the grain; when viewed with one eye in bright daylight, the hole appeared larger and larger like a door. Gazing at it for a long time, one could see a hall through the door, then a hall, and then a temple, revealing a statue of the Three Jewels of the Buddha. The statue was several zhang tall, adorned with intricate ornaments, showing a large character pattern on its chest. Two statues of Manjusri and Samantabhadra stood beside it, as if they were speaking. Suddenly, with a blink, the illusion disappeared, leaving only the grain. According to Shen, this object was passed down to a certain official's family in Huzhou. It was brought to China by Li Xi Gong during the Ming dynasty from the Western region of Mowalarni. In that country, during the autumn harvest, this grain appeared in the fields, causing vast areas of land to turn red and barren. One of his disciples, Wang Tan, personally saw this grain but does not know where it is now. ○ In Dan Tu, there was an unusual incident. The magistrate of Dan Tu County, named Zhang Ming Zhen Gang, was called out by his attendants. Suddenly, an object fell from the sky and landed on the canopy of his sedan chair. As the sedan chair moved briskly in the wind, the object suddenly fell into his robe and began jumping around. Startled, he looked inside and saw a male organ, about two inches long. He quickly got out of the sedan chair and ordered his attendants to catch it, but it kept jumping, drawing a crowd. He then took it back to the treasury and searched for it everywhere, but it could not be found.

After one month, a woman named Wang Dalang, who carried water from the west gate, reported that a mother-in-law and daughter-in-law from a certain household had committed murder. They were promptly arrested and interrogated. It turned out that the two women had previously had relations with a man from Shaanxi, and later with a man surnamed Chen, leading to mutual infidelity. The husband then murdered the man from Shaanxi, dismembered his body, and buried it, ensuring that the body could not be identified as male or female. In the rush, he cut off the genitalia and threw the body parts out of a window. Unexpectedly, they fell into the sedan chair of the county magistrate. When the officials were informed, they all laughed heartily. According to the law for conspirators, the mother-in-law, daughter-in-law, and the adulterer were all executed together. **○ Ghosts Fear Collecting Debts** In Changzhou, a poor man died, and his house was sold to a wealthy family. The ghost of the poor man caused mischief, and the wealthy person locked it up for several decades. Later, the wealthy person also became poor, sold the large house, and moved elsewhere. Suddenly, the ghost of the poor man caused a big disturbance, demanding money and offerings, and made the entire family sick. By the end of winter, the landlord owed the most debt, and debt collectors came into the house, shouting and cursing day and night. The ghost suddenly stopped, and the sick people all recovered. By the next year, the debt was somewhat cleared, and the accounts were burned. The ghost again loudly complained during the day, saying, "I saw a lot of debt collectors last year, and I suspected it was my old debts from my lifetime, so I avoided them. Now reading the burned accounts, all of your accumulated debts, have nothing to do with me, why should I avoid them!" Therefore, it threw bricks and set fires, and the bad words became more severe each day. The landlord also moved away and no longer lived there.

Lan Zhi Mountain, the Northern Immortal of Kuaiji On Lan Zhi Mountain in Kuaiji, there stands a Daoist temple known as the Lanting Temple, which is the abode of the Northern Immortal. The Northern Immortal is a fox spirit. In his youth, Chen Jia from Kuaiji traveled to Chu, where he lost his capital, fell into poverty, and was unable to support himself, and he was also ill, living in an abandoned temple. One night, a lady arrived, her appearance was extremely beautiful, and her clothes were dazzling, adorned with pearls. Chen Jia was startled and rose. The lady removed a bracelet from her arm and gave it to him, saying, "I know you are in need, so I have come to offer you this." She then left. The next day, she returned again. This continued for several months, during which their relationship became harmonious and intimate, and their feelings grew stronger each day. Chen Jia used the gold bracelet to gradually regain his capital and restart his old business, while the lady also bought a new home, managed her household affairs, and daily provided him with a vast amount of gold, silver, and jewels, amounting to tens of thousands.

Lan Zhi Mountain, the Northern Immortal of Kuaiji On Lan Zhi Mountain in Kuaiji, there stands a Daoist temple known as the Lanting Temple, which is the abode of the Northern Immortal. The Northern Immortal is a fox spirit. In his youth, Chen Jia from Kuaiji traveled to Chu, where he lost his capital, fell into poverty, and was unable to support himself, and he was also ill, living in an abandoned temple. One night, a lady arrived, her appearance was extremely beautiful, and her clothes were dazzling, adorned with pearls. Chen Jia was startled and rose. The lady removed a bracelet from her arm and gave it to him, saying, "I know you are in need, so I have come to offer you this." She then left. The next day, she returned again. This continued for several months, during which their relationship became harmonious and intimate, and their feelings grew stronger each day. Chen Jia used the gold bracelet to gradually regain his capital and restart his old business, while the lady also bought a new home, managed her household affairs, and daily provided him with a vast amount of gold, silver, and jewels, amounting to tens of thousands.

After several years, a message from Jia's family suddenly arrived. Jia wanted to show off to his hometown and also suspected the woman of being a fox spirit. One day, when the woman was not at home, Jia suddenly called hundreds of people and servants to leave the house in an orderly manner. When the woman returned, she found the house completely empty. She pursued Jia to the river mouth, but by then, Jia had already raised the sails and was singing and shouting. The woman cried by the river, unable to cross, and Jia thus returned as a wealthy man. Ten years later, the woman appeared and called out to Jia, saying, "I am a fox spirit. I have accumulated good deeds over a thousand years and have been recorded in the books of immortals. Now you have broken your heart, and I have reported it to the Heavenly Emperor. He has ordered the river god to deliver a written command to this place. You should die now." She then threw knives and set fire, causing unrest in Jia's household. Despite trying every means to appease her, it was ineffective. One day, the woman sighed in the air and said, "I am doing this because of our past affection. If I let you die, I fear that people in love will laugh at us. If your family can perform grand rituals to appease me and choose a famous mountain to house my spirit, I will forgive you." At that time, there was a Taoist priest on Lan Mountain who was known for his high level of spiritual practice. They performed rituals for forty-nine days. The Taoist priest asked the woman, "Why don't you live on Lan Mountain?" The woman replied, "That would be good, but I must stay for five hundred years before I can leave." From then on, the situation ceased.

The Taoist temple is now the property of the Luo family, and the Luo family made a very beautiful statue for it. The woman also goes out at night seasonally to talk with people. A scholar from Hangzhou prayed for a dream at Wen Su Temple. Just as he was sleeping, a fierce ghost arrived carrying a kidney bag, as large as a jar, and said, "If you want to pass the imperial examination, you should eat this, otherwise you will not pass." The scholar was afraid and struggled to eat it. At first, the taste was very sweet, like a jujube, and in a short time, all the thick skin on all sides was eaten completely. Only the two kidney pills could not be swallowed because the teeth could not break through them. The ghost said, "Discard it, you have already passed." The scholar was happy, but from then on, his outcomes were repeatedly criticized. Until the list was posted in the 17th year of Qianlong, the scholar became the top scorer, and then suddenly understood. It turns out that in Zhejiang, the kidney is called an egg, 'Gui' (癸) is the character for the ghost, and 'Mao' (卯) is the character for the egg without the core.

The Deity of Yang and the Midwife Afeng In the temples of Yang, located in the towns and villages of Jiaxing, the deity of Yang is often invoked for its effectiveness. The midwife Afeng, renowned for her skill in delivering babies, had become wealthy through her profession. Families near and far would invite her to ensure a safe delivery, and without her, many would face difficult labors. One snowy night, someone knocked on her door. "Who comes?" she asked. "From Cold Water Bay, the Yang family is welcoming a newborn son. The master has sent me to summon you; you should hurry to the boat." Afeng donned her fur coat and went with her servant to the boat. Indeed, they arrived at Cold Water Bay. The mansion was grand and beautiful. Upon entering, the master stood at the veranda, overjoyed to see Afeng. He ordered his servant to lead her to the inner hall. There, the mother lay in bed, calling out in pain. The maids and servants stood holding lamps, all looking grim and saying, "My lady has been in labor for four days." Afeng examined her and found that the intestines were entangled in the fetus, preventing it from descending quickly. Using her method, she delivered the fetus with a single pull. Reporting to the master, he rewarded her with two gold ingots. Afeng accepted them, saying, "I will come back in three days." It was snowing heavily outside, but the room was very warm. Afeng took off her clothes and began the work. When she got to the boat, she remembered she had left her outer clothes behind. When she returned home, it was already morning, and she saw that the gold ingots were actually made of paper. But her fur coat had already been sent home. Thus, the villagers held a three-day celebration for the deity, performing rituals and inviting gods from various temples to join the festivities.

### Final Translation #### 溺壶失节 A Westerner named Zhang served as the magistrate of Rugao. His private secretary, Wang Gongnan from Hangzhou, shared a boat with him one day. One night, Wang Gongnan borrowed Zhang's urinal, which Zhang became extremely angry about. He shouted, "In our Western custom, the urinal is considered as a wife or concubine. How can it contain something so inappropriate that I can allow others to misuse it? You are extremely disrespectful!" Immediately, he ordered his servant to beat the urinal thirty times with a stick, threw it into the water, and cast Wang Gongnan's luggage onto the shore, then sailed away. #### 三虎索命 The General of Pacification, Yan Sheng, had two daughters, both of whom were exceptionally beautiful. One married the fourth son of Li Minda, the Grand Secretary, named Xingyao, and the other married the son of Li Shao, the Lesser Official of Ceremonies, named Shouqian. In the spring of the year Renxu during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, I met the historian at Tiger Hill. We briefly discussed past events, and he said, "Strange! The death of my wife. After our marriage, we lived in harmony for nearly three years. Suddenly, one day, she prepared a banquet in the inner chamber and made a farewell gesture towards me, saying, 'In my previous life, I was a household where I killed three tigers. Their spirits have not dispersed and are coming to seek revenge. Now I am pregnant, and next year, during the year of the Tiger, I will give birth. Since the year of the Tiger is associated with tigers, I fear I cannot escape.' I asked, 'How do you know this?' She replied, 'Last night, I dreamed of a divine being wearing golden armor and a tiger crown who told me this. Among the three tigers I killed, two had harmed people, so Heaven does not allow them to seek revenge; but one tiger did not harm anyone, so it is permitted to seek revenge.' After she finished speaking, she wept uncontrollably. The following year, she indeed died due to complications during childbirth."

Liang Wendong, the Duke of Liang, fell gravely ill and dreamed of arriving at a place where the palace was grand and majestic, and the seated guests were all unfamiliar to him. After talking for a while, he suddenly wanted to smoke but found no fire. Someone pointed to a hall and said, "There is fire in there." A divine figure in the middle of the hall beckoned to Liang and said, "Wait a moment before smoking. I have a riddle for you to solve." He wrote the seven characters "三代之英汝继泰" (The three generations of heroes, you will follow Tai). Liang was startled and woke up, calling his disciples to come and interpret the dream for him. None of them could understand it. After a long time, he said, "I will not recover. The three refer to the three Zhongtang (treasures); Ying refers to Ying Zhongtang (integrity); Tai refers to Wu Zhongtang (peaceful). The three officials held the same rank as I and all died. Will I follow them? Hasten the arrangements for after my death." Three days later, he died.

Mount Tianmu is known for its numerous monkeys. Those who wish to offer food to the monkeys first go to the Vajrapani Temple to burn incense and make vows, saying, "On a certain day, I will come to the mountain to offer food to the monkeys." The temple monks hang a sign to inform the public. On the appointed day, the host buys a thousand steamed buns and places them on the ground outside the temple. Early in the morning, all the monkeys gather, and among them is an extremely old one with a white beard about a foot long, coming in a waving and bent-over manner, accompanied by two other white-bearded monkeys. The old monkey is supported by the others, and the monkeys kneel to welcome him. The old monkey sits facing south on the ground, and the other monkeys bow their hands and sit down, creating a quiet and solemn atmosphere, not daring to make noise. Two servants carry steamed buns to offer to the old monkey, who eats first, and then the other monkeys eat together. After eating, they bow their hands and thank the host before leaving. Mr. Liang Lusu, a scholar-official, personally witnessed the event. I wanted to go to offer food, but because the path was dangerous and the grass was deep, I did not dare to go.

In the year 1761 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, at Huqiu in Suzhou, a beggar kept a bear that was as large as a river horse, with fur standing erect like arrows. The bear could write and recite Tang poems but could not speak. Those who came to see it paid one coin for each look, and if they requested writing on plain paper, the bear would write a Tang poem in large characters, for which they paid one hundred coins. One day, when the beggar went out, the bear was left alone. Another person came and asked for a piece of paper to write on. The bear wrote: "I am a teacher from a village in Changsha, my name is Jin Ruli. When I was young, this beggar and his companions caught me. They first gave me a drug to make me mute. They kept a dog bear at home. They stripped me naked and tied me up, then stabbed me all over with needles, causing my blood to flow profusely. When the blood was still hot, they killed the dog bear, skinned it, and wrapped it around me. Human and dog blood mixed and stuck together tightly, never to come off. They chained me with an iron chain to deceive people, and now they have made tens of thousands of coins." After writing, the bear pointed to its mouth, and tears fell like rain. The crowd was greatly shocked, and they arrested the beggar and sent him to the authorities. According to the law against mutilation and killing for profit, he was executed immediately by beating. The bear was then escorted to Changsha and delivered to its original owner.

I have examined the year of Jiwei, when an official in the capital seduced a servant woman, who bit off the tip of his tongue. A Mongol doctor arrived and ordered the killing of a dog to take its tongue, attaching it with hot blood and warning him to stay indoors for a hundred days. Later, when he was summoned, he answered as before. A certain general from the Yuan dynasty suffered countless wounds from swords and arrows in battle, and blood gushed out, causing him to lose consciousness. The imperial physician ordered the killing of a horse, cut open its belly, and placed the general inside the horse's abdomen, then had dozens of people shake it. After a meal's time, the general stood up covered in blood. Both cases follow the same principle. ○ Lightning Fragment Two men from Wu, Cai Mingxi and Xu Peiyu, were cousins. They traveled together from Chu on a boat carrying ramie fibers. On the night of the 13th day of the 9th lunar month in the 5th year of the Qianlong era (1740), they anchored in Jiujiang. A heavy thunderstorm occurred. Cai, being timid, covered himself with a blanket and lay down. There was a copper rice pot on a tripod, which was shaking and about to fall. Xu got up and moved it. He saw lightning strike directly below, dazzling his eyes. A loud thunderclap struck, and the ship's rudder was torn off, causing water to flood in. The boatmen all got up and pulled the boat to the shore. In the darkness, they moved things around. As dawn approached, they saw a piece of wood stuck in Xu's head, about three to four inches long and over an inch in circumference. The others were shocked and asked Xu. Xu did not feel any pain or discomfort, as if it had always been there. It was heavy and uncomfortable, making it hard to bear even for a moment.

A man on the neighboring boat was skilled in spells and incantations. He said, "This is a fragment of thunder. If someone is innocent and accidentally touches it, I can remove it." Xu was very pleased. Cai was concerned that it might be false and reported it to the county magistrate. The magistrate came to the riverbank to examine it. The man wrote a spell on Xu's head, muttered softly, raised his hand and pulled once, and the wood came out with his hand. Then he wrote another spell on a small yellow paper and stuck it on the wound. The wood that had been embedded in Xu's head was more than an inch long and was as sharp as a cone. Some said that it could ward off evil spirits. The magistrate thought it should be recorded and took it away. The next day, the paper on Xu's head fell off by itself, and he was just as good as before. Strange emotions, strange events, strange skills, strange people—what does not exist! ○ The Niu Fen River in Linwu County is often turbulent and dangerous. Thirty miles southeast of the county is a place called Niu Tou Fen, named after the shape of the mountain. The area is rich in fish, and the water rushes violently, making it difficult to use nets. People usually throw white pigeon droppings into the water, and the fish all float stiffly to the surface. Then they either ride small boats or go naked and follow the current to pick them up.

One night, two men returned from a drinking session and walked along the shore. They saw a large fish floating on the surface of the water. One of them happily told his companion, "Why don't we wait a bit? I will bring this fish back." He then took off his clothes and entered the water. After a long time, both the man and the fish were silent. They were surprised that he had drowned, so they quickly searched for Zhang, a villager who was skilled at swimming, and asked him to go into the water to search for the man. They promised a certain amount of gold as a reward. Zhang agreed, asked for some wine to drink, and immediately drank several jugs at once, getting so drunk that he could barely stand. He stepped onto a small boat to the place where the fish was floating, dove into the water, and swam several strokes, sometimes rising and sometimes sinking. He did this several times, struggling to climb onto the shore. He said, "I saw a man sitting in the sand, and whenever someone approached, he moved away. Quickly bring me some wine to drink, then I will go back and bring him along." He drank several more jugs and went back into the water. After a short while, the waves surged, and Zhang was seen catching a man by the hair, climbing onto the shore, and throwing him to the ground. He hit him with his hand and said, "You made me go back and forth several times, costing me so much effort. It is really hateful. Should I beat you?" With the help of the bystanders, they managed to free him, and they saw that the man was already dead, the same person who had asked for the fish the previous day. They paid him the agreed-upon amount of gold. Zhang laughed and said, "I drank heavily twice, and my stomach is full. If I use this method to trick people's money, how different is it from a water ghost who lures people?" He shook his head, waved his hand goodbye, and left. Perhaps Zhang was a remarkable man who hid in the water, they speculated.

On that day, Wu Gate's Gu Jun Langcun happened to pass by that place and personally witnessed it. The locals claim there is a dragon palace beneath it. Once, a young child accidentally fell into the water. He arrived at the gate of an official residence where two people were playing chess, their appearance strange and crab-like. They saw the child and were surprised, asking why he was there, and then sent him out of the water. The young child still exists today, now in his early thirties. He has often talked to people about this strange occurrence. --- In the southern part of Xinning County, there is a place called Huoshi Ridge. A man surnamed Tang, who lived a vegetarian lifestyle and recited Buddhist scriptures, was in his fifties when he suddenly died without any illness. Four days later, his chest was still warm, and his family did not dare to hastily bury him. Gradually, he regained consciousness, and they fed him soup and porridge, and he revived. He told his family, "I went out the other day and saw a Taoist wearing a patched robe and going barefoot, calling me to walk with him. I felt as though I had no control over my body. We walked several miles, and I heard the sound of rushing water. Soon, we reached a river that was so wide and vast that it could not be measured. A great bridge spanned the sky. People on the bridge saw the Taoist and laughed, calling out, 'The spirit has arrived.' I asked, 'Where are we?' They replied, 'The Yellow River.' After walking a few more miles, we came to a steep mountain. I asked, 'What mountain is this?' They answered, 'Yin Yang Mountain.' We crawled up the mountain, and the cliffs were jagged and strange, filled with awe-inspiring and mysterious sights. The atmosphere was dark and gloomy, with a narrow path just wide enough for a person to walk. On both sides were thorny bushes. I saw many people moving about among the thorns, as if searching for a path. Their skin was all injured by the thorns, and they were bleeding and crying. I was frightened and asked them what was happening."

The Taoist priest said, "Those who are honest, fair, and selfless will find this great path open before them; those who are cunning, deceitful, and hypocritical will only throw themselves into thorns and suffer in vain. It is because they did not follow the right path in life!" When the mountain came to an end, the sky was clear and the sun bright, and the city walls were in sight. The Taoist priest said, "This is the Peaceful City, where many people are gathered, all awaiting their fate." Suddenly, a clerk came forward with a tablet and called out, "Take thirty-six people away." The Taoist priest hurriedly called me into the city. There were many government offices in the city, all of which were quiet. Soon we arrived at one office, the sign above the door read "Mirror of Karma Office." They pulled me in through the east gate and made me stand under the eaves of the main hall. I saw a man sitting on a chair in the right wing, dressed in formal attire and wearing a hat, with a woman about seventeen or eighteen years old standing in front of him, pulling at him and weeping in complaint. Glancing at the man, I recognized him as the magistrate of Wu County from my hometown. I asked about it, and the Taoist priest said, "When Wu was a magistrate, there was a woman from the Chen family who remained loyal to her deceased husband. Her father wanted her to remarry, but she refused. Later, she brought a lawsuit against Wu. Wu saw that they were both handsome young men and thought they would certainly be a good match, so he ruled in favor of the match. The woman eventually hanged herself to death, and she is also here waiting to be judged."

After a while, I heard the sound of a procession, and a man ascended the hall to sit high, wearing a scholar's cap and a long robe, resembling the attire of a Daoist priest. Two rooms of officials and servants stood ready, the atmosphere being very solemn. I inquired secretly about his identity, and he replied, "This is the Chief Administrator of the Underworld." The Daoist priest bowed to pay respects, and they exchanged greetings and questions, though I could not understand their conversation. Soon, I was led to kneel and pay homage. The official on the seat asked, "Did you recite sutras while alive?" I responded, "Yes, I did." He then asked, "Which sutra did you recite?" I replied, "The *Diamond Sutra*." He said, "You are a good person. But how did you mispronounce 'sāmākā' as 'shāmākā'? Because of this mistake, you are punished with a year of life lost. Now, I call you here to quickly repent and restore your ten years of life in the mortal world. Go." I then bowed and stood up. A woman appeared before me, bowed, and recounted her story, which matched the Daoist priest's account. The official on the seat said, "You should have died in this manner." He threw an object shaped like a square measure from his desk and said, "Look at it yourself." The woman remained silent. He then added, "You have vowed to remain chaste. Now, I command you to be reborn in the Yan region, where you will receive rewards on a royal estate. Go." He then left the hall, and the sound of the cloud-shaped board and toad drum resembled the ceremonial procedures of the mortal world. When I looked back at the right side of the hall, Wu was no longer there.

Leaving Pingyang, I saw thirty-six people squatting and facing each other. A servant came up and fanned them with a large fan, causing flames to rise several meters high. After a moment, the fire subsided, and the thirty-six people were still there. The servant then took out a pearl from his bosom, as large as an egg, placed it on the ground, and fanned it again, causing a strong wind to suddenly arise. The thirty-six people disappeared without a trace. I was astonished and asked a hermit, who said, "The underworld's methods are not like those of the earthly realm. It uses this fire of yin and yang to eliminate evil beings, followed by a gale to scatter their remnants. Those who fall on land become insects and crustaceans, while those who fall into water become fish and shrimp. Those who have done good deeds will take another good path." I continued on my way and met a relative who was carrying a pig's skin on his back, crying, "I am unfortunate to have died in Lichuan and am now turning into a pig." Upon reaching my home, the hermit left, and I woke up without knowing I had died. I sent family members to visit Wu, who was indeed seriously ill, with cold extremities for several days, but has now recovered completely. When I inquired about the woman's matter, it turned out to be a case that occurred during the time of Blue Field. Shortly after, the relative's son came and reported that his father had indeed died on a certain day in Lichuan County. This incident took place in April of the twenty-second year of Qianlong. The Tang family still exists, and they described the events vividly. Wu, a scholar of the Gengzi year of Kangxi, served in Qin and lived in the back乡 of Xin County. I once visited his home, where his son, named Xie, was a local school student, skilled in poetry and prose, and knowledgeable in medicine. He also provided a detailed account of the events.

A Man Leading His Wife to the Underworld to See Her Grandparents During the reign of Emperor Qianlong, Lady Lu's husband, Family Thousand from Piling, died young. She remained alone, and during the summer, on the sixth day of the seventh month, she suddenly dreamed that her deceased husband led her to a door. The hall was quite like her old family home. Ascending the hall, she saw her in-laws all present, each of them both sad and happy. Soon, behind the screen came an old man with a beard and his wife, supporting themselves with canes. Family Thousand said, "These are your grandfather and grandmother; you have not seen them before, now you should pay your respects." Lady Lu knelt and paid her respects. The old man with a beard said, "When the granddaughter-in-law first sees me, there should be something to entertain her." His son replied that they were poor. The old man then reached into his bag and took out some silver, giving it to those on the side. Soon, dishes were arranged, and they sat around and ate together. The old man pointed to the meatballs in the dish and said to Family Thousand, "Why not take some to eat with the granddaughter-in-law?" Family Thousand suddenly looked solemnly at his grandfather, as if he thought it was not appropriate, and the old man then remained silent. After finishing the meal, Lady Lu asked, "Since I am here, should I see King Yama (阎王)?" The old man said, "You have no sins, there is no need to go see him." He then pointed to someone nearby and said to Lady Lu, "Tomorrow at戌时 (戌时 is a specific time in the Chinese time system), they will send a sedan chair to pick you up." She then woke up, describing what she saw of the old man with a beard and his wife, indeed their appearance and manner of speaking were as they were in life. As for the servants and maids who ran around and served, they were all deceased servants from her family, without a single mistake. Lady Lu said that in her dream, the entire family gathered and were very happy.

The next day on the Qixi Festival, he indeed saw two servants from his dream carrying a sedan chair to greet him, and he passed away as scheduled. The bearded old man, named Chun with the courtesy name Shunian, had once served as the magistrate of Shehong and was known for his straightforwardness throughout his life. His son, named Qianfu with the courtesy name Shijun, was also a sincere and good person. --- In the tenth chapter, the sins of lust and flattery are severely punished in the afterlife. An old servant named Zhu Ming, who died and then revived after one day, told people: "I was called to the underworld, where I was tasked with serving as a witness in a debt dispute from a past life. Both parties mutually accused each other, and my presence was necessary to clarify the matter. After presenting the facts in detail to Yama, the case was resolved, and I was allowed to return to the living world. As I left the palace, I saw a couplet on the column that read: 'A place of right and wrong, a day of clarity.' I admired it and considered it worthy of the mouth of a deity."

While hesitating, I saw a group of ghosts awaiting rebirth descending from the hall. Most were unfamiliar to me, but there was one woman and one old man, both of whom were my neighbors. The woman had engaged in immoral behavior, and the old man flattered wealthy families. I thought these two would surely fall into the Avici Hell. As the judge passed by, holding the rebirth register, he asked about them. The judge said, "The woman was very filial, so she will be reborn as a nobleman's son in Shanxi; the old man was very kind, so he will be reborn as a wealthy woman's daughter in Shandong." I was不服, saying, "I have always known that the woman was immoral, and the old man lacked character, yet both received favorable rebirths. Then how can the Hell Court be fair and clear?" The judge sighed and replied, "This is precisely why it is called fair and clear. Why? Because the misconduct of men and women in private is often unclear and ambiguous, so the laws of the mortal world stipulate: 'To catch an adulterer, both parties must be caught.' It also says: 'Only relatives may apprehend.' This is to prevent false accusations in the dark. The King of Hell is a dignified and upright deity, would he stoop to lying under someone's bed to spy on their private affairs? Moreover, since ancient times, the Duke of Zhou established rituals, and only then did the saying 'a woman should remain faithful to one husband until death' come into being."

Let me ask, before the time of Duke Zhou, who were the women who lost their virtue in the historical records of the Three Sovereigns, Five Emperors, and the Xia dynasty spanning over a thousand years? As for the poor and lowly, who could not make a living, and thus had to seek favors at powerful doors or rush to the homes of the wealthy, often being ridiculed by others, this was also an act of necessity. The saying goes, "He who follows the will of Heaven prospers," so what sin would prevent them from being reborn in a good place? Moreover, consider the ancients like Chen Taiqiu, who visited Zhang Rang to defuse a political crisis, or Kang Hai, who met Liu Jin to save Li Kōngdòng, humbling themselves to practice benevolence, their merits and virtues were especially great, and the Heavenly Deity recorded them among the Bodhisattvas, and they were rewarded with good fortune. As for those who committed grave sins by indulging in lust and causing the loss of life, or by flattery and harming innocent people, such actions are indeed great sins, and in the underworld, there hangs a mirror that reflects evil, clearly showing one's karmic obstacles, not just through accusations by enemies. "Zhu heard this and woke up with great realization, saying." The judge was also Zhu's cousin, named Qihong, who served as the magistrate of Huanggang Prefecture, and was known for his integrity in life.

Human life has a fixed limit, and the underworld cannot increase or decrease it. Cheng, from the six directions, was a man who did not usually believe in ghostly matters. At the age of sixty or so, he fell ill and could not get up, unable to eat solid food for more than forty days. Suddenly, one of his eyes told his wife, "I cannot get up from my illness, but my two grandchildren are getting married soon, and I will not be able to see my granddaughters-in-law. People will surely laugh at my lack of good fortune. Why not quickly arrange for me to see them, to comfort my heart?" His wife followed his words, and the two new brides came to bow before him at his bedside. Cheng's face brightened with joy, saying, "I can leave tomorrow. Please help me up at dawn and dress me in the shroud." The family brought a ceremonial robe, but he ordered it to be removed, saying, "I have never held an official position, and wearing this robe would surely be laughed at by the ghosts. I will wear my regular clothes instead." After dressing, after a long while, he said, "There are two people waiting outside. You should burn paper money and prepare wine and food for them." His wife asked, "Who are they?" He replied, "Yu Long and Jiang Xin." These two were already dead people who had once given their lives to serve as officials under the City God. After saying this, he fell into a deep sleep for nearly a day, and then suddenly woke up, saying, "Help me up, and take off the shroud. The City God's wife is celebrating her birthday, and there are many guests coming and going. There is no time to check names, so Yu and Jiang let me return. I will go back tomorrow and await my fate." He continued to drink pear juice and clear tea as before.

Two days later, after waking up, I ordered to get dressed and said, "This time I am truly leaving and will not return. Many of my children have gone to the City God to burn incense and borrow life for me. Some wish to reduce their lives by five years, others by ten years. Though it is their filial piety, it is rather amusing. A person's lifespan is determined by fate. It is not like other things that can be transferred or swapped. But there is a strange thing: I saw the City God, and a woman I did not know was weeping and pleading on my behalf to let me return to the living. The City God shook his head and refused. I was greatly suspicious and questioned the two bailiffs: 'Who is this woman from which family?' They replied, 'It is Tang Li. Do you not remember the events of thirty-six years ago? Li married Tang and her husband died. This woman took care of her mother-in-law until her death and then adopted a son for her husband. After completing these tasks, she bowed before the spirit tablet and hanged herself to death. You valued her virtue and had someone teach Tang's younger brother to submit a petition for commendation. All the expenses were covered by you. Why do you not remember?' Cheng heard this and suddenly remembered it as if it happened yesterday. And realized that the City God's refusal was because a person's lifespan is determined by fate and cannot be increased or decreased by the City God. After saying this, he drank several cups of pear juice and then passed away. Cheng's son, named Shiquan, personally told me this."

A Scholar of Learning, Summoning a Deity A scholar of learning, who had summoned a deity, one day found that Guan Di appeared on the altar. He asked a question about a section of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, and the response on the divining board was clear and accurate. After writing, Guan Di left. When the scholar returned home, he secretly doubted, saying, "Guan Di's loyalty is as bright as the sun and moon, and his position is supreme. How can a single piece of paper summon him instantly?" Feeling greatly dissatisfied, he intended to draft a petition and burn it to heaven to file a complaint. As he was drafting the petition, he suddenly heard a knock on the door. When he opened the door, he saw no one. He became even angrier and picked up his pen to continue. Suddenly, someone appeared on his desk and said, "Sir, please slow down." The scholar asked, "Who are you?" The apparition replied, "I am the one who appeared on the altar. I was actually a scholar from the Tang Dynasty. I was killed by rebel soldiers, and my soul is trapped in the temple, where I clean the temple daily. The Holy Emperor pitied my hard work and ordered me to enjoy the sacrificial offerings in the temple, but I am not Guan Di." The scholar laughed and immediately wanted to burn the petition. The apparition on the desk said again, "Please, slow down." The scholar asked, "Why?" The apparition replied, "If you burn the petition, you are still complaining about me. Please, sir, put the petition in water and erase the writing, and only then will I be unharmed."

Someone asked, "Does the God of Guan really appear at the altar?" The answer was, "The God of Guan has only one form. All the blood offerings in the temples around the world are enjoyed by us, but only when the emperor offers a sacrifice does he appear at the altar." Someone asked, "How do you know this?" The answer was, "There was once a fox that had been cultivating for thousands of years and heard that the emperor was offering a sacrifice. One month before, it fasted and bathed, then went to spy. Seven months before, it saw the General Zhou appear at the altar to clean the altar room, and the room was filled with red light, burning all the demons and spirits to death. Therefore, it knew the time when the emperor offered a sacrifice, and the God of Guan appeared at the altar." The evil man was reborn as a turtle. In Yangzhou, there was a man surnamed Hu whose son was very intelligent and was about to be twenty years old. Several months before his marriage, he suddenly became insane, eating and sleeping irregularly, sometimes clear and sometimes confused, talking to himself and laughing. One day, while sitting on the bed, he said to his parents, "Last night, I was appointed by the God of the Mountains to be the local city god. There were ten unresolved cases in our county, and I was ordered to handle them fairly. I am afraid of making mistakes, so I need to invite a private secretary. After careful consideration, I think only my former teacher, who is known for his Confucianism and is trustworthy, can be invited. Please prepare the gifts quickly." At that time, the teacher had been dead for many years. After a short while, he suddenly stood up and said, "The teacher is here! The teacher is here!" He kept murmuring continuously. The family members listened, and it was like a conversation between two people. The tone of voice and manner of speaking were exactly like the usual, saying, "Out of the ten cases, seven cases still follow the previous agreement, and the remaining three cases, one should be decapitated, one should have his hand cut off, and one should be exiled to military service."

Due to the doctor's advice that his illness required nourishing yin, he bought a turtle and, at the stove, led its head and cut it off. The turtle's head fell to the ground, its eyes glaring angrily and fiercely, terrifying to behold. Despite being far from the bedroom, his son suddenly shouted from the bed, "This wicked man should be punished, and what is there for him to be不服? Even after being chopped off, it still dares to glare at me!" The family had not yet returned from praying at the City God Temple, and the son said again on the bed, "Why does the grandfather burn incense before the judge? How could he deserve the grandfather's bow?" All ten cases have names, and upon careful investigation, they are all notorious criminals who have died but whose evil deeds are still known to people. ○ After the adulterer's death, revenge was sought by He Er, a county official from Yizheng. He had once had an affair with a woman. The woman had an old friend named Hu Si, with whom she had been in contact for many years, and she profited from his wealth. Later, as Hu Si gradually became impoverished and destitute, the woman gradually distanced herself from him, and He Er further abused him, leading to his depression and death. The woman's husband also died, and she then returned to He Er, becoming his wife, and after several years, they had some savings.

### Final Translation He You had a wife who had passed away, and he had once had a son. Suddenly, the son contracted a mad illness, wielding a knife and an axe. Upon seeing a woman come, he immediately wanted to kill her, saying, "I am Hu Si. Your family used me for thousands of gold pieces, and when my wealth was exhausted, my heart departed, and I changed to another surname, so content. I die unwillingly, and I have already reported this to the gods, who have allowed me to take revenge." Medical treatment was ineffective, and they invited monks and Taoists, observed fasts, and prayed, but there was no miraculous effect. After several months, his son was as thin as a twig. One day, he suddenly started singing and performing, then suddenly rode a horse from the post station and galloped through the streets, and then suddenly smashed household items, searching for and distributing silver and money to others, saying, "The gods have allowed me to scatter your family's wealth and take your son's life." To this day, his son still exists, but the family's wealth has been emptied. ○ Governor Dong Solving a Wrongful Case During his tenure as the governor of Hai Ning Prefecture, Governor Dong Rong went down to inspect the countryside. A whirlwind met his carriage, following wherever he turned left or right. The governor found this strange and said, "If there is an unusual injustice, you may circle three times before my carriage and then retreat, and I will order my men to follow your lead." After the blessing, the whirlwind indeed circled three times and then retreated, so the governor ordered his men to follow the wind to investigate. They arrived at a remote area and found a tomb where the wind had disappeared. They knew it was the tomb of the daughter of a certain top scholar. They reported back to the governor, who ordered a summons to investigate. It was claimed that the girl had died suddenly from an illness. The governor did not believe this and wanted to exhume the tomb for inspection. The person involved demanded a written document from the governor stating "unjustified opening of a coffin" before they would allow the tomb to be opened. The governor, having no choice, gave them the document. When the tomb was opened and inspected, it was indeed found that the girl had died of illness. The governor deeply regretted his actions and could only wait for a report and further investigation. ---

Upon returning in his carriage, he had not taken many steps when a whirlwind came again, startling him. He stopped the carriage and thought carefully, recalling that there must be a reason for the coffin inside the tomb being placed under a stone slab. He returned to the tomb, lifted the stone, and examined it, finding another coffin. Upon opening it, he discovered another female corpse, who appeared as if she were still alive, with a beauty that could topple a nation, but was covered in lacerations. It was found that she had been coerced by a top scholar through threats, raped against her will, and died from the injuries. The official then investigated thoroughly according to the law, abolished the wrongful judgment, cleared her innocence, and honored her memory. ○ Liu the Tiger, whose name was Liu Mingjie, was from Jiangxi. He was nicknamed the Tiger, was strong and powerful, and was a rascal in his village. One night, after drinking heavily, on his way home, he felt the alcohol rising, and he walked by feeling the walls. Upon encountering a door, he entered, thinking it was his own home. His feet grew weak and soft, and he fell to the ground and lay down. He finally woke up when the fifth watch of the night ended. He heard someone ask, "Where is someone?" He replied, "In a certain cave." Another person asked, "Who is it this time?" He answered, "某某." Several names were mentioned, and Liu's name was among them. He wondered what case he had committed and which office had detained and interrogated him. Looking up at himself, he saw that the sky was also gradually brightening. Upon closer examination, he realized he was in an earth god temple, and upon searching everywhere, there were no human traces, which was very strange. Thinking that the cave was not far from there, he decided to investigate it. He quickly ran to the cave and indeed found a big man sleeping soundly. He thought the man was strong and healthy, and it would not be advisable to speak gently. He then drew his sword, grabbed the big man, and placed the sword at his throat.

Great Han was startled and asked, "What are you doing?" Liu replied, "You are an evil person, why are you asking me?" Great Han said, "I am a passing traveler, why do you consider me an evil person?" Liu said, "Since you are a passing traveler, why don't you stay at an inn, and why do you have suspicious actions? If you do not speak the truth, I will kill you first." Great Han hurriedly said, "I am on official duty, arresting a suspect." He showed the ticket, and the first person on it was Liu. He asked what crime Liu had committed and why he needed to be saved. Great Han said, "It is predestined by fate, ordained by God, how dare I indulge or show favoritely?" Liu said, "If so, whether I kill you or release you, I will die either way. Since it is all the same, why not die with you?" He again wanted to stab him. Great Han waved his hand and stopped him, saying, "I will save you. You can bite your finger yourself, let the blood stain my ticket, change your name, and move far away to another place, perhaps you can delay your fate for a few years." Liu followed his advice, and as Great Han rolled out of the cave and onto the ground, he transformed into a tiger, roaring and running into the mountains.

Liu stumbled home, arrived at his house, and found it was already light outside. He then changed his name and moved to a distant prefecture. From then on, he repented and stopped being a troublemaker, learned to manage his livelihood, married, had children, and lived to be seventy years old. Because of a ritual at his friend's house, he acted as a guarantor for a sick person. Reflecting on the importance of the ritual, Liu realized that he could not use a false name for such a significant ceremony. He decided to reveal his past and wrote his real name on the form before returning home. Having just taken a few steps out of the gate, he was immediately carried away by a tiger. In Fengqiao Town, Suzhou, there was a beggar named Qu. This town was a place where merchants and grain ships gathered. At the end of the village, there was an ancient temple where Qu lived. He had numb feet and went out in the morning and returned in the evening, never far from Fengqiao. One morning, Qu found a lost bag near the outhouse. He picked it up and examined its contents, which included several hundred taels of silver. He thought that this must be a bag left by a traveler. As a person with a short life, how could he enjoy such wealth? Moreover, he did not know what the traveler was involved in, and if he lost the bag, it could affect his life and death. Therefore, he returned to the temple and sat waiting.

At midday, indeed, a person came running swiftly, stamping his feet and beating his chest, looking extremely anxious and hurried. He asked, "Are you not the one who lost something?" The guest replied, "Yes, did you find it?" The beggar said, "I have it, but only if what you say is not mistaken can I return it to you." The guest was delighted and described the number of envelopes, the amount of money, the color of the silver, and the packaging, which all matched exactly. The beggar then took it out and returned it to him. The guest, seeing the original silver, was overjoyed and offered to give half as a gift. The beggar laughed and said, "Are you crazy? I do not accept your full generosity, and do I want half of it? And if you lose half, you will not be able to finish the big task. Please go away quickly, do not delay my begging." The guest, unwillingly, picked up the silver and left. The beggar reached the street corner and suddenly saw a young girl with braids, extremely beautiful, crying while clinging to her father. The crowd was like a wall, so he asked the people around. Someone told him, "The debt collector from the Cao family is about to take this girl as payment, so she is crying." He asked, "How much does she owe?" The people said, "Ten gold pieces." The beggar, upon hearing this, became angry and said, "Usurious private loans, so cruel and vicious. If she owed the government, what would happen then? Ten gold pieces is a small matter. Why be rich and unkind, to the point of taking a child?" Who knew the creditor was nearby, heard this, and became angry, pointing at the beggar and asking, "Like you, who will end up in the gutter, to talk about righteousness? Having made such a bold statement, can you pay for her?" The beggar, with great generosity, took the silver from the previous guest to pay for her, took the debt note, and left.

Cao's original intention was focused on the woman rather than the money. Enraged by Qu's deceitful plan, Cao bribed the arresting officers, falsely accused Qu of being a thief, and had him arrested and delivered to the authorities. Mr. Chen of Wu County deeply suspected Qu's innocence and, upon hearing about it, immediately went to the county to clear his name. Upon learning this, Mr. Chen said with delight, "This is a righteous beggar." According to the principle of reverse liability, the arresting officers were severely punished, and the rice merchants from Fengqiao were summoned. He instructed them, saying, "All the daily rice samples should be given as a reward to Qu, to relieve his daily hardship of begging door-to-door." He also had Qu adorned with red cloth and ordered him to be carried home in a sedan chair. As a result, this beggar now enjoyed the benefit of receiving a daily supply of rice. He gradually sought famous doctors. He encountered a hermit who used dried lotus petals, Atractylodes, and other herbs for washing and treatment. Within a few days, his foot disease was completely cured, and he was as healthy as a normal person. Within ten years, he unexpectedly built a large house, married a wife, and became a wealthy man.

In Shaoxing, there was a man surnamed Xu who had a newly acquired grand mansion. The mansion had three rooms for studying, with pavilions and terraces all present, serving as the place where the tutor Master Zhang set up his teaching. One night, Master Zhang read until after the second watch when he suddenly heard the sound of a window being opened in the east room. Suspecting it was a violent intruder, he peered through the window crack and saw a young woman enjoying the moonlight. She climbed a fake mountain, reached the top of a tree, and then crossed the wall of the neighbor's house and left. Master Zhang suspected it was the behavior of eloping and stopped reading, extinguished the candle, and went to sleep. Before dawn, when the rooster crowed, he heard a rustling sound at the top of the tree, which seemed like someone returning from a romantic tryst. Early in the morning, the bookboy brought Master Zhang to bathe. Master Zhang asked, "Who lives in the east room? Does it connect to the inner room?" The boy replied, "It does not connect; it is an idle room sealed by the previous owner." Master Zhang was greatly suspicious and went to look at it. The door was sealed, and the window was closed as usual. Peering inside, he saw a coffin inside. At night, he carefully observed, and it happened again. Master Zhang then lit a candle and opened the window to look inside. The lid of the coffin was tilted, and inside it was empty. Master Zhang helped to lift the lid of the coffin and took the *I Ching* and tore it apart, laying it thickly on the coffin. Then he returned and climbed up to wait. By the fifth watch of the night, he saw a woman enter through the window. Seeing the *I Ching*, she hesitated, circled the coffin once, looking around nervously, and lifted her head to see Master Zhang. Knowing what he had done, she bowed and pleaded. Master Zhang laughed and refused. The ghost said, "If you do not come down from the building, I will climb up." Master Zhang still refused to listen. The ghost then transformed into a green-faced, fanged monster and jumped and climbed straight up. Master Zhang then became dizzy and fell from the building, losing consciousness.

Until the servant brought tea and soup to the study, they searched everywhere for Zhang Sheng but could not find him. Then they went up to the tower with the host to observe. They saw someone in the eastern room downstairs. Opening the door to look inside, they found Zhang Sheng lying on the ground with the female corpse. Feeling him, his body was still warm. So they lifted him out and administered first aid. After a while, he regained consciousness and described what he had seen. They reported it in detail to the authorities, who investigated and summoned the relatives of the deceased to claim the body. But the relatives had already moved away. Because no one was guarding the house, it was sold off. By the time Xu acquired it, it had changed hands three times. It was also because of the ghost of the zombie causing trouble. So they burned the coffin. The neighbors' children who suffered from ghostly illnesses disappeared from that point on.

○ The Shens, who were self-pinched by someone, arranged for their son to marry a girl from the Shens. After the marriage for more than a year, they were very affectionate. One day, the girl became dazed and speechless, with her hands hanging straight down. Suddenly, she raised her hands and joined them in a gesture, crossing her eight fingers in a measuring posture, experiencing extreme pain and shouting as if to die. She could not open her hands herself, so those around her tried to separate her fingers, but they could not move them. Even those with strength tried to separate her fingers together, but they could not move them even a little. They urgently asked the reason, and the girl said, "There is a woman behind me, causing me to be like this." Before she finished speaking, she shouted even louder, and her cheeks turned completely red, as if she had been beaten. The girl dared not speak, for if she spoke, it would be more painful, and she only moaned. After some time, she opened her hands herself, and the skin and flesh of her eight fingers were red and swollen, but after half an hour, they recovered. The girl spoke and acted as usual, but she would not clearly state the reason. From then on, her eyes would close one or two times, or three or four times, and the pain was indescribable, and medicine, herbs, and charms could not cure it, and she still suffers like this today, unable to understand the reason. Some say: The girl had a peculiar nature, and when she was at her mother's house, her family was originally wealthy. The girl would write out the dishes and condiments on a water card for each meal and eat them. If they were slightly unsatisfactory, she would scold and break the utensils. When a maid brought tea, if the maid held the cup by the rim, she would break the cup and severely whip the maid, thinking that the maid's hands were unclean and could not touch the tea. The clothes she wore, if washed once, she would not wear again. Some say: The current pinching she is suffering is a punishment for her wastefulness, is it true?

A Fairy of the Yandang Mountains In the prefecture of Wenzhou, there was a woman named Dai, a fairy of the Yandang Mountains. She had a son who was eighteen years old, handsome and well-groomed. One day, he closed the door to study, and suddenly he disappeared. His family searched everywhere but could not find him. One day, he suddenly descended from a fragrant citrus tree in the garden and said, "One evening, I was strolling in the garden under the moonlight when I saw a beautiful woman flying down from the sky. She took me up and said, 'I am an ordinary person. How can I ascend to heaven?' The woman smiled, picked a fragrant citrus leaf, and told me to step on it. Immediately, I was lifted into the air. We arrived at the top of a high mountain, where there were dozens of stone pavilions. Inside, there were pavilions, flowers, and other decorations, everything one could imagine. I asked, 'Where are we?' She replied, 'This is the Yandang Mountains of Wenzhou. Even the Tiantai Mountains have stories of Liu and Ruan. How could my Yandang Mountains, which are over a thousand feet higher than Tiantai, lack beautiful tales to be told on earth?' We then became husband and wife. In the various stone pavilions, there were fairy maidens of all ages. The words they spoke were all esoteric and profound teachings, which I could not remember, but I felt that the food, clothing, and daily life were luxurious and delightful. I was so happy that I forgot to return. Yesterday, she suddenly told me, 'Your father will celebrate his 80th birthday tomorrow. Not only should you go to congratulate him, but I should go too.' She then picked another fragrant citrus leaf and told me to step on it. I ascended into the sky again and returned to my home."

The family and neighbors heard this news and came to watch, so many that they were like hemp. Suddenly, they smelled an unusual fragrance that filled their noses, and they heard the sound of flutes and drums in the air. Indeed, there was a beautiful woman with a jeweled crown and jade ornaments, bowing in the clouds. Every time she knelt and rose, the rosy light flashed in all directions, and all the birds sang. The family was thinking of holding on to them, but as soon as a gentle breeze arose, the woman and her son had already slowly taken each other's hands and left. The father missed his son and could not stop crying. Someone said, "This strange being knows etiquette; await when the old man is ninety years old, and it will definitely come back with your son again." In Jinshan County, there was an old farmer who, on the first day of the month, dreamed of a blue-clad person who looked like a messenger bringing a document. The person told him, "You will die on the seventeenth day of this month because you have been diligent and cautious throughout your life without major faults. After death, you will be reborn as a son in a certain family, and you will also have a comfortable life and live a long time without worry. I came to tell you beforehand so that you can settle your family affairs early. At the appointed time, I will come with you to reincarnate." The farmer woke up and told everyone in the family, handing over all family matters to his son. Within a few days, everything was settled, and he wiped his hands, waiting for the appointed time. On the twelfth night, he suddenly dreamed again of the blue-clad person coming to urge him to leave. The farmer refused, saying, "I knew it all along, but the woman on the tenth night accidentally tripped and fell, damaging the fetus. How could she wait until the seventeenth? She gave birth that night. The child has been born, but the soul needs to enter the body to be able to eat and drink. It has already been three days. If you do not go, she cannot survive." The farmer woke up and told his family about the dream, then calmly lay down to die.

In the 46th year of the reign of Emperor Qianlong, in the West City of Changsha, in the neighborhood of Chang'an, there was a place called Qing Shi Bing. There lived a military officer named An, who had a daughter aged five. She was raised as a future wife in the household of Zhang, a military officer, but her stepmother treated her harshly, beating her severely for any minor offense. She could not bear the suffering and, at the age of thirteen, fled back to her father's home. Zhang demanded the return of his daughter, but An, noting that the girl had not yet reached the age of marriage (笄), refused to give her away, saying he would keep her at home until a favorable time (吉期) to prepare the dowry and send her off in a proper manner. When the girl turned seventeen, her fiancé was also grown up, and Zhang informed An of the planned wedding date. An prepared the dowry items in readiness for the marriage. However, the girl knew that the wedding was approaching and feared the strictness of her stepmother. She spent the entire night crying and praying to the heavens, asking for a quick death and not wanting to marry. Her mother, seeing her in such distress, felt very sorry for her and said, "It is useless to cry and pray for death. If you can pray to the heavens and change into a male body, you can avoid marriage." That night, the girl dreamed of an old man holding three pills, each as large as a bullet, two red and one white. The old man put the pills into her mouth and then left. When she woke up, she felt an intense heat in her lower abdomen and an unusual pain in her throat. Within the time it takes to cook a meal, she transformed into a tall man with a swollen throat. Surprised and doubtful, she told her mother, who verified the transformation. An and his wife had no sons and only this daughter. When they saw her transformed into a boy, they were very happy and went to inform Zhang. However, Zhang, considering the matter strange and unbelievable, suspected that An was fabricating the story to avoid giving away his daughter and filed a complaint in the county court.

At that time, the magistrate of the county in Shanxi, named Dang Zhaoxiong, brought the woman to the case for examination. She appeared to be a woman, but her genitalia were fresh and red, confirming that she was indeed a man. It was difficult to proceed with the marriage. The magistrate ordered the woman to be given a dowry and a substitute bride, to be given to her son. In the courtroom, the woman was ordered to unbind her feet, shave her head, remove her earrings, and put on boots, transforming into a man and leaving. --- A Man Transformed into a Rat for Theft Observing Officer Wang, upon arriving in Changsha to collect his salary, the county magistrate Chen prepared a guesthouse for him and placed the salary in his bedroom. One night, as he was just about to go to bed, he felt short of breath and could not sleep, turning and tossing until the third watch. Suddenly, a loud gnawing sound came from a crack in the ceiling, and he hung a curtain to look up. He saw a hole in the ceiling as large as a bowl, and something fell from above. Upon closer inspection, it was a rat, about two feet long, standing upright and walking. Wang was greatly startled and searched everywhere around his bed for something to hit it with, but could not find anything in the suddenness. Near his pillow, there was a box containing seals, which he picked up and threw at the rat. The box broke, and the seals flew out, striking the rat. The rat fell to the ground, its skin peeling off, revealing a naked man. Wang was greatly alarmed and shouted, and the servants came running. Soon after, the county magistrate Chen also arrived. Upon seeing the man, he recognized him as a local gentleman whom he had known before, who was quite wealthy. He did not know how this had happened. When questioned, the man trembled and could not answer. Wang then sat in the guesthouse and was about to begin the punishment.

The person said: "I was originally poor and destitute, unable to sustain myself, and intended to drown in the river. I met someone who asked the reason and advised me not to die, saying, 'I will make you have enough food and clothing.' He led me to his house, took out a bag, and told me to put my hand in and feel it. All were bundles of skin rolled up, stacked and layered. I took one piece of skin out at hand, and it was a piece of rat skin. The person taught me incantations, and I wore the skin and stepped on the trigram, knelt to the Big Dipper, and recited the incantation twenty-four times. I rolled on the ground once, and my body immediately turned into a rat. Then he gave me a small bag to wear on my side, and I put stolen money into it. The bag was not big, nor was it heavy. When I got home and recited the incantation, the skin came off, and I returned to human form. I reported all the stolen money accumulated over the years, not less than tens of thousands."

The king then asked, "Before your recent failure, had you ever been discovered?" The subject replied, "This technique is so powerful that it cannot fail. I remember ten years ago, I saw a wealthy guest on a wooden sign and considered stealing from him. I transformed into a mouse and climbed onto the sign. Suddenly, a cat bit my neck, and I quickly used the technique to shed my skin, trying to escape. With a loud sound, the cat's skin fell off, and it turned into a human, and I was captured. Upon investigation, the person turned out to be my fellow disciple, whose technique was even more refined. He could transform into any object with his mind, without needing to rely on skin. Considering our shared teacher, he released me and warned me not to do such things again. I have changed my ways for three years now. My family has five sons; two have already entered officialdom, one is a jiedushi, and there are still two sons. I wanted to donate the position of county magistrate to each of them. The silver in my home was insufficient, and I learned that the public funds were substantial, so I tried to steal half to make up the amount, but unexpectedly, I was discovered and failed." The king then took the skin and recited a spell to test it, but the skin and the person did not match. He then handed the person over to the county for further investigation, and only after the verdict was finalized did he leave.

A Singing Dog In the city of Changsha, there were two people who owned a dog that was slightly larger than an ordinary dog. Its front paws had toes longer than those of a typical dog, while its hind paws resembled those of a bear. It had a small tail, and its ears and nose looked like those of a human, making it unlike any other dog, yet its body was covered in dog fur. The dog could speak like a human and sing various small tunes, always in perfect rhythm. Spectators gathered in large crowds, competing to give money to hear a song, and the noise could be heard far and wide. The magistrate of the county, surnamed Jing, encountered them on his way and ordered his servants to bring the dog back, using the excuse that his mother wanted to see it and planned to give a generous gift.
